topics.title: Nature of Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT722 - FOOD PROCESS AND EQUIPMENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nature of Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Process and Equipment Design (OEFTT722)**, the term **"Nature of Equipment"** refers to the systematic classification, characterization, and functional description of the physical apparatus and machinery used to transform raw agricultural, dairy, marine, or biological materials into safe, shelf-stable, and consumer-ready food products. It encompasses the **mechanical, thermal, chemical, and biochemical** operating principles that govern how each piece of equipment interacts with the food matrix.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) syllabus for Module 1, the "Nature of Equipment" is studied under the broader umbrella of **Equipment Selection, Design Philosophy, and Process Integration** in the food industry. It establishes the foundation required for every subsequent module dealing with mechanical design, sanitary fabrication, and process optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> Food process equipment is defined as any apparatus, machine, or assembly of components that performs a defined unit operation — such as heating, cooling, mixing, separation, size reduction, or packaging — on a food material, while preserving its nutritional, sensory, and microbiological quality attributes in accordance with **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India, 2006)** and **Codex Alimentarius** standards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine a large, modern, industrial-scale kitchen in a five-star hotel. Inside it, you will find dedicated devices: a *blast chiller* to rapidly cool cooked food, a *planetary mixer* to knead dough, a *pressure cooker (retort)* to sterilize canned food, and a *centrifuge* to separate cream from milk. Each device is designed for **one specific physical purpose** and is built from materials (often **stainless steel AISI 304 / AISI 316 grade**) that will not react with food.

The "Nature of Equipment" in food engineering is **exactly this same concept, scaled up and made scientifically rigorous**:
- The **"what does the machine do?"** question = the *unit operation* it performs.
- The **"how does it do it?"** question = the *underlying physical principle* (heat transfer, momentum transfer, mass transfer, or reaction kinetics).
- The **"is it safe to touch food?"** question = the *material of construction* and *sanitary design* philosophy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples of "Nature of Equipment" in Industry:**
> - *Dairy Plant:* Cream separator (centrifugal force), Homogenizer (high-pressure shear), Pasteurizer (plate heat exchanger).
> - *Beverage Industry:* Carbonator, Bottle Washer, Tunnel Pasteurizer.
> - *Bakery:* Spiral Mixer, Proofer, Tunnel Oven, Spiral Freezer.
> - *Fruits & Vegetables:* Pulper, Juice Extractor, De-aerator, Aseptic Filler.

## 1.3 Key Classification Metrics (Standard Engineering Terms)

The following bolded classification metrics are universally used by KTU examiners and the food industry to describe the **nature** of any equipment:

- **Solid, Liquid, or Gas (Phase Handled)** — Determines the *physical state* of the food during processing.
- **Batch vs. Continuous (Mode of Operation)** — Determines throughput and flexibility.
- **Pressure Regime** — Atmospheric, Vacuum (< 1 atm), or High-Pressure (> 1 atm).
- **Temperature Regime** — Cryogenic (< -40°C), Refrigeration (0–8°C), Pasteurization (72–90°C), Sterilization (121–135°C), or Baking (180–250°C).
- **Aggressive / Corrosive Food (pH & Acidity)** — Affects **material of construction (MOC)** selection.
- **Hygiene / Cleanability Class** — Open-frame, partially closed, or **sanitary (3-A / EHEDG compliant)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Discuss the nature of food processing equipment"*, immediately structure your answer around the **five broad categories** shown in Section 2 — and anchor each category to **one specific industrial example** with a clear **material of construction**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Fundamental "Natures" of Food Processing Equipment

Any food process equipment can be classified under one (or a hybrid combination) of the following five operational natures, each governed by a specific engineering principle:

### (A) Mechanical / Physical Separation Equipment
- **Principle:** Application of mechanical forces (gravity, centrifugal, pressure, filtration) to separate components of a food mixture.
- **Examples:** Centrifuge (cream separator), Filter press, Cyclone separator, Sieve, Decanter, Membrane filtration (UF / NF / RO).
- **Engineering Discipline:** Momentum transfer, mass transfer.

### (B) Thermal Processing Equipment
- **Principle:** Controlled addition or removal of heat to achieve pasteurization, sterilization, blanching, baking, evaporation, or freezing.
- **Examples:** Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE), Shell-and-Tube Heater, Steam-jacketed kettle, Spray Dryer, Refrigerated Cold Store, Cryogenic tunnel.
- **Engineering Discipline:** Heat transfer (conduction, convection, radiation).

### (C) Size Reduction & Forming Equipment
- **Principle:** Application of compressive, shear, or impact forces to alter the physical dimensions, shape, or surface area of a food material.
- **Examples:** Hammer mill, Ball mill, Disc mill (attrition), Colloid mill, Homogenizer, Extruder (single / twin-screw).
- **Engineering Discipline:** Mechanical comminution, rheology.

### (D) Mixing, Blending & Agitation Equipment
- **Principle:** Achievement of uniformity in composition, temperature, or phase distribution within a food mass.
- **Examples:** Paddle mixer, Ribbon blender, Planetary mixer, Propeller agitator, Static mixer (in-line).
- **Engineering Discipline:** Momentum transfer, fluid dynamics.

### (E) Packaging, Storage & Material Handling Equipment
- **Principle:** Preservation, containment, and controlled transfer of processed food.
- **Examples:** Aseptic filler, Retort pouch sealer, Conveyor, Bucket elevator, Pneumatic conveyor, Cold storage warehouse.
- **Engineering Discipline:** Packaging technology, logistics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern (Recurring Theme):**
> Almost every Module 1 question in KTU 2024 scheme maps a given food industry (dairy, bakery, beverage, marine) to the *correct category* of equipment above. Mastery of this mapping is mandatory for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 2.2 Design Constraints That Define the "Nature"

The following constraints dictate *why* a particular equipment is built the way it is:

1. **Sanitary / Hygienic Design** — All product-contact surfaces must be **stainless steel AISI 304 (food-grade, corrosion-resistant, non-toxic)** or **AISI 316 (chloride-resistant, used for salty / acidic foods)**. Surface finish must be **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm (mirror polish)** to prevent microbial adhesion. This is a hallmark of **3-A Sanitary Standards** and **EHEDG (European Hygienic Engineering & Design Group)** guidelines.
2. **CIP (Clean-In-Place) Compatibility** — Equipment must be designed with **no dead legs, no crevices, and self-draining slopes (typically 1 in 100 gradient)** to allow circulation of cleaning solutions (typically 1–2% NaOH at 75–80 °C, followed by 0.5–1% HNO₃ rinse).
3. **Thermal Efficiency** — Governed by the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)**, typically **500 – 3000 W/m²·K** for plate heat exchangers and **10 – 50 W/m²·K** for jacketed vessels.
4. **Energy Economy** — Modern food plants target a **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** in the range of **0.1 – 0.5 kWh per kg of product processed**.
5. **Regulatory Compliance** — FSSAI (India), USDA-FSIS (USA), EFSA (Europe), Codex Alimentarius (International).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below summarizes every formula a KTU 2024 student must know when discussing the *nature* of food processing equipment in numerical or design-based questions.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Symbol Meanings & Units | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacity / Throughput | $Q = \dfrac{m}{t}$ | $Q$ = mass flow rate (kg/h), $m$ = mass (kg), $t$ = time (h) | 100 – 50,000 kg/h (plant-scale) |
| 2 | Power Requirement | $P = \dfrac{W}{\eta} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta P}{\eta}$ | $P$ = shaft power (W), $W$ = useful work, $\eta$ = efficiency, $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $\Delta P$ = pressure rise | $\eta_{motor} \approx 0.85 - 0.95$ |
| 3 | Heat Transfer (LMTD) | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1, \Delta T_2$ = temperature differences at two ends (K or °C) | — |
| 4 | Overall Heat Transfer | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | $Q$ = heat duty (W), $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $A$ = area (m²) | $U_{PHE} = 1500-3000$ |
| 5 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $v$ = velocity (m/s), $D$ = diameter (m), $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s) | Laminar $\leq 2100$, Turbulent $\geq 4000$ |
| 6 | Centrifugal Force | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = m \cdot v^2 / r$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = radius (m) | 5000 – 15000 × g (separator) |
| 7 | Filtration (Darcy) | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + \alpha \cdot w)}$ | $R_m$ = medium resistance, $\alpha$ = cake resistance, $w$ = mass of cake per filtrate volume | — |
| 8 | Particle Size Reduction (Rittinger) | $E = K_R \cdot (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$ | $E$ = energy, $K_R$ = constant, $D_1, D_2$ = initial / final diameter (m) | — |
| 9 | Pressure Drop in Pipe | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = pipe length, $D$ = diameter | $f \approx 0.02 - 0.04$ (turbulent) |
| 10 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{input}}{m_{product}}$ | $E_{input}$ = energy input (kJ or kWh), $m_{product}$ = mass produced (kg) | 0.1 – 0.5 kWh/kg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Table-Breaking Pitfall:** Never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` for absolute value inside these tables — KTU board-evaluators often run the answer script through automatic markdown renderers. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead. The tables above already comply with this rule.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Classification System

- **Process Engineers** use the five-category classification to draft a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** for any food plant.
- **Equipment Manufacturers** (e.g., GEA, Tetra Pak, SPX Flow) market their products *category-wise*, helping food technologists select equipment quickly.
- **Capital Cost Estimation** is done per category: a thermal evaporator costs ≈ ₹15–50 lakhs per 1000 L/h capacity, while a separator costs ≈ ₹25–80 lakhs.
- **Sanitary Audits (FSSAI / ISO 22000)** are conducted using these categories to verify CIP, SIP (Sterilize-in-Place), and hygienic design compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Diameter for a Non-Circular Duct (used in many food equipment)

Many food equipment ducts (e.g., rectangular steam jackets, square chutes) are non-circular. To apply the Reynolds number formula, we use the **hydraulic (equivalent) diameter**, derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The hydraulic diameter is defined as four times the cross-sectional area divided by the wetted perimeter.

$$D_h = \frac{4 \cdot A_c}{P_w}$$

**Step 2 — Explanation of variables:**
$A_c$ is the cross-sectional area of flow in $\text{m}^2$ and $P_w$ is the wetted perimeter (the portion of the perimeter actually in contact with the fluid) in m.

**Step 3 — For a circular pipe of diameter $D$:**
The area is $A_c = \pi D^2 / 4$ and the wetted perimeter is $P_w = \pi D$. Substituting:

$$D_h = \frac{4 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\right)}{\pi D} = \frac{\pi D^2}{\pi D} = D$$

**Step 4 — For a square duct of side $a$:**
The area is $A_c = a^2$ and the wetted perimeter is $P_w = 4a$. Substituting:

$$D_h = \frac{4 \cdot a^2}{4a} = a$$

**Step 5 — For a rectangular duct of sides $a$ and $b$:**
The area is $A_c = a \cdot b$ and the wetted perimeter is $P_w = 2(a + b)$. Substituting:

$$D_h = \frac{4 \cdot a \cdot b}{2(a + b)} = \frac{2ab}{a + b}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Insight:**
This equivalent diameter is then used in the **Reynolds number** formula to determine whether flow inside a food heat exchanger, evaporator, or pipe is laminar or turbulent — a critical input for **pump selection, heat transfer coefficient prediction, and CIP flow rate design.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Reference:** This derivation supports Formula #5 in the KTU Formula Sheet. It will appear in 14-mark design problems for shell-and-tube or plate heat exchangers.

## 3.2 Derivation: Rittinger's Law for Size Reduction Equipment

When a food material (say, wheat, sugar, spices) is reduced in size, the energy required is proportional to the **new surface area created**. Rittinger's Law expresses this quantitatively.

**Step 1 — Statement of the Law:**
The energy $E$ required for size reduction is directly proportional to the increase in surface area of the particles.

$$E = K_R \cdot \Delta S$$

**Step 2 — Surface Area of a Sphere of Diameter $D$:**
The surface area of a single spherical particle is given by $S = \pi D^2$.

**Step 3 — For $N$ particles per unit mass:**
The total surface area per unit mass is $S_{total} = N \cdot \pi D^2$. Since $N$ is inversely proportional to $D^3$ (volume per particle), we have $N \propto 1/D^3$, so $S_{total} \propto 1/D$.

**Step 4 — Change in Surface Area:**
The change in total surface area per unit mass going from an initial diameter $D_1$ to a final diameter $D_2$ is:

$$\Delta S = S_2 - S_1 = K' \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final Rittinger Equation:**

$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Practical Application in Food Industry:**
This equation is used to size a **hammer mill** for spice grinding, a **ball mill** for cocoa processing, and a **roller mill** for flour manufacture. A higher $K_R$ indicates a *harder, more energy-intensive* material.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Numerical problems typically give $D_1$, $D_2$, and the *required energy* — and ask the student to *find the new $D_2$* for a different energy input. Always show the algebraic rearrangement step-by-step.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Food Industry to Equipment Nature

The following decision matrix is a **high-yield KTU analytical tool** used to match any food industry scenario to the correct equipment nature:

| Food Industry | Raw Material | Primary Unit Operation | Equipment Nature | Material of Construction | Key Design Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dairy | Raw milk | Pasteurization | Thermal (PHE) | AISI 316 SS | $T = 72 \,^\circ\text{C}$ for 15 s |
| Dairy | Cream | Separation | Mechanical (Centrifuge) | AISI 304 SS | $g$-force $\approx 8000 \times g$ |
| Edible Oil | Crude oil | Refining | Thermal + Chemical | Carbon steel + SS lining | Vacuum < 5 mbar |
| Bakery | Wheat flour | Size reduction | Mechanical (Roller mill) | Hardened cast iron | Particle size 100 – 200 μm |
| Bakery | Dough | Mixing | Mechanical (Spiral mixer) | SS bowl, coated arms | Torque $\approx$ 200 – 500 N·m |
| Beverages | Fruit pulp | Pasteurization | Thermal (Tubular) | AISI 316 SS | $U = 2000 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ |
| Beverages | Concentrate | Evaporation | Thermal (Multi-effect) | SS + copper tubes | Steam economy 3 – 5 |
| Marine | Fish | Freezing | Refrigeration | Galvanized + SS | $-40 \,^\circ\text{C}$ blast freeze |
| Confectionery | Chocolate | Conching | Mixing + Thermal | SS jacketed vessel | $T = 50 - 70 \,^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Spices | Whole spices | Grinding | Mechanical (Hammer mill) | MS / SS screen | $D_{final} \leq 100 \, \mu\text{m}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Recall KTU Strategy:** Memorize *one row per industry*. Examiners love to ask: *"Suggest a suitable equipment with material of construction for processing X in industry Y."*

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Sizing a Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)

**Problem:** A dairy plant pasteurizes 5000 kg/h of milk from 4 °C to 72 °C using hot water entering at 90 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 2500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Calculate the required heat exchanger area.

**Step 1 — Heat Duty:**
Specific heat of milk $C_p \approx 3.9 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$.

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 5000 \cdot 3.9 \cdot (72 - 4)$$

$$Q = 5000 \cdot 3.9 \cdot 68 = 1,326,000 \, \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Watts:**
$1 \, \text{kJ/h} = 1/3600 \, \text{kW} = 1000/3600 \, \text{W} \approx 0.2778 \, \text{W}$.

$$Q = 1,326,000 \cdot 0.2778 \approx 368,335 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD):**
Assume hot water exits at $\approx$ 80 °C, then:
- $\Delta T_1 = 90 - 72 = 18 \,^\circ\text{C}$
- $\Delta T_2 = 80 - 4 = 76 \,^\circ\text{C}$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1}{\ln(\Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1)} = \frac{76 - 18}{\ln(76/18)}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{58}{\ln(4.222)} = \frac{58}{1.4404} \approx 40.26 \,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Required Area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{368,335}{2500 \cdot 40.26} = \frac{368,335}{100,650} \approx 3.66 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion:**
A standard PHE module of $\approx 4 \, \text{m}^2$ area would be selected, with a design safety factor of **1.2 – 1.5** to account for fouling, giving a final installed area of $\approx 5 \, \text{m}^2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Classification of Food Process Equipment

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the hierarchical classification of food process equipment by its **nature** — a high-recall KTU visual aid.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Food Process Equipment] --> B[Mechanical Separation]
    A --> C[Thermal Processing]
    A --> D[Size Reduction]
    A --> E[Mixing and Blending]
    A --> F[Packaging and Handling]

    B --> B1[Centrifuge]
    B --> B2[Filter Press]
    B --> B3[Membrane Module]
    B --> B4[Cyclone Separator]

    C --> C1[Plate Heat Exchanger]
    C --> C2[Shell and Tube Heater]
    C --> C3[Spray Dryer]
    C --> C4[Blast Freezer]
    C --> C5[Steam Jacketed Kettle]

    D --> D1[Hammer Mill]
    D --> D2[Ball Mill]
    D --> D3[Colloid Mill]
    D --> D4[Roller Mill]
    D --> D5[Extruder]

    E --> E1[Paddle Mixer]
    E --> E2[Ribbon Blender]
    E --> E3[Planetary Mixer]
    E --> E4[Static Mixer]

    F --> F1[Aseptic Filler]
    F --> F2[Conveyor]
    F --> F3[Bucket Elevator]
    F --> F4[Retort Pouch Sealer]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Design Constraints Governing Equipment Nature

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Raw Food Material] --> P[Process Requirement Analysis]
    P --> N1{Physical State?}
    N1 -->|Solid| S1[Size Reduction / Mixing]
    N1 -->|Liquid| S2[Pumping / Heat Exchange]
    N1 -->|Gas-Vapor| S3[Drying / Evaporation]
    P --> N2{Hygiene Level?}
    N2 -->|Open Frame| M1[Carbon Steel MOC]
    N2 -->|Sanitary| M2[AISI 304 Stainless Steel]
    N2 -->|Aseptic| M3[AISI 316 Stainless Steel]
    P --> N3{Mode of Operation?}
    N3 -->|Batch| O1[Agitated Vessels]
    N3 -->|Continuous| O2[PHE / PHE Modules]
    S1 --> Q[Final Equipment Selection]
    S2 --> Q
    S3 --> Q
    M1 --> Q
    M2 --> Q
    M3 --> Q
    O1 --> Q
    O2 --> Q
    Q --> X[Output: Sanitary, Efficient, FSSAI Compliant Equipment]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Typical Dairy Processing Line

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Milk Reception] --> B[Milk Chiller at 4 deg C]
    B --> C[Centrifugal Clarifier]
    C --> D[Plate Heat Exchanger Pasteurizer]
    D --> E[Homogenizer 200 bar]
    E --> F[Storage Tank]
    F --> G[Aseptic Packaging]
    G --> H[Finished Product Dispatch]

    D -.CIP Cleaning.-> D1[CIP Loop Returns to D]
    E -.CIP Cleaning.-> E1[CIP Loop Returns to E]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Representation — A Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) Module

Since a physical PHE drawing is complex, the Mermaid block below conveys the **functional architecture** of cold and hot streams in counter-current flow — a common KTU diagram type.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Hot Fluid In 90 deg C] --> A2[Plate 1]
    A2 --> A3[Plate 2]
    A3 --> A4[Plate 3]
    A4 --> A5[Plate 4]
    A5 --> A6[Hot Fluid Out 80 deg C]

    B1[Cold Fluid In 4 deg C] --> B2[Plate 4]
    B2 --> B3[Plate 3]
    B3 --> B4[Plate 2]
    B4 --> B5[Plate 1]
    B5 --> B6[Cold Fluid Out 72 deg C]

    A2 -.- B5
    A3 -.- B4
    A4 -.- B3
    A5 -.- B2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The hot fluid (top arrow) and cold fluid (bottom arrow) flow in *opposite directions* (counter-current) through alternating plates. The dashed lines represent the **heat transfer interface** between the two fluids. The plates are pressed together in a frame, and food-grade gaskets (EPDM / NBR) seal each plate.

## 4.5 Material of Construction Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Equipment MOC Selection] --> Q1{Food pH?}
    Q1 -->|pH less than 4.5 Acidic| A1[Use AISI 316 SS]
    Q1 -->|pH 4.5 to 7 Neutral| A2[Use AISI 304 SS]
    Q1 -->|pH greater than 7 Alkaline| A3[Use AISI 304 SS or MS with epoxy coating]

    Start --> Q2{Temperature Regime?}
    Q2 -->|Above 100 deg C| B1[AISI 316 SS]
    Q2 -->|0 to 100 deg C| B2[AISI 304 SS]
    Q2 -->|Below 0 deg C Freezing| B3[Galvanized MS or AISI 304 SS]

    Start --> Q3{Cl Content?}
    Q3 -->|High Salt| C1[AISI 316 SS Mandatory]
    Q3 -->|Low Salt| C2[AISI 304 SS Acceptable]
```
