topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT722 - FOOD PROCESS AND EQUIPMENT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design of Heat Exchangers, Double Pipe Heat Exchangers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Heat Exchanger — Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heat Exchanger (KTU 2024 Official Terminology):** > A heat exchanger is a process equipment designed to transfer thermal energy between two or more fluids at different temperatures, separated by a solid wall (direct-contact type being the only exception), while preventing the fluids from mixing. In food process engineering, heat exchangers are critical for operations like pasteurization, sterilization, UHT processing, cooling, and evaporation feed pre-heating. > [!NOTE] > **Classification Basis (as per KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus):** > Heat exchangers are classified based on: > 1. **Construction Geometry** — Double pipe, Shell & tube, Plate, Spiral > 2. **Flow Arrangement** — Co-current (parallel), Counter-current, Cross-flow > 3. **Heat Transfer Mechanism** — Recuperative (continuous wall), Regenerative (cyclic storage), Direct contact (mixing) > 4. **Service Function** — Heater, Cooler, Condenser, Reboiler, Vaporizer ## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Hot Tea in a Steel Glass" Imagine you are drinking hot tea ($\approx 85°C$) from a thin steel glass, and you place the glass in a bowl of cold water. The steel wall acts as the **heat transfer surface**, the tea is the **hot fluid**, the cold water is the **cold fluid**, and heat moves from tea → steel → cold water (driven by the **temperature gradient**). The steel never mixes with the tea or water. The rate at which your tea cools depends on the steel's **thermal conductivity**, the **area** of contact, the **thickness** of the steel, and the **flow velocities** of the tea and water. This is precisely a **double-pipe heat exchanger** — a hot fluid in an inner pipe, a cold fluid in an annulus, separated by a pipe wall. ## 1.3 Double Pipe Heat Exchanger — The KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Double Pipe Heat Exchanger (DPHE):** > The simplest type of heat exchanger consisting of two concentric pipes. The hot fluid flows through the inner pipe, while the cold fluid flows through the annular space between the two pipes. Heat transfers radially through the inner pipe wall. DPHEs are typically used for **low-to-moderate flow rates** (less than $\mathbf{15 \, m^3/h}$), high pressure drop services, and viscous/dirty food streams. **Standard Industrial Nomenclature (KTU Convention):** - **Inner pipe (flow side)** = also called the *tube* or *core pipe* - **Outer pipe (annular side)** = the *shell* side or *jacket* - **Inner pipe outer diameter** = $D_o$ - **Inner pipe inner diameter** = $D_i$ - **Outer pipe inner diameter** = $D_{shell}$ or $D_s$ - **Annular hydraulic diameter** = $D_h = D_s - D_o$ ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded) | Constant / Parameter | Standard Value | Units | |---|---|---| | **Universal Gas Constant, $R$** | **8.314** | $\text{J/(mol·K)}$ | | **Standard atmospheric pressure** | **101.325** | $\text{kPa}$ | | **Steam latent heat (saturated, 100°C)** | **2257** | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | | **Thermal conductivity of steel** | **45** | $\text{W/(m·K)}$ | | **Fouling factor for food streams (typical)** | **0.0002 – 0.0009** | $\text{m}^2\text{·K/W}$ | | **Acceptable LMTD correction factor, $F$** | $\geq$ **0.75 – 0.90** | dimensionless | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Heat Transfer Equation (The Heart of the Subject) The overall rate of heat transfer in any heat exchanger is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling** combined with the **Fourier conduction law** integrated over the entire surface area: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$ where: - $Q$ = heat duty $[\text{W or kW}]$ - $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient $[\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}]$ - $A$ = effective heat transfer area $[\text{m}^2]$ - $\Delta T_m$ = true mean temperature difference $[\text{K or }^\circ\text{C}]$ ## 2.2 Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) For a simple double-pipe exchanger, the LMTD is calculated assuming constant fluid properties and no phase change: $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$ **Temperature difference convention:** - $\Delta T_1$ = temperature difference at the **inlet** end - $\Delta T_2$ = temperature difference at the **outlet** end > [!NOTE] > **Why LMTD and not arithmetic mean?** > The temperature profile along a heat exchanger is exponential (because the rate of heat transfer is proportional to the *instantaneous* temperature difference, not the average). LMTD correctly weights this exponential profile. Arithmetic mean works only when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (i.e., when one fluid undergoes a phase change at constant temperature). ## 2.3 Counter-Flow vs. Co-Current Flow (KTU 2024 Highlight) | Flow Type | Description | LMTD Magnitude | |---|---|---| | **Co-current (parallel)** | Both fluids enter at the same end, flow in the same direction | Smaller (inferior for coolers/heaters) | | **Counter-current** | Fluids enter at opposite ends, flow in opposite directions | **Larger** (preferred for most process designs) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Trick:** > For **identical inlet/outlet temperatures**, the counter-current arrangement gives a *higher* LMTD than co-current. For the same heat duty, this means a **smaller area (cheaper exchanger)** is required in counter-current flow. ## 2.4 Energy Balance (The Two-Equation Foundation) Heat lost by hot fluid = Heat gained by cold fluid (assuming no losses): $$Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$ where $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate $[\text{kg/s}]$, $c_p$ = specific heat $[\text{kJ/(kg·K)}]$. ## 2.5 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) — The Resistance Network The overall heat transfer coefficient is determined by the **series resistance model**, where each resistance represents a layer that heat must cross: $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{D_i}{D_o \cdot h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning of each term:** > 1. $\dfrac{1}{h_i}$ — convective resistance inside the inner pipe > 2. $\dfrac{x_w}{k_w}$ — conductive resistance through the pipe wall > 3. $\dfrac{D_i}{D_o \cdot h_o}$ — convective resistance on the annulus side (area-corrected) > 4. $R_{f,i}$ + $R_{f,o}$ — fouling (dirt/scale/milk-stone) resistances > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to apply the **area correction factor** $\dfrac{D_i}{D_o}$ when the convective coefficient is defined on the outer surface area. This is a **guaranteed 2-mark loss** in KTU valuations. ## 2.6 Individual Heat Transfer Coefficients (Sieder-Tate / Dittus-Boelter) **For turbulent flow in inner pipe (Sieder-Tate equation, for heating/cooling viscous food fluids):** $$h_i = 0.023 \cdot \frac{k}{D_i} \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{\mu_b}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}$$ **For turbulent flow in annulus (equivalent diameter method):** $$h_o = 0.023 \cdot \frac{k}{D_h} \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$$ where the **equivalent diameter (hydraulic diameter) of annulus**: $$D_h = \frac{4 \cdot A_c}{P_w} = D_s - D_o$$ ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Essential for Exam Hall) | # | Formula | Application | Key Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Energy balance | For sensible heat transfer | | 2 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ | Latent heat | Condenser / reboiler | | 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Core design eq. | Master equation | | 4 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Mean ΔT | For constant $c_p$, no phase change | | 5 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{D_i}{D_o h_o} + R_f$ | Resistance network | Includes fouling | | 6 | $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{n}$ | Convective h | $n = 0.4$ heating, $0.3$ cooling | | 7 | $D_h = D_s - D_o$ | Annulus equivalent diameter | For $h_o$ calculation | | 8 | $\Delta P_f = 4f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Friction loss | For straight pipe | | 9 | $L = \dfrac{A}{\pi D_o}$ | Required pipe length | For DPHE design | | 10 | $A = \pi D_o L$ | Heat transfer area | Inner pipe outer surface | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility of DPHE in Food Industry:** > - Pasteurization of milk, juice, and beer > - Pre-heating of oil, ghee, and syrups > - Cooling of butter and chocolate mass > - Heating/cooling of viscous sauces, ketchup, and starch slurries > - Laboratory and pilot-scale studies due to simple construction # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference) ### Step 1: Differential Heat Transfer Consider an elemental length $dL$ of the exchanger. Heat transferred across the elemental area $dA = \pi D_o \, dL$ is: $$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$ ### Step 2: Energy Balance on the Hot Fluid Heat lost by hot fluid over length $dL$: $$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot dT_h$$ ### Step 3: Energy Balance on the Cold Fluid Heat gained by cold fluid over length $dL$: $$dQ = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c$$ ### Step 4: Co-current Flow Case For co-current flow, both fluids traverse the same direction, and the difference $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ is the key variable. Differentiating: $$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$ ### Step 5: Substituting Energy Balances From the energy balance, $dT_h = -\dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}$ and $dT_c = \dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}$: $$d(\Delta T) = -dQ \cdot \left[\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right]$$ ### Step 6: Eliminating dQ Since $dQ = U \cdot \pi D_o \cdot (T_h - T_c) \, dL = U \cdot \pi D_o \cdot \Delta T \, dL$: $$d(\Delta T) = -U \pi D_o \cdot \Delta T \cdot dL \cdot \left[\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right]$$ ### Step 7: Separation of Variables Rearranging: $$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \pi D_o \cdot \left[\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right] dL$$ ### Step 8: Integration over the Full Length Integrating from inlet ($\Delta T_1$ at $L=0$) to outlet ($\Delta T_2$ at $L$): $$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \pi D_o \left[\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right] \int_0^L dL$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U \pi D_o L \left[\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right]$$ ### Step 9: Recovering the Total Heat Duty The total heat duty from the hot fluid stream: $$Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$ ### Step 10: Eliminating the Logarithmic Term Combining the integrated relation with the total energy balance yields the LMTD: $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Counter-Flow vs. Co-Current Boundary Conditions | Case | Hot Fluid Inlet Position | Cold Fluid Inlet Position | $\Delta T_1$ | $\Delta T_2$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Co-current** | Inlet | Inlet (same end) | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ | | **Counter-current** | Inlet | Outlet (opposite end) | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ | ## 3.3 Complete Numerical Design Worked Example (DPHE) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is a KTU 2024-Model Question type. Master the method.** **Problem Statement (Standard KTU numerical):** A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool $5000 \, \text{kg/h}$ of hot milk ($c_p = 3.9 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, density $= 1030 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, viscosity $= 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$, $k = 0.55 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$) from $80°C$ to $35°C$ using chilled water ($c_p = 4.18 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$) entering at $15°C$ and exiting at $30°C$. The inner pipe is **stainless steel, 1-inch nominal (OD = 33.4 mm, ID = 28.9 mm, $k_w = 16 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$)**. The outer pipe ID is **52.5 mm**. Assume a fouling factor of $0.0002 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$ on both sides. Calculate the required pipe length. ### Solution — Step by Step **Step 1: Heat Duty from the Hot Fluid** $$Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot \Delta T_h = \left(\frac{5000}{3600}\right) \cdot 3.9 \cdot (80 - 35)$$ $$\dot{m}_h = 1.389 \, \text{kg/s}$$ $$Q = 1.389 \cdot 3.9 \cdot 45 = 243.75 \, \text{kW}$$ **Step 2: Cold Water Mass Flow Rate (Energy Balance)** $$Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot \Delta T_c$$ $$\dot{m}_c = \frac{243.75}{4.18 \cdot 15} = \frac{243.75}{62.7} = 3.888 \, \text{kg/s}$$ **Step 3: LMTD (Counter-current)** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 80 - 30 = 50°C$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 35 - 15 = 20°C$$ $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{50 - 20}{\ln(50/20)} = \frac{30}{\ln(2.5)} = \frac{30}{0.9163} = 32.74°C$$ **Step 4: Velocity in Inner Pipe** $$A_i = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0289)^2 = 6.56 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2$$ $$v_i = \frac{\dot{m}_h}{\rho \cdot A_i} = \frac{1.389}{1030 \cdot 6.56 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.055 \, \text{m/s}$$ **Step 5: Reynolds Number (Inner Pipe — Milk)** $$Re_i = \frac{\rho v_i D_i}{\mu} = \frac{1030 \cdot 2.055 \cdot 0.0289}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 61{,}194$$ Since $Re > 10{,}000$ → **Turbulent flow**. **Step 6: Prandtl Number (Milk)** $$Pr = \frac{c_p \mu}{k} = \frac{3900 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.55} = 7.09$$ **Step 7: Nusselt Number (Dittus-Boelter, heating case, $n = 0.4$)** $$Nu = 0.023 \cdot (61{,}194)^{0.8} \cdot (7.09)^{0.4}$$ $$Nu = 0.023 \cdot 5345.6 \cdot 2.20 = 270.5$$ **Step 8: Inner Heat Transfer Coefficient** $$h_i = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D_i} = \frac{270.5 \cdot 0.55}{0.0289} = 5148 \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$ **Step 9: Annulus Hydraulic Diameter** $$D_h = D_s - D_o = 0.0525 - 0.0334 = 0.0191 \, \text{m}$$ **Step 10: Annulus Velocity & Reynolds (Water)** $$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_s^2 - D_o^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0525^2 - 0.0334^2) = 1.39 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$ $$v_o = \frac{3.888}{1000 \cdot 1.39 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.797 \, \text{m/s}$$ $$Re_o = \frac{1000 \cdot 2.797 \cdot 0.0191}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 53{,}422$$ **Step 11: Outer Heat Transfer Coefficient** $$Nu_o = 0.023 \cdot (53{,}422)^{0.8} \cdot (7.0)^{0.4} \approx 256.2$$ $$h_o = \frac{256.2 \cdot 0.6}{0.0191} = 8049 \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$ **Step 12: Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient** $$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{5148} + \frac{0.00225}{16} + \frac{0.0289}{0.0334 \cdot 8049} + 0.0002 + 0.0002$$ $$\frac{1}{U_o} = 1.943 \times 10^{-4} + 1.406 \times 10^{-4} + 1.075 \times 10^{-4} + 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\frac{1}{U_o} = 8.424 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$U_o = 1187 \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$ **Step 13: Required Area and Length** $$A = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{243{,}750}{1187 \cdot 32.74} = 6.27 \, \text{m}^2$$ $$L = \frac{A}{\pi D_o} = \frac{6.27}{\pi \cdot 0.0334} = 59.7 \, \text{m}$$ **Step 14: Practical Implementation** A single straight pipe of 60 m is impractical → split into **multiple hairpin bends** of 3 m each → 20 hairpin sections (in practice, 2-3 sections are standard for industrial use). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Double Pipe Heat Exchanger — Component Architecture (Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Fluid Inlet
T h,in] --> B[Inner Pipe
Turbulent Flow Region] B --> C[Heat Transfer Wall
Stainless Steel Tube] C --> D[Annular Region
Counter-Flow Cold Fluid] D --> E[Cold Fluid Outlet
T c,out] F[Cold Fluid Inlet
T c,in] --> D D --> G[Hot Fluid Outlet
T h,out] H[End Fittings
Return Bends / U-Bends] --> B H --> D ``` ## 4.2 LMTD Calculation Flow Chart (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Given Inlet and Outlet Temperatures] --> B{Flow Arrangement} B -->|Co-current| C[Delta T1 = Th,in minus Tc,in] B -->|Counter-current| D[Delta T1 = Th,in minus Tc,out] C --> E[Delta T2 = Th,out minus Tc,out] D --> F[Delta T2 = Th,out minus Tc,in] E --> G[Apply LMTD Formula] F --> G G --> H[Check: Delta T1 not equal to Delta T2] H --> I[Compute LMTD in degrees C or Kelvin] I --> J[Use in Q equals U times A times LMTD] ``` ## 4.3 DPHE Design Calculation Sequence (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Mass Flow and Energy Balance] --> B[Step 2: Compute Q in kW] B --> C[Step 3: Calculate LMTD] C --> D[Step 4: Calculate h_i from Dittus-Boelter] D --> E[Step 5: Calculate h_o from annulus formula] E --> F[Step 6: Build Resistance Network, Compute U] F --> G[Step 7: Solve A from Q equals U times A times LMTD] G --> H[Step 8: Solve Length L from A equals pi times D_o times L] H --> I[Step 9: Pressure Drop Check] I --> J{Acceptable?} J -->|Yes| K[Final Design] J -->|No| L[Adjust Diameter or Number of Hairpins] L --> D ``` ## 4.4 Resistance Network — Heat Transfer Path ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Bulk Milk] -->|Convective 1 over h_i| B[Inner Wall Surface] B -->|Conduction x_w over k_w| C[Outer Wall Surface] C -->|Convective D_i over D_o times h_o| D[Cold Bulk Water] A1[Fouling R_f,i] -.-> B C1[Fouling R_f,o] -.-> C ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the resistance network:** > Heat must pass through each "box" in series. The largest resistance dominates the overall U. In milk pasteurization, **fouling (milk-stone)** is often the largest single resistance, which is why CIP (clean-in-place) protocols are critical in food industry heat exchangers. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). Why is it preferred over arithmetic mean temperature difference for heat exchanger design?** **Model Answer:** The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) is defined as the temperature difference that, if applied uniformly over the entire heat transfer area, would give the same total heat transfer as actually occurs with the varying temperature difference. $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ LMTD is preferred over arithmetic mean because the temperature profile along a heat exchanger is **exponential** (since $dQ \propto \Delta T$). The arithmetic mean is accurate only when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$, which occurs in condensers and reboilers with phase change at constant temperature. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **List any three advantages of counter-current flow over co-current flow in a double-pipe heat exchanger.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Higher LMTD** for the same inlet and outlet temperatures, leading to a smaller required heat transfer area. 2. **The cold fluid can be heated to a temperature higher than the hot fluid outlet temperature** (impossible in co-current), enabling greater energy recovery. 3. **More uniform temperature difference** along the length, reducing thermal stress on the pipe wall (important for viscous food products like ghee or chocolate). 4. **Higher overall mean temperature driving force**, giving better energy efficiency. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper] **(a)** Explain the **overall heat transfer coefficient** for a double-pipe heat exchanger, deriving the resistance-in-series expression. List typical values of $U$ for various food industry services. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool $8000 \, \text{kg/h}$ of edible oil ($c_p = 2.1 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $\rho = 920 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $k = 0.16 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$, $\mu = 50 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$) from $150°C$ to $60°C$ using cooling water entering at $25°C$ and leaving at $50°C$. The inner pipe has OD $= 42.4 \, \text{mm}$, ID $= 36.1 \, \text{mm}$, $k_w = 16 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$. Outer pipe ID $= 70 \, \text{mm}$. Assume fouling factors: $R_{f,i} = 0.0004$, $R_{f,o} = 0.0002 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$. Calculate the required pipe length. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation of $U$:** For steady-state heat transfer through concentric pipes, the rate of heat flow through each layer must equal $Q$ (steady state). Through the inner convection film: $Q = h_i \cdot A_i \cdot (T_b - T_{w,i})$, so $A_i (T_b - T_{w,i}) = Q/h_i$ Through the pipe wall: $Q = \dfrac{k_w A_{lm}}{x_w} (T_{w,i} - T_{w,o})$ Through the outer convection film: $Q = h_o \cdot A_o \cdot (T_{w,o} - T_{c,b})$ For thin walls, $A_i \approx A_o \approx A$, summing: $$\frac{Q}{A} \left[\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}\right] = (T_b - T_{c,b}) = \Delta T_{LMTD}$$ Including fouling resistances: $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{D_i}{D_o h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$ **Typical $U$ values in food industry:** | Service | $U \, [\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}]$ | |---|---| | Milk → water (DPHE) | 1000 – 1500 | | Steam → milk (plate) | 3000 – 5000 | | Condensing steam → oil | 800 – 1200 | | Oil → water (DPHE) | 200 – 500 | **(b) Numerical Solution:** $\dot{m}_h = 8000/3600 = 2.222 \, \text{kg/s}$ $$Q = 2.222 \times 2.1 \times (150 - 60) = 420 \, \text{kW}$$ Cold water: $\dot{m}_c = 420 / (4.18 \times 25) = 4.02 \, \text{kg/s}$ LMTD: $\Delta T_1 = 150 - 50 = 100$, $\Delta T_2 = 60 - 25 = 35$ $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{100 - 35}{\ln(100/35)} = \frac{65}{1.0498} = 61.92°C$$ Velocities & $Re$ (similar method to worked example): $h_i \approx 145 \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ (laminar/turbulent borderline due to high viscosity, may use Sieder-Tate) $h_o \approx 5200 \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ $$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{145} + \frac{0.00315}{16} + \frac{0.0361}{0.0424 \times 5200} + 0.0004 + 0.0002$$ $$\frac{1}{U_o} = 6.897 \times 10^{-3} + 1.97 \times 10^{-4} + 1.64 \times 10^{-4} + 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$U_o \approx 126 \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$ $$A = \frac{420{,}000}{126 \times 61.92} = 53.8 \, \text{m}^2$$ $$L = \frac{53.8}{\pi \times 0.0424} = 404 \, \text{m}$$ Practically split into ~14 hairpins of 30 m each (or 6 sections of 6 m each using 75 mm outer pipe). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** Discuss the various **flow arrangements** in a double-pipe heat exchanger with neat sketches. Compare counter-current and co-current flow with respect to LMTD, area, and outlet temperatures. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DPHE cools $3000 \, \text{kg/h}$ of tomato pulp ($c_p = 3.85$, density $= 1050$, $k = 0.55$, $\mu = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$) from $90°C$ to $40°C$ using chilled water at $10°C$ (exit at $25°C$). Inner pipe: 1.5" nominal (OD = 48.3 mm, ID = 41.0 mm, $k_w = 16$). Outer pipe ID = 80 mm. Fouling factor on pulp side $= 0.0006$, water side $= 0.0003 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$. Determine the heat transfer area and pipe length. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Flow Arrangements:** | Type | Description | Use | |---|---|---| | **Co-current (parallel)** | Both fluids enter same end | Limited use; for viscous fluids, mixing | | **Counter-current** | Fluids enter opposite ends | **Most common**; highest efficiency | | **Cross-flow** | Streams cross at 90° (not in DPHE) | Shell & tube, plate exchangers | **Comparison Table (must reproduce in exam):** | Parameter | Co-current | Counter-current | |---|---|---| | LMTD (same endpoints) | Smaller | **Larger** | | Area required | Larger | **Smaller** | | Cold fluid max exit $T$ | $T_{c,out} < T_{h,out}$ | $T_{c,out}$ can exceed $T_{h,out}$ | | Temperature profile | Convergent | Parallel | **(b) Numerical Solution (similar method):** $\dot{m}_h = 0.833 \, \text{kg/s}$, $Q = 0.833 \times 3.85 \times 50 = 160.4 \, \text{kW}$ $\dot{m}_c = 160.4 / (4.18 \times 15) = 2.56 \, \text{kg/s}$ LMTD: $\Delta T_1 = 90 - 25 = 65$, $\Delta T_2 = 40 - 10 = 30$ $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{65 - 30}{\ln(65/30)} = \frac{35}{0.7726} = 45.3°C$$ After calculating $h_i$, $h_o$, and $U$ (similar procedure to worked example): Expected $U \approx 850 - 1100 \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ $$A = \frac{160{,}400}{U \times 45.3}, \quad L = \frac{A}{\pi \times 0.0483}$$ (Typical answer: $A \approx 4.5 \, \text{m}^2$, $L \approx 30 \, \text{m}$) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (As Per KTU Valuation Key Patterns):** > 1. **Forgetting area correction** $\dfrac{D_i}{D_o}$ on the annulus-side resistance. This alone can cost **2 marks**. > 2. **Using Sieder-Tate without viscosity correction** at the wall — important for heating viscous food products. > 3. **Mixing up counter-current vs. co-current $\Delta T_1$, $\Delta T_2$ definitions.** Always draw the temperature profile first. > 4. **Skipping units conversion** (kg/h → kg/s; °C = K is allowed, but kW ≠ W). > 5. **Not including fouling factor** in $U$ calculation — for food industry, this is **mandatory**. > 6. **Forgetting to check the Reynolds number regime** before applying Dittus-Boelter (valid only for turbulent, $Re > 10{,}000$). > 7. **Ignoring viscosity correction term** $(\mu_b/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ for high-viscosity food fluids. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print this for last-minute revision):** - **Master Equation:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ — memorize first. - **LMTD formula:** LMTD = (ΔT₁ − ΔT₂) / ln(ΔT₁ / ΔT₂) - **Counter-current gives higher LMTD** than co-current → smaller area. - **Resistance network for $U$:** convective (inner) + wall conduction + convective (annulus) + fouling. - **Annulus hydraulic diameter:** $D_h = D_s - D_o$ (this is the *equivalent diameter* for the outer side). - **Dittus-Boelter** is valid for $Re > 10{,}000$ (turbulent) and $0.7 < Pr < 16000$. - **Sieder-Tate** is preferred for viscous food fluids with significant viscosity variation across the wall. - **Fouling factor** for milk in HTST pasteurization ≈ 0.0002 – 0.0006 m²K/W; for sugar syrups can be up to 0.001. - **Pipe length calculation:** $L = A / (\pi D_o)$ — use the **outer diameter** of the inner pipe. - **Pressure drop** must be checked: $\Delta P = 4f (L/D) (\rho v^2 / 2)$ for straight pipe; add equivalent length of 60$D$ for each return bend. - **Acceptable velocity range in food DPHE:** $1 - 3 \, \text{m/s}$ (tube side), $0.5 - 2 \, \text{m/s}$ (annulus side). - **In counter-current, cold fluid exit temperature CAN exceed hot fluid exit temperature** — a key conceptual distinction from co-current. - **For 14-mark numerical:** always draw the temperature profile diagram first, label all four temperatures, then apply LMTD. - **Number of hairpin bends in practice** is usually 2-4 (not 20 as raw calculation suggests) — industrial design optimizes via multiple parallel passes or larger diameter. - **For very viscous fluids** like chocolate, $Re$ may be laminar → use **Sieder-Tate laminar correlation** instead.

topics.title: Plate Heat Exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT722 - FOOD PROCESS AND EQUIPMENT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Plate Heat Exchanger # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** is a compact, highly efficient thermal processing device composed of a series of corrugated metal plates clamped together within a rigid frame. The plates are separated by gaskets that direct two process fluids into alternate channels, allowing heat transfer across the thin plate walls while keeping the fluids physically isolated. The corrugations (chevron patterns) induce turbulence and increase the effective heat transfer area per unit volume. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A plate heat exchanger is a device that transfers heat between two fluids through a pack of thin, corrugated metal plates, with each fluid confined between alternate plates by elastomeric gaskets, and the plates pressed together by a fixed and movable pressure plate." ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a stack of hot, freshly-made dosas in a restaurant kitchen. Each dosa is thin, flat, and made of metal (cast iron tawa). A cook flips them rapidly to transfer heat to batter quickly. Now imagine stacking **50 such hot plates** with **thin layers of batter sandwiched between each**, where hot oil flows on one side and cold batter on the other. The heat from oil instantly cooks the batter, but the oil and batter never physically mix. The "dosa stack" is your **Plate Heat Exchanger** – many thin plates, large surface area, minimal distance between hot and cold fluid, and a gasket (the tawa's non-stick coating) to keep things separated. > [!NOTE] > **Why thin plates?** Thinner plates = shorter heat conduction path = higher heat transfer rate. Stainless steel plates are typically **0.5 mm to 0.8 mm** thick. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |-----------|---------------|--------------| | Plate Thickness | **0.5 – 0.8 mm** | Conduction resistance minimized | | Plate Spacing (gap) | **1.5 – 5 mm** | Holds fluid between plates | | Operating Pressure | **up to 25 bar** | Typical industrial limit | | Operating Temperature | **−40 °C to +180 °C** | Limited by gasket material | | Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) | **3000 – 7000 W/m²·K** | Very high vs. shell-and-tube (~1000–2000) | | Area per Plate | **0.1 – 2.5 m²** | Industry common range | | Chevron Angle (β) | **30°, 45°, 60°** | Determines turbulence strength | | Plate Material | **SS 304 / SS 316 / Titanium** | Food-grade corrosion resistance | > [!TIP] > For food applications, **SS 316** is preferred due to superior chloride resistance (critical for salty/dairy products). ## 1.4 KTU Board Expectation Snapshot A KTU examiner typically expects students to define PHE, sketch its construction, identify the role of gaskets, and explain why PHEs are preferred in **HTST (High Temperature Short Time) pasteurization** of milk. Marks are heavily allocated to **labelled diagrams** and **flow arrangement** clarity. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Major Components of a Plate Heat Exchanger The PHE consists of the following functional parts, each engineered for a specific role: ### 2.1.1 Stationary Frame Components - **Fixed Frame Plate (Carrying Bar Assembly):** Holds the fixed connections (fluid inlets/outlets). Anchors the pack. - **Movable Pressure Plate:** Slides along the carrying bar to compress the plate pack. - **Columns / Carrying Bars:** Two vertical pillars that support the weight of all plates and provide alignment. - **Tie Rods / Bolting:** Clamping mechanism to compress the plate pack with uniform pressure (typically **20–50 kN/m²** of plate surface). ### 2.1.2 Active Heat Transfer Components - **Heat Transfer Plates:** Thin corrugated metal sheets; the heart of the equipment. Four ports punched at corners allow fluid entry/exit. - **Elastomeric Gaskets:** Seal each plate, segregating fluids into alternate channels. Materials: **NBR, EPDM, Viton (FKM), PTFE**. - **Fluid Distribution Zones:** Areas around the four ports that distribute flow uniformly across the plate width. ### 2.1.3 Connection Components - **Nozzles / Connections:** Located on the fixed frame plate; sized based on flow rate and velocity (typically **2–4 m/s**). - **Protective Sheets:** Inlet/outlet transitional plates that protect the main heat transfer plates from inlet jet erosion. > [!NOTE] > **Gasket Selection Matrix (KTU High-Yield):** > - **NBR (Nitrile):** Dairy, beverages, water — up to **110 °C** > - **EPDM:** Steam, hot water, CIP chemicals — up to **150 °C** > - **Viton (FKM):** Oils, aggressive chemicals — up to **180 °C** > - **PTFE:** Highly corrosive / pharmaceutical — up to **200 °C** ## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The PHE operates on the principle of **indirect heat transfer across plate walls with induced turbulence**. **Step 1 — Plate Compression:** The plates are compressed within the frame, creating sealed channels of 1.5–5 mm gap between adjacent plates. **Step 2 — Counter-Current Flow:** The hot fluid enters through one corner port and flows downward (or upward) through alternate channels. The cold fluid enters through the opposite port and flows through the remaining channels in the opposite direction, achieving **true counter-current flow**. **Step 3 — Heat Transfer:** Heat passes through the thin stainless steel plate (0.5–0.8 mm) by conduction, then is removed by the cooler fluid via forced convection on the other side. **Step 4 — Turbulence Promotion:** The chevron corrugations disrupt the boundary layer, creating high turbulence even at low Reynolds numbers (**Re > 10** for laminar-like flow due to plate geometry). This dramatically increases the heat transfer coefficient. **Step 5 — Collection and Exit:** Fluids are collected at the opposite corner ports and exit the heat exchanger. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why counter-current is critical:** It provides the highest **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**, maximizing heat transfer efficiency and enabling **Temperature Cross** situations (where the cold outlet can exceed the hot outlet) — impossible in co-current or shell-and-tube. ## 2.3 Classification of Plate Heat Exchangers | Type | Description | Typical Application | |------|-------------|---------------------| | **Gasketed PHE** | Standard; plates separated by removable gaskets | Dairy, beverage, general food | | **Welded PHE** | Plates welded in pairs (semi-welded) for hazardous media | Refrigerants, aggressive chemicals | | **Brazed PHE** | Vacuum-brazed plate pack (no gaskets) | Refrigeration, HVAC | | **Spiral PHE** | Plates rolled into spirals; handles fouling/slurries | Sludge, viscous fluids | | **Plate-Fin PHE** | Brazed aluminum fins between plates | Cryogenic, aerospace | | **Plate Evaporator/Condenser** | Plates with refrigerant side | Steam-heated or refrigerant-based PHE | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Heat duty (sensible heat) | W | | 2 | $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Overall heat transfer (single pass) | W | | 3 | $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lmtd} \cdot F$ | With correction factor F | W | | 4 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD | K or °C | | 5 | $A = n \, A_p$ | Total area = plates × area per plate | m² | | 6 | $N_p = \dfrac{A}{A_p} + 1$ | Number of plates (2-stream arrangement) | dimensionless | | 7 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_h} + \dfrac{x}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_c} + R_f$ | Overall resistance | m²·K/W | | 8 | $Nu = a \, Re^b \, Pr^{1/3}$ | Nusselt correlation (chevron plates) | dimensionless | | 9 | $f = c \, Re^{d}$ | Friction factor (plate geometry) | dimensionless | | 10 | $\Delta P = 4 f \dfrac{L}{D_e} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2} \dfrac{N_p}{2}$ | Pressure drop (per stream) | Pa | | 11 | $D_e = \dfrac{2 b}{\sqrt{\beta}}$ | Equivalent hydraulic diameter | m | | 12 | $NTU = \dfrac{U A}{\dot{m}_{min} c_{p,min}}$ | NTU for effectiveness method | dimensionless | | 13 | $\varepsilon = f(NTU, C_r)$ | Effectiveness | dimensionless | | 14 | $C_r = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{min} c_{p,min}}{\dot{m}_{max} c_{p,max}}$ | Heat capacity rate ratio | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the $+1$ in the plate count formula. For an N-channel, (N+1) plates are needed because both end channels need plates on the outside. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering PHEs dominate the **HTST pasteurization of milk** (72 °C × 15 s) due to: 1. **Rapid heating and cooling** — critical for short-time microbial kill. 2. **CIP compatibility** — gasket-removable design allows cleaning-in-place. 3. **Precise temperature control** — counter-current flow ensures tight ΔT control. 4. **Compact footprint** — 50–80% smaller than shell-and-tube for the same duty. **Other food industry applications:** - Juice pasteurization (mango, apple, orange) - Beer wort cooling in breweries - Yogurt and cheese milk heating - Edible oil tempering - Sauce and soup heating - Reverse-osmosis feed pre-heating # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Plates and Passes for a PHE ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Heat Duty For a food process where a fluid is heated or cooled without phase change, the heat duty is calculated from the energy balance: $$Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$$ Where: - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of the fluid (**kg/s**) - $c_p$ = specific heat capacity at constant pressure (**J/kg·K**) - $\Delta T$ = temperature change of the fluid (**K** or **°C**) ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) For a true counter-current flow heat exchanger, the mean driving force is the LMTD: $$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ Where: - $\Delta T_1$ = temperature difference at the **inlet** end of the exchanger - $\Delta T_2$ = temperature difference at the **outlet** end of the exchanger Both $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are taken in the same flow direction (e.g., always as hot fluid minus cold fluid). ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Effective ΔT with Correction Factor F A multi-pass PHE does NOT operate in true counter-current flow throughout. The correction factor F accounts for the deviation from true counter-current flow: $$\Delta T_{effective} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}$$ The factor F is read from charts or calculated using the helper parameters: $$P = \frac{T_{h,out} - T_{h,in}}{T_{c,in} - T_{h,in}}$$ $$R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$$ For an N-stream arrangement (1 pass each side), the F factor is calculated as: $$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2 + 1} \cdot \ln\left[ \frac{(1 - P)/(1 - PR)}{(1 - PR) / (1 - PR/R)} \right]}{(R - 1) \cdot \ln \left[ \frac{2 - P(R + 1 + \sqrt{R^2 + 1})}{2 - P(R + 1 - \sqrt{R^2 + 1})} \right]}$$ This is complex; in KTU exams, students typically refer to **standard F-chart values** or use single-pass counter-current (F = 1). ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Required Heat Transfer Area Rearranging the heat transfer equation: $$A_{required} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}}$$ ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Number of Plates Each plate has a known effective heat transfer area $A_p$ (from manufacturer catalogue, typically 0.1 to 2.5 m²). For a 2-stream arrangement (1 pass on each side, all channels active): $$N_p = \frac{A_{required}}{A_p} + 1$$ The **+1** accounts for the extra plate that closes the last channel. ### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Milk is pasteurized using a PHE. Cold milk enters at 4 °C and leaves at 72 °C. Hot water enters at 95 °C and leaves at 78 °C. Required heat duty: Q = 250 kW. Use U = 5500 W/m²·K. Each plate has $A_p = 0.5 \, m^2$. Find the number of plates. **Solution:** Step 1: Compute temperature differences (counter-current flow): $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 95 - 72 = 23 \, °C$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 78 - 4 = 74 \, °C$$ Step 2: Compute LMTD: $$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{23 - 74}{\ln(23 / 74)} = \frac{-51}{\ln(0.3108)} = \frac{-51}{-1.1699} = 43.6 \, °C$$ Step 3: For 1-pass counter-current, F = 1. Step 4: Compute required area: $$A = \frac{250{,}000}{5500 \times 1 \times 43.6} = \frac{250{,}000}{239{,}800} = 1.043 \, m^2$$ Step 5: Number of plates: $$N_p = \frac{1.043}{0.5} + 1 = 2.086 + 1 = 3.086$$ Round up to **4 plates** (always the next higher integer to ensure sufficient area). > [!TIP] > In practice, **safety factor of 1.1 to 1.25** is added to account for fouling and operational variations. ## 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Drop in a Plate Channel ### 3.2.1 Equivalent Diameter for Plate Channels A plate channel is not a circular pipe, so we use the equivalent diameter (hydraulic diameter): $$D_e = \frac{4 \times \text{Flow Cross-Section}}{\text{Wetted Perimeter}}$$ For a plate channel of width W and gap b between plates: $$D_e = \frac{2 b}{\sqrt{\beta}}$$ Where $\beta$ is the **area enlargement factor** accounting for chevron corrugations ($\beta \approx 1.15$ to $1.25$ for typical chevron angles). ### 3.2.2 Friction Factor (Plate Geometry) For chevron-angled plates, the friction factor is correlated as: $$f = c \cdot Re^{d}$$ Where c and d are constants based on chevron angle: - **β = 30°:** c = 47, d = −0.65 (soft plates) - **β = 45°:** c = 14, d = −0.36 - **β = 60°:** c = 7, d = −0.18 (hard plates) ### 3.2.3 Pressure Drop Across a Single Pass $$\Delta P_{channel} = 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D_e} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$ Where: - L = effective plate length - $\rho$ = fluid density - v = mean channel velocity ### 3.2.4 Total Pressure Drop (Multi-Pass) If the stream passes through the exchanger in N passes: $$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{channel} \times N_{passes}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Design Rule:** Pressure drop should not exceed **~70 kPa (10 psi)** per stream for normal operation; higher values signal need to add plates (reducing velocity) or split streams. ## 3.3 KTU Design Procedure — Stepwise Algorithm The standard PHE design follows a structured sequence: 1. **Define Process Data:** Mass flow rates, inlet/outlet temperatures, pressures, fluid properties (ρ, μ, k, c_p), and operating constraints. 2. **Calculate Heat Duty Q:** $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ for both streams. Verify energy balance. 3. **Determine LMTD:** Compute $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$; calculate LMTD using logarithmic formula. 4. **Estimate Overall U:** From experience or literature — for milk-water systems, U ≈ **5000–6000 W/m²·K**. 5. **Compute Required Area:** $A = Q / (U \cdot F \cdot LMTD)$. 6. **Select Plate Geometry:** Choose chevron angle, plate dimensions, and area per plate $A_p$ from manufacturer catalogue. 7. **Determine Number of Plates:** $N_p = A / A_p + 1$, rounded up to nearest integer. 8. **Check Velocity and Pressure Drop:** Calculate Re, f, and ΔP. Verify ΔP < 70 kPa. 9. **Iterate:** Adjust plate count, chevron angle, or pass arrangement if constraints are violated. 10. **Select Number of Passes:** Decide 1-pass, 2-pass, or series-parallel based on temperature approach and pressure drop. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Construction of a Gasketed PHE ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Fixed Frame Plate
