Less Than 70 kPa} L -->|Yes| M[Finalize Design] L -->|No| N[Increase Plates
or Modify Geometry] N --> J ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define a Plate Heat Exchanger and list its main components.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model QP]` **Model Answer:** A Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) is a compact heat transfer device that consists of a series of thin corrugated metal plates clamped together within a frame. Two fluids flow in alternate channels and exchange heat through the plate walls, with the fluids kept separate by gaskets. **Main Components:** 1. **Heat transfer plates** – Thin (0.5–0.8 mm) corrugated stainless steel sheets, typically SS 316 for food applications. 2. **Gaskets** – Elastomeric seals (NBR, EPDM, Viton) that direct fluid flow and prevent cross-contamination. 3. **Fixed and movable frame plates** – Provide structural support and allow compression of the plate pack. 4. **Tie rods / clamping bolts** – Apply uniform pressure on the plate pack. 5. **Carrying bars** – Two vertical pillars supporting the plate pack. 6. **Connection nozzles** – Inlet and outlet ports for the two fluids. 7. **Protective sheets / transition plates** – Protect the main plates from inlet jet erosion. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **gaskets** as a separate component. This is a 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation. --- ### **Question 2: Why are plate heat exchangers preferred over shell-and-tube exchangers in HTST milk pasteurization?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP]` **Model Answer:** PHEs are preferred for HTST (High Temperature Short Time, 72 °C × 15 s) milk pasteurization due to the following reasons: 1. **High heat transfer coefficient** (U ≈ 5000–6000 W/m²·K vs. 1000–2000 W/m²·K for shell-and-tube), enabling rapid heating essential for the short 15-second hold time. 2. **Compact and small footprint** — occupies 50–80% less floor space. 3. **True counter-current flow** — provides maximum LMTD and tight temperature control. 4. **Easy to clean** — plates can be removed and CIP (cleaned-in-place) is more effective. 5. **Easy capacity expansion** — just add more plates to the frame. 6. **High turbulence** — corrugated plates ensure turbulent flow even at low Reynolds numbers, preventing fouling and scaling. 7. **Lower hold-up volume** — less product is exposed to heat, preserving milk quality and flavor. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A: Design a Plate Heat Exchanger for Milk Pasteurization** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Statement:** Milk is to be pasteurized from **4 °C to 72 °C** at a mass flow rate of **2 kg/s**. Hot water is available at **95 °C** and must not be cooled below **78 °C**. Take $c_{p,milk} = 3.9 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$, $c_{p,water} = 4.18 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$, and the overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 5500 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$. Each plate has an effective area of $0.5 \, m^2$. Assume single-pass counter-current flow. **Solve the following sub-parts:** #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks] Calculate the heat duty, LMTD, and required heat transfer area.** **Step 1: Calculate heat duty on the milk side.** $$Q = \dot{m}_{milk} \cdot c_{p,milk} \cdot \Delta T_{milk}$$ $$Q = 2 \, kg/s \times 3.9 \, kJ/kg \cdot K \times (72 - 4) \, K$$ $$Q = 2 \times 3.9 \times 68 = 530.4 \, kW$$ **Step 2: Verify water-side balance (to confirm water flow).** $$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{Q}{c_{p,water} \cdot \Delta T_{water}} = \frac{530{,}400}{4180 \times (95 - 78)}$$ $$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{530{,}400}{71{,}060} = 7.465 \, kg/s$$ This is reasonable; a 7.5 kg/s water flow is available. **Step 3: Calculate temperature differences at both ends (counter-current).** At end 1 (hot water inlet, milk outlet): $$\Delta T_1 = 95 - 72 = 23 \, °C$$ At end 2 (hot water outlet, milk inlet): $$\Delta T_2 = 78 - 4 = 74 \, °C$$ **Step 4: Compute LMTD.** $$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{23 - 74}{\ln(23/74)} = \frac{-51}{\ln(0.3108)} = \frac{-51}{-1.1699} = 43.6 \, °C$$ **Step 5: Calculate required heat transfer area.** $$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}}$$ For single-pass counter-current, $F = 1$: $$A = \frac{530{,}400 \, W}{5500 \, W/m^2 \cdot K \times 1 \times 43.6 \, K} = \frac{530{,}400}{239{,}800} = 2.21 \, m^2$$ **Part (a) Result: Q = 530.4 kW, LMTD = 43.6 °C, A = 2.21 m²** #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks] Determine the number of plates and check if the design is feasible.** **Step 1: Compute number of plates (2-stream arrangement).** $$N_p = \frac{A}{A_p} + 1 = \frac{2.21}{0.5} + 1 = 4.42 + 1 = 5.42$$ Rounded up to next integer: **N_p = 6 plates** (with 3 hot channels and 3 cold channels). **Step 2: Apply a 15% safety factor for fouling and operational variations.** $$A_{design} = 2.21 \times 1.15 = 2.54 \, m^2$$ $$N_{p,design} = \frac{2.54}{0.5} + 1 = 5.08 + 1 = 6.08 \rightarrow 7 \, plates$$ **Step 3: Check pressure drop (qualitative).** For milk at 2 kg/s across 4 channels (3 cold-side channels), the mean velocity: $$v = \frac{\dot{m} / \rho}{N_{channels} \times b \times W}$$ Assuming density of milk = 1030 kg/m³, b = 3 mm, W = 0.4 m: $$v = \frac{2/1030}{3 \times 0.003 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.00194}{0.0036} = 0.54 \, m/s$$ This is well within the recommended velocity range (0.3–0.8 m/s), confirming the design is feasible. **Part (b) Result: N_p = 6 to 7 plates, design feasible.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Students often forget to round **up** the plate count and may not include the **+1** term. Also, the **fouling factor** (R_f) is frequently omitted — always include a 10–20% safety margin on heat transfer area. --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice): Plate Heat Exchanger — Chevron Geometry, Gaskets, and Pressure Drop** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]` **Statement:** A PHE is used to heat **8000 kg/h of fruit juice** from **20 °C to 85 °C** using steam at **120 °C** condensing at **110 °C**. The juice has $c_p = 3.85 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ and the overall U is estimated as **4500 W/m²·K**. Plate dimensions: 0.5 m wide × 1.2 m long, with 3 mm gap between plates, chevron angle 45°. **Solve the following sub-parts:** #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks] Calculate the number of plates, plate area, and LMTD.** **Step 1: Heat duty.** $$\dot{m} = 8000 \, kg/h = 2.222 \, kg/s$$ $$Q = 2.222 \times 3.85 \times (85 - 20) = 2.222 \times 3.85 \times 65 = 556 \, kW$$ **Step 2: Temperature differences.** For steam-condensing (isothermal hot side) and counter-current juice: $$\Delta T_1 = T_{steam,in} - T_{juice,out} = 120 - 85 = 35 \, °C$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{steam,out} - T_{juice,in} = 110 - 20 = 90 \, °C$$ **Step 3: LMTD.** $$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{35 - 90}{\ln(35/90)} = \frac{-55}{\ln(0.3889)} = \frac{-55}{-0.9439} = 58.27 \, °C$$ **Step 4: Required area.** $$A = \frac{556{,}000}{4500 \times 1 \times 58.27} = \frac{556{,}000}{262{,}215} = 2.12 \, m^2$$ **Step 5: Plate area per plate.** $$A_p = 0.5 \times 1.2 = 0.6 \, m^2$$ Effective area (after deducting gasket/port area, ~10% reduction): $$A_{p,eff} = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54 \, m^2$$ **Step 6: Number of plates.** $$N_p = \frac{2.12}{0.54} + 1 = 3.93 + 1 = 4.93 \rightarrow 5 \, plates$$ **Part (a) Result: Q = 556 kW, LMTD = 58.27 °C, N_p = 5 plates** #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks] Estimate the pressure drop on the juice side and verify it is within design limits.** **Step 1: Determine channel velocity.** Using 4 active juice channels (5 plates → 4 channels on juice side, since steam occupies alternate channels): Mass velocity per channel: $$G = \frac{2.222 / 4}{0.5 \times 0.003} = \frac{0.5556}{0.0015} = 370.4 \, kg/s \cdot m^2$$ **Step 2: Reynolds number.** Assuming juice density ρ = 1050 kg/m³, μ = 2 × 10⁻³ Pa·s: $$Re = \frac{G \cdot D_e}{\mu}$$ For 45° chevron, $\beta \approx 1.2$: $$D_e = \frac{2 \times 0.003}{\sqrt{1.2}} = \frac{0.006}{1.0954} = 0.00548 \, m$$ $$Re = \frac{370.4 \times 0.00548}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.03}{0.002} = 1015$$ **Step 3: Friction factor (45° chevron, soft plates).** $$f = 14 \times Re^{-0.36} = 14 \times (1015)^{-0.36} = 14 \times 0.0613 = 0.858$$ **Step 4: Pressure drop per pass.** $$v = \frac{G}{\rho} = \frac{370.4}{1050} = 0.353 \, m/s$$ $$\Delta P = 4 \times 0.858 \times \frac{1.2}{0.00548} \times \frac{1050 \times 0.353^2}{2}$$ $$\Delta P = 4 \times 0.858 \times 218.98 \times 65.40$$ $$\Delta P = 49{,}127 \, Pa \approx 49.1 \, kPa$$ **Step 5: Verify against design limit (< 70 kPa).** $$\Delta P = 49.1 \, kPa < 70 \, kPa \quad \checkmark \, \text{Design is feasible.}$$ **Part (b) Result: ΔP = 49.1 kPa, design acceptable.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** For condensation on one side, F = 1 is used (no F correction needed for isothermal hot stream). However, students frequently apply unnecessary F corrections. Also, the chevron-angle-dependent friction factor constants (c and d) must be quoted correctly — memorizing c = 14, d = −0.36 for 45° chevron is essential. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before your exam!)** - [x] **Definition:** PHE = series of thin corrugated plates with alternating fluid channels. - [x] **Plate material:** SS 316 (food-grade, chloride-resistant). - [x] **Plate thickness:** 0.5–0.8 mm. - [x] **Chevron angle:** 30° (soft, low ΔP), 45° (medium), 60° (hard, high U). - [x] **U value range:** 3000–7000 W/m²·K (vs. 1000–2000 for shell-and-tube). - [x] **Flow type:** True counter-current (highest efficiency). - [x] **Heat duty formula:** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ - [x] **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ - [x] **Area formula:** $A = Q / (U \cdot F \cdot LMTD)$ - [x] **Plate count:** $N_p = A/A_p + 1$ — **round UP**, **always +1**. - [x] **F correction factor:** F = 1 for single-pass counter-current and isothermal (condensing/boiling) hot side. - [x] **Pressure drop limit:** < 70 kPa per stream. - [x] **Equivalent diameter:** $D_e = 2b / \sqrt{\beta}$ - [x] **Friction factor:** $f = c \cdot Re^{d}$ (constants depend on chevron angle). - [x] **Gasket materials:** NBR (≤ 110 °C), EPDM (≤ 150 °C), Viton (≤ 180 °C). - [x] **Safety factor on area:** Add 10–20% to account for fouling. - [x] **Key food applications:** HTST milk pasteurization, juice pasteurization, beer wort cooling, CIP heating. - [x] **Plate count rounding:** Always round UP to the next integer to ensure sufficient area. - [x] **CIP compatibility:** PHEs are preferred due to gasketed design (easy disassembly and cleaning). - [x] **Temperature cross:** Possible in PHE due to true counter-current flow; impossible in shell-and-tube (single pass). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Exam Mistakes (Top 5 KTU Valuation Deductions):** > 1. Forgetting the **+1** in plate count formula (−1 mark). > 2. Using the wrong **LMTD sign convention** (always $T_{hot} - T_{cold}$) (−1 mark). > 3. Not rounding **plate count UP** to the nearest integer (−0.5 mark). > 4. Omitting **fouling factor** and design safety margin (−1 mark). > 5. Forgetting to specify **gasket material** in design answers (−0.5 mark).

topics.title: Shell and Tube Heat Exchangers
Design of Evaporators, Mass and Energy Balance, Single Effect and Multiple Effect Evaporators
Design of Dryers, Mass and Energy Balance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT722 - FOOD PROCESS AND EQUIPMENT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design of Dryers, Mass and Energy Balance # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Drying? (KTU Formal Definition) **Drying** is defined as the unit operation in which the moisture content of a wet solid food material is reduced by the application of heat, causing simultaneous **heat and mass transfer**. The process involves thermal vaporization of water (or other volatile liquid) from the food matrix into an unsaturated gas phase (usually hot air). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Drying in food processing is fundamentally a **simultaneous heat and mass transfer (SHMT)** operation governed by the second law of thermodynamics. The water activity ($a_w$) of the food must drop below levels that inhibit microbial growth and biochemical deterioration to ensure shelf stability. ### Moisture Content Representations (Critical KTU Concept) The moisture content of food can be expressed on two bases: **1. Wet Basis (w.b.) — Mass of water per unit total mass** $$X_w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} = \frac{m_w}{m_{total}}$$ **2. Dry Basis (d.b.) — Mass of water per unit mass of bone-dry solid** $$X_d = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Formula (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):** > > $$X_d = \frac{X_w}{1 - X_w} \quad \text{and} \quad X_w = \frac{X_d}{1 + X_d}$$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Laundry" Model Imagine you have just washed a cotton shirt. The drying process is exactly analogous: | Real-World Event | Engineering Equivalent | |------------------|------------------------| | Wringing out water | **Mechanical dewatering** (NOT drying) | | Hanging shirt on a line under the sun | **Solar/convective drying** | | Using a hot hair dryer | **Hot-air convective drying** | | Putting shirt in a hot oven | **Conduction drying** (tray dryer) | | Spray painting fine mist into hot air | **Spray drying** (milk powder) | | Tumbling shirt in a heated drum | **Rotary/Drum drying** | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for Students:** Drying removes moisture by **evaporation**, not by mechanical means. This requires latent heat of vaporization (**2257 kJ/kg** at 100°C, **2454 kJ/kg** at 20°C). The energy cost is dominated by this latent heat demand, making dryer design an exercise in **energy efficiency engineering**. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Food Dryers (KTU High-Yield Chart) Food dryers are classified by **heat transfer mode** and **physical state of feed**: ### A. By Mode of Heat Transfer 1. **Convection Dryers (Direct/Hot-Air)** — Hot air contacts the wet solid - Tray/Cabinet Dryer - Tunnel Dryer - Belt/Conveyor Dryer - Fluidized Bed Dryer - Spray Dryer - Rotary Dryer - Pneumatic/Flash Dryer 2. **Conduction Dryers (Indirect)** — Heat supplied through a metal surface - Drum/Roller Dryer - Vacuum Shelf Dryer - Paddle Dryer 3. **Radiation Dryers** — Infrared or microwave radiation - IR Dryer, Microwave Dryer 4. **Dielectric Dryers** — High-frequency electric fields > [!IMPORTANT] > **Spray Dryer** is the most important industrial dryer for liquid foods (milk, coffee, egg, fruit juices). It atomizes the feed into fine droplets (10–500 μm) in a co-current or counter-current hot air stream. The residence time is only **5–30 seconds**, making it ideal for heat-sensitive foods. --- ## 1.4 Bound vs. Free Moisture — Critical Distinction | Property | Free Moisture | Bound Moisture | |----------|---------------|----------------| | **Definition** | Water in excess of equilibrium content | Water held in cells/ capillaries below $X^*$ | | **Location** | On surface & in large pores | Inside capillaries, cell walls | | **Removal ease** | Easy, surface evaporation | Difficult, diffusion-controlled | | **Energy required** | Just latent heat | Latent heat + desorption energy | | **Removal rate** | Constant rate period | Falling rate period | Where $X^*$ = **equilibrium moisture content** of the food at given T and RH. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Drying Process — Two Distinct Periods The drying rate curve of a food material exhibits two principal regimes: ### Period I — Constant Rate Drying Period (CRDP) - Surface moisture behaves as if the solid were a **free water body**. - Drying rate is governed entirely by **external heat/mass transfer** (air-side resistance). - Surface temperature ≈ wet-bulb temperature of air. - Equation: $$\frac{dX}{dt} = -\frac{h}{\rho_s \lambda_w X_1} (T_{air} - T_{wet-bulb}) = \text{constant}$$ ### Period II — Falling Rate Drying Period (FRDP) - Surface dries out; moisture must migrate from interior by **diffusion** (liquid or vapor). - Drying rate decreases continuously with moisture content. - Surface temperature rises towards dry-bulb temperature. - Rate controlled by Fick's law: $$N_A = -D_{eff} \frac{\partial C_w}{\partial x}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for design:** CRDP requires only psychrometric + heat transfer calculations. FRDP requires solving Fick's diffusion equation with appropriate boundary conditions, often using Page's model: $\frac{X - X^*}{X_0 - X^*} = \exp(-k t^n)$ where **k** is the drying constant and **n** ≈ 0.5–1.2. --- ## 2.2 Psychrometric Fundamentals (Pre-requisite for Energy Balance) **Humidity ratio (Y)** — kg water vapor per kg dry air: $$Y = 0.622 \frac{P_w}{P_{total} - P_w}$$ **Relative Humidity (RH):** $$RH = \frac{P_w}{P_{sat}(T)} \times 100\%$$ **Humid volume (V_H)** — m³ per kg dry air: $$V_H = \frac{R T}{P_{total} - P_w} \left(1 + 1.6078 Y\right)$$ **Saturated volume** at $P_{total} = 101.325$ kPa: $$V_{H,sat} = (0.00283 + 0.00456\,Y_s)\, T \quad [T \text{ in K}]$$ **Humid heat (C_H)** — kJ per (kg dry air · K): $$C_H = 1.005 + 1.88\, Y$$ **Enthalpy of moist air (H)** — kJ per kg dry air: $$H = C_H (T - T_{ref}) + Y\, \lambda_w$$ with $T_{ref} = 0°C$, $\lambda_w = 2501$ kJ/kg (commonly used value at 0°C reference). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Most students confuse $Y$ (humidity ratio, kg/kg dry air) with **absolute humidity** in g/m³. They are related but NOT identical. The KTU answer script valuation always demands $Y$ in kg water/kg dry air (the standard engineering basis). --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Drying Calculations | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Remarks** | |-------|--------------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | Moisture (wet basis) | $X_w = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s}$ | kg water/kg total | | 2 | Moisture (dry basis) | $X_d = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$ | kg water/kg dry solid | | 3 | Wet→Dry conversion | $X_d = \dfrac{X_w}{1-X_w}$ | KTU favourite 3-mark Q | | 4 | Free moisture | $X_f = X - X^*$ | kg/kg dry basis | | 5 | Total Mass Balance | $F = S + V$ | kg/h | | 6 | Moisture Component Balance | $F X_{F} = S X_{S} + V Y_{V}$ | — | | 7 | Mass of bone-dry solid | $S = \dfrac{F(1-X_{F,w})}{1-X_{S,w}}$ | kg/h (invariant) | | 8 | Moisture evaporated | $V = S(X_1 - X_2)$ | kg water/h | | 9 | Enthalpy of moist air | $H = C_H T + Y(2501 + 1.88T)$ | kJ/kg dry air | | 10 | Humid heat | $C_H = 1.005 + 1.88 Y$ | kJ/(kg·K) | | 11 | Air requirement | $L_s = \dfrac{V}{Y_2 - Y_1}$ | kg dry air/h | | 12 | Total heat Q | $Q = L_s(H_2 - H_1) + S c_{ps}(T_{S2}-T_{S1}) - F c_{pw}T_{F1}$ | kJ/h | | 13 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{used}}{Q_{input}} = \dfrac{V\lambda_w + Sc_{ps}\Delta T}{Q}$ | % | | 14 | Log mean temp. diff. | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | K | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In food industry practice, dryer design is governed by four pillars: 1. **Product quality** — retention of colour, vitamins, aroma, and rehydration capacity. 2. **Energy economy** — drying alone accounts for **10–25%** of national industrial energy use globally. 3. **Throughput economics** — capital cost vs. operating cost. 4. **Food safety** — preventing Maillard browning, case-hardening, microbial recontamination. > [!TIP] > **Spray dryer design** for milk powder: typical evaporation capacity = **3000–25000 kg water/h** with inlet air at **180–220°C** and outlet at **80–100°C**. The co-current design keeps particle temperature low (~60°C) despite very high inlet gas temperature — critical for preserving protein functionality. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Balance on a Continuous Dryer Consider a generic co-current continuous convective dryer with: - **F** = feed rate of wet solid (kg/h) - **S** = discharge rate of dried solid (kg/h) - **V** = water evaporated (kg/h) - **L** = inlet dry air rate (kg dry air/h) - $Y_1, Y_2$ = inlet/outlet humidity ratios (kg/kg dry air) ### Step 1 — Overall Total Mass Balance Mass entering = Mass leaving (steady state, no accumulation). $$F + L(1 + Y_1) = S + L(1 + Y_2)$$ > **Logic:** The wet air mass flow at inlet equals $L(1+Y_1)$ because each kg of dry air carries $Y_1$ kg of water vapor. Same at outlet. ### Step 2 — Simplification Cancelling the dry-air term $L$ from both sides: $$F = S + V \quad \text{where} \quad V = L(Y_2 - Y_1)$$ > **Logic:** Solid feed minus product equals evaporated water. Dry air serves only as a carrier for the vapor. ### Step 3 — Component Balance on Water Water entering with feed + water entering with air = water leaving with product + water leaving with air. $$F X_{F,w} + L Y_1 = S X_{S,w} + L Y_2$$ > **Logic:** The bone-dry solid mass is invariant across the dryer, so all water removed from solid must appear as additional humidity in the exhaust air. ### Step 4 — Invariant Dry Solid Define $S$ = mass flow rate of bone-dry solid (kg dry solid/h): $$S = F(1 - X_{F,w}) = S_{product}(1 - X_{S,w})$$ > **Logic:** Since dry matter (carbohydrate, protein, fat, ash) is conserved and only water leaves, the bone-dry solid rate is the same on both sides. ### Step 5 — Expressing Evaporated Water in Dry Basis The water evaporated per hour can be written cleanly as: $$\boxed{V = S \cdot (X_1 - X_2)}$$ where $X_1, X_2$ are the **dry-basis** moisture contents of feed and product respectively. > **Logic:** The dry solid carries $X_1$ kg water at inlet and $X_2$ kg water at outlet; the difference is what becomes vapor and exits with the air. ### Step 6 — Required Dry Air Flow Rate Setting $V = L(Y_2 - Y_1)$ from Step 2: $$\boxed{L = \frac{S(X_1 - X_2)}{Y_2 - Y_1}}$$ > **Logic:** To remove $V$ kg/h of water when the air can absorb $(Y_2 - Y_1)$ kg/kg, you need $L = V/(Y_2-Y_1)$ kg/h of dry air. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Energy Balance (General Drying) ### Step 1 — System Definition Control volume: dryer shell, including feed inlet, product outlet, air inlet, air outlet. Steady state, adiabatic (no heat loss case first). ### Step 2 — Energy In vs. Energy Out $$Q + H_{air,in}\cdot L + H_{feed}\cdot F = H_{air,out}\cdot L + H_{product}\cdot S + Q_{loss}$$ where $Q$ = heat supplied externally (kJ/h), $Q_{loss}$ = heat lost to surroundings. ### Step 3 — Adiabatic Limit ($Q_{loss}=0$, $Q=0$) For an **adiabatic dryer** (no external heating, no losses), the energy equation reduces to: $$L H_1 + F H_F = L H_2 + S H_S$$ Expanding using the **inlet air as reference stream**: $$L H_1 + F c_{pw} T_{F1} = L H_2 + S c_{ps} T_{S2}$$ > **Logic:** We neglect water in feed enthalpy because feed temperature is usually modest. The dry air and water vapor enthalpies are bundled into $H$ (per kg dry air). ### Step 4 — Solving for Air Requirement (Adiabatic) Rearranging: $$L(H_2 - H_1) = F c_{pw} T_{F1} - S c_{ps} T_{S2}$$ Or substituting $F \approx S + V$: $$L(H_2 - H_1) = (S+V)c_{pw}T_{F1} - S c_{ps}T_{S2}$$ > **Logic:** The sensible heat of the entering wet solid must be "consumed" by the air; the air cools from $H_2$ (lower) to $H_1$ (higher) condition across the dryer. ### Step 5 — General Case with External Heating Adding external heat $Q$ and heat loss $Q_{loss}$: $$\boxed{Q = L(H_2 - H_1) + S c_{ps}(T_{S2} - T_{S1}) - F c_{pw} T_{F1} + Q_{loss}}$$ > **Logic:** Total heat input must account for (a) raising air enthalpy, (b) heating dry solid, (c) supplying feed sensible heat, (d) compensating losses. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style) > **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 style]:** > > A continuous tray dryer processes **500 kg/h** of wet food from initial moisture content **80% (w.b.)** to final moisture content **10% (w.b.)**. Air enters at **80°C** with humidity ratio $Y_1 = 0.015$ kg/kg dry air and leaves at **50°C** with $Y_2 = 0.035$ kg/kg dry air. The wet food enters at **25°C** and exits at **45°C**. Specific heat of dry solid $c_{ps} = 1.5$ kJ/(kg·K), specific heat of water $c_{pw} = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K). Assume no heat loss. > > Calculate: > (a) The mass of bone-dry solid $S$ and water evaporated $V$. > (b) The dry air requirement $L$. > (c) The total heat input $Q$ if adiabatic. > (d) The thermal efficiency of the dryer. ### Part (a): Bone-Dry Solid $S$ and Water Evaporated $V$ **Step A1:** Convert wet basis to dry basis. $$X_1 = \frac{X_{w1}}{1-X_{w1}} = \frac{0.80}{1-0.80} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$ $$X_2 = \frac{X_{w2}}{1-X_{w2}} = \frac{0.10}{1-0.10} = \frac{0.10}{0.90} = 0.1111 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$ **Step A2:** Bone-dry solid flow rate. $$S = F(1 - X_{F,w}) = 500 \times (1 - 0.80) = 500 \times 0.20 = 100 \text{ kg dry solid/h}$$ > **Logic:** 80% of feed is water; remaining 20% is bone-dry solid. Therefore, $S = 100$ kg/h. **Step A3:** Water evaporated. $$V = S(X_1 - X_2) = 100 \times (4.0 - 0.1111) = 100 \times 3.8889 = 388.89 \text{ kg water/h}$$ > **Logic:** The bone-dry solid carries 4 kg water at inlet and 0.111 kg water at outlet per kg dry solid; difference (3.889 kg) is evaporated per kg dry solid. ### Part (b): Dry Air Requirement $L$ $$L = \frac{V}{Y_2 - Y_1} = \frac{388.89}{0.035 - 0.015} = \frac{388.89}{0.020} = 19{,}444.4 \text{ kg dry air/h}$$ > **Logic:** Air must absorb 388.89 kg water/h; each kg of dry air can carry only 0.020 kg of additional moisture (from 0.015 to 0.035). Therefore, $L = 19{,}444$ kg/h. ### Part (c): Total Heat Input (Adiabatic, $Q_{loss}=0$) **Step C1:** Compute humid heat at inlet. $$C_{H1} = 1.005 + 1.88 Y_1 = 1.005 + 1.88(0.015) = 1.005 + 0.0282 = 1.0332 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$ **Step C2:** Compute inlet air enthalpy $H_1$ (using $H = C_H T + 2501 Y$ with $T$ in °C). $$H_1 = 1.0332 \times 80 + 2501 \times 0.015 = 82.656 + 37.515 = 120.17 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$ **Step C3:** Compute humid heat at outlet. $$C_{H2} = 1.005 + 1.88 Y_2 = 1.005 + 1.88(0.035) = 1.005 + 0.0658 = 1.0708 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$ **Step C4:** Compute outlet air enthalpy $H_2$. $$H_2 = 1.0708 \times 50 + 2501 \times 0.035 = 53.54 + 87.535 = 141.075 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$ **Step C5:** Apply energy balance. $$Q = L(H_2 - H_1) + S c_{ps}(T_{S2} - T_{S1}) - F c_{pw} T_{F1}$$ Substituting values: $$Q = 19{,}444.4 \times (141.075 - 120.17) + 100 \times 1.5 \times (45 - 25) - 500 \times 4.186 \times 25$$ Compute each term: $$L(H_2-H_1) = 19{,}444.4 \times 20.905 = 406{,}441 \text{ kJ/h}$$ $$S c_{ps}\Delta T_S = 100 \times 1.5 \times 20 = 3{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ $$F c_{pw} T_{F1} = 500 \times 4.186 \times 25 = 52{,}325 \text{ kJ/h}$$ $$Q = 406{,}441 + 3{,}000 - 52{,}325 = 357{,}116 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 357.1 \text{ MJ/h}$$ ### Part (d): Thermal Efficiency Heat utilized for evaporation + sensible heating of product: $$Q_{useful} = V \lambda_w + S c_{ps}(T_{S2} - T_{S1})$$ $$Q_{useful} = 388.89 \times 2257 + 3{,}000 = 877{,}764 + 3{,}000 = 880{,}764 \text{ kJ/h}$$ $$\eta = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{useful} + Q} \times 100 = \frac{880{,}764}{880{,}764 + 357{,}116} \times 100 = 71.1\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Validation:** The thermal efficiency of 71% is realistic for a well-designed continuous tray dryer (typical range 50–80%). Higher efficiencies are achieved with **exhaust air heat recovery** (recirculation through heat exchangers) and **insulation**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Food Dryers | **Parameter** | **Tray** | **Drum** | **Spray** | **Fluidized Bed** | **Rotary** | |--------------|----------|----------|-----------|-------------------|------------| | Feed state | Paste/granules | Liquid/paste | Liquid | Granules | Granules | | Particle size | 1–50 mm | — | 10–500 μm | 0.5–5 mm | 0.5–10 mm | | Air temp. (°C) | 60–150 | 130–180 | 150–220 | 80–200 | 100–400 | | Evap. rate (kg/m²·h) | 1–3 | 30–75 | 25–200 | 50–250 | 30–100 | | Residence time | 4–48 h | 3–30 s | 5–30 s | 1–10 min | 5–60 min | | Thermal efficiency (%) | 50–70 | 70–85 | 55–75 | 65–80 | 60–75 | | Capital cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium | High | | Best for | Fruits, herbs | Milk, soup | Milk, coffee | Vegetables, sugar | Cereals, grains | | Heat-sensitive OK? | Moderate | No | Yes | Moderate | No | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Continuous Convective Dryer ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["WET FEED
F kg/hr
XF moisture"] nodeB["PREHEATER
Heat Exchanger"] nodeC["HOT AIR INLET
T1 degC
Y1 humidity"] nodeD["DRYING CHAMBER
Tray or Spray"] nodeE["DRIED PRODUCT
S kg/hr
XS moisture"] nodeF["EXHAUST AIR
T2 degC
Y2 humidity"] nodeG["CYCLONE
Particulate recovery"] nodeH["WET FEED INLET
25 degC"] nodeI["EXTERNAL HEAT
Q kJ/hr"] nodeJ["BAG FILTER
Fines collection"] nodeH --> nodeA nodeA --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeD nodeI --> nodeB nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK["STACK / VENT"] style nodeD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px style nodeB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px style nodeG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Spray Dryer Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INLET_SYSTEM nodeA1["LIQUID FEED TANK
F kg/hr concentrate"] nodeA2["HIGH-PRESSURE PUMP
or Rotary Atomizer"] nodeA3["ATOMIZER
10 to 500 um droplets"] end subgraph AIR_SYSTEM nodeB1["AMBIENT AIR INLET"] nodeB2["AIR FILTER"] nodeB3["HEATER / BURNER
T1 = 180 to 220 C"] nodeB4["BLOWER / FAN"] end subgraph DRYING_CHAMBER nodeC1["CHAMBER BODY
co-current flow"] nodeC2["DROPLET-AIR CONTACT
5 to 30 sec residence"] nodeC3["DRY PARTICLE FORMATION"] end subgraph RECOVERY_SYSTEM nodeD1["CYCLONE SEPARATOR"] nodeD2["BAG FILTER"] nodeD3["PRODUCT HOPPER"] nodeD4["EXHAUST FAN
T2 = 80 to 100 C"] end nodeA1 --> nodeA2 --> nodeA3 --> nodeC1 nodeB1 --> nodeB2 --> nodeB3 --> nodeB4 --> nodeC1 nodeC1 --> nodeC2 --> nodeC3 --> nodeD1 --> nodeD3 nodeC1 --> nodeD4 nodeD3 --> nodeD2 style nodeC1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px style nodeC2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style nodeD1 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mass and Energy Balance Streams ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INLET_STREAMS nodeIn1["F: Wet Feed
kg/hr solid plus water"] nodeIn2["L: Dry Air
kg dry air/hr"] nodeIn3["LY1: Water in Air
Y1 = 0.015 kg/kg"] nodeIn4["Q: External Heat
kJ/hr from heater"] end subgraph DRYER_BODY["DRYER CONTROL VOLUME"] nodeCV1["Heat Transfer
Convection"] nodeCV2["Mass Transfer
Water to Vapor"] nodeCV3["Two Stages
Constant then Falling Rate"] end subgraph OUTLET_STREAMS nodeOut1["S: Dried Product
kg dry solid/hr"] nodeOut2["V: Water Vapor
kg/hr evaporated"] nodeOut3["LY2: Exhaust Air
Y2 = 0.035 kg/kg"] nodeOut4["Qloss: Heat Loss
to surroundings"] end nodeIn1 --> nodeCV2 nodeIn2 --> nodeIn3 nodeIn3 --> nodeCV1 nodeIn4 --> nodeCV1 nodeCV1 --> nodeCV3 nodeCV2 --> nodeCV3 nodeCV3 --> nodeOut1 nodeCV3 --> nodeOut2 nodeCV2 --> nodeOut3 nodeCV1 --> nodeOut4 style nodeCV3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px style nodeOut1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px style nodeOut3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Drying Rate Curve Phases ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["INITIAL
ADJUSTMENT
Short transient"] nodeB["PERIOD I
CONSTANT RATE
Surface saturation"] nodeC["CRITICAL POINT
Xc moisture
transition"] nodeD["PERIOD II-A
FIRST FALLING
Wet zones shrink"] nodeE["PERIOD II-B
SECOND FALLING
Internal diffusion"] nodeF["EQUILIBRIUM
X star moisture
drying stops"] nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF style nodeB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **Visualization Tip:** Plot drying rate $R$ (kg/m²·h) vs. moisture $X$. The plateau (Period I) is followed by a knee (critical point) and a downward curve (Period II). Most food drying time is spent in Period II, making diffusion coefficients the critical design parameters. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define the term "equilibrium moisture content" of a food material and explain its significance in dryer design.** **Model Answer:** Equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$) is the moisture content to which a wet food material will eventually lose or gain moisture when exposed to air at a specific **temperature (T)** and **relative humidity (RH)** for an infinite time. At equilibrium, the vapor pressure of water in the food equals the partial pressure of water vapor in the surrounding air, resulting in **zero net mass transfer**. **Significance in dryer design:** 1. **Lower bound for drying:** The product moisture content ($X_2$) can never be reduced below $X^*$ under given air conditions. To achieve lower $X_2$, either air temperature must be raised or RH must be lowered. 2. **Determines free moisture:** The amount of water actually removable is $X - X^*$, not the total water present. 3. **Governs energy requirement:** Only free moisture demands evaporation energy; bound moisture remains in the food. 4. **Storage stability planning:** Final product moisture must be safely above $X^*$ for the storage RH to prevent moisture re-absorption. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Differentiate between dry-basis and wet-basis moisture content. A food contains 60% moisture (w.b.). Express it on a dry basis.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Formula | Range | Use | |-------|---------|-------|-----| | **Wet basis (w.b.)** | $X_w = m_w / (m_w + m_s)$ | 0 to 1 | Industrial trade, regulatory labelling | | **Dry basis (d.b.)** | $X_d = m_w / m_s$ | 0 to ∞ | Engineering design, mass balance | **Numerical:** $$X_d = \frac{X_w}{1 - X_w} = \frac{0.60}{1 - 0.60} = \frac{0.60}{0.40} = 1.5 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$ --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module Choice — Answer ANY ONE of the following:** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** (a) **[7 Marks]** A continuous conveyor dryer handles **2000 kg/h** of a wet food from **65% (w.b.)** to **12% (w.b.)** moisture. Air enters at **95°C** with humidity ratio **0.020 kg/kg dry air** and leaves at **55°C** with humidity ratio **0.040 kg/kg dry air**. The wet food enters at **30°C** and the product leaves at **50°C**. Take $c_{pw} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_{ps} = 1.6$ kJ/(kg·K), and latent heat of vaporization = **2300 kJ/kg**. Assume no heat loss. Calculate: (i) Mass flow rate of bone-dry solid (kg/h). (ii) Mass of water evaporated per hour. (iii) Required dry air flow rate (kg dry air/h). (b) **[7 Marks]** For the same dryer, compute: (i) The total heat input rate $Q$ in kJ/h using the energy balance equation. (ii) The thermal efficiency of the dryer. (iii) The volume of inlet air per second (m³/s) if the humid volume at inlet conditions is **0.95 m³/kg dry air**. #### Model Solution **Part (a):** **(i) Bone-dry solid rate:** $$S = F(1 - X_{F,w}) = 2000 \times (1 - 0.65) = 2000 \times 0.35 = 700 \text{ kg dry solid/h}$$ **(ii) Water evaporated:** Convert to dry basis first: $$X_1 = \frac{0.65}{1-0.65} = 1.857 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad X_2 = \frac{0.12}{1-0.12} = 0.1364 \text{ kg/kg}$$ $$V = S(X_1 - X_2) = 700 \times (1.857 - 0.1364) = 700 \times 1.7206 = 1204.4 \text{ kg water/h}$$ **(iii) Dry air flow rate:** $$L = \frac{V}{Y_2 - Y_1} = \frac{1204.4}{0.040 - 0.020} = \frac{1204.4}{0.020} = 60{,}220 \text{ kg dry air/h}$$ **Part (b):** **(i) Total heat input:** Compute humid heat values: $$C_{H1} = 1.005 + 1.88(0.020) = 1.0426 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$ $$C_{H2} = 1.005 + 1.88(0.040) = 1.0802 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$ Compute enthalpies: $$H_1 = 1.0426 \times 95 + 2501 \times 0.020 = 99.047 + 50.020 = 149.07 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$H_2 = 1.0802 \times 55 + 2501 \times 0.040 = 59.411 + 100.040 = 159.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Apply energy balance: $$Q = L(H_2 - H_1) + S c_{ps}(T_{S2} - T_{S1}) - F c_{pw} T_{F1}$$ Term 1: $$L(H_2 - H_1) = 60{,}220 \times (159.45 - 149.07) = 60{,}220 \times 10.38 = 625{,}084 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Term 2: $$S c_{ps}\Delta T_S = 700 \times 1.6 \times (50-30) = 22{,}400 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Term 3: $$F c_{pw} T_{F1} = 2000 \times 4.18 \times 30 = 250{,}800 \text{ kJ/h}$$ $$Q = 625{,}084 + 22{,}400 - 250{,}800 = 396{,}684 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 396.7 \text{ MJ/h}$$ **(ii) Thermal efficiency:** $$Q_{useful} = V\lambda_w + S c_{ps}\Delta T_S = 1204.4 \times 2300 + 22{,}400 = 2{,}770{,}120 + 22{,}400 = 2{,}792{,}520 \text{ kJ/h}$$ $$\eta = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{useful} + Q} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}792{,}520}{2{,}792{,}520 + 396{,}684} \times 100 = 87.6\%$$ **(iii) Volumetric flow rate of inlet air:** $$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{L \times V_H}{3600} = \frac{60{,}220 \times 0.95}{3600} = \frac{57{,}209}{3600} = 15.89 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** (a) **[7 Marks]** Describe the working principle, construction, and industrial applications of a **spray dryer** for food products. Compare its merits and demerits against a **tray dryer**. (b) **[7 Marks]** A batch tray dryer processes **100 kg** of wet food from **75% (w.b.)** to **8% (w.b.)** moisture in **6 hours**. The drying is entirely in the **constant rate period** with a drying rate of **2.5 kg water/m²·h**. Calculate: (i) Total water evaporated (kg). (ii) The drying area required (m²). (iii) If the latent heat of vaporization is **2400 kJ/kg**, compute the total heat duty (kJ). #### Model Solution **Part (a):** Spray Dryer — Comprehensive Description **Working Principle:** Liquid feed (concentrate, solution, slurry) is atomized into a fine spray of 10–500 μm droplets inside a large chamber. Hot air (150–220°C) contacts the droplets co-currently, evaporating moisture in 5–30 seconds. Dried particles are separated via cyclone and bag filter. **Construction:** Stainless steel cylindrical-conical chamber; atomizer (centrifugal disc, pressure nozzle, or pneumatic nozzle); air heater (indirect gas-fired or steam); feed pump; product recovery cyclone; exhaust fan; instrumentation panel. **Applications:** - Skim milk powder, whole milk powder - Instant coffee, tea extracts - Egg powder, whey protein - Fruit juice powders (orange, mango) - Infant formula, probiotics - Starch derivatives, enzymes **Merits vs. Tray Dryer:** | Parameter | Spray Dryer | Tray Dryer | |-----------|-------------|------------| | Product form | Powder, free-flowing | Lumps, slabs, granules | | Drying time | Seconds | Hours | | Heat damage | Minimal (co-current) | Possible (long exposure) | | Energy efficiency | 55–75% | 50–70% | | Capital cost | High | Low | | Capacity | Very high | Moderate | | Suitable for liquid feeds | Yes | No | **Demerits of Spray Dryer:** - High capital cost - Requires atomization system maintenance - Wall deposition issues with sticky feeds - Limited to pumpable liquids **Part (b):** Batch Tray Dryer Calculation **(i) Total water evaporated:** Convert w.b. to d.b.: $$X_1 = \frac{0.75}{1-0.75} = 3.0 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad X_2 = \frac{0.08}{1-0.08} = 0.0870 \text{ kg/kg}$$ Bone-dry solid: $$S = 100 \times (1 - 0.75) = 25 \text{ kg}$$ Water evaporated: $$V = 25 \times (3.0 - 0.0870) = 25 \times 2.913 = 72.83 \text{ kg water}$$ **(ii) Drying area:** $$A = \frac{V}{R \times t} = \frac{72.83}{2.5 \times 6} = \frac{72.83}{15} = 4.855 \text{ m}^2 \approx 4.86 \text{ m}^2$$ **(iii) Total heat duty:** $$Q = V \times \lambda_w = 72.83 \times 2400 = 174{,}783 \text{ kJ} \approx 174.8 \text{ MJ}$$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes — Read Carefully Before Exam:** > > 1. **Wet vs. Dry basis confusion:** Forgetting to convert $X_w \rightarrow X_d$ before applying $V = S(X_1 - X_2)$ costs **3–4 marks** per question. Always check the basis stated in the problem. > > 2. **Sign errors in energy balance:** Mixing up "+" and "−" for feed sensible heat ($F c_{pw} T_{F1}$) is the most frequent error. Feed contributes sensible heat, so it is **subtracted** from $Q$ (reduces external heating needed). > > 3. **Using wrong latent heat value:** At **0°C**, $\lambda_w = 2501$ kJ/kg; at **100°C**, $\lambda_w = 2257$ kJ/kg. The KTU board allows either, but you **must declare** which temperature you're using. > > 4. **Forgetting to convert kg/h to kg/s** for volumetric flow rate calculations. Remember: $\dot{V} = L \cdot V_H / 3600$ where $3600$ s/h. > > 5. **Not specifying units:** Every numerical answer must carry units. KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** per missing unit in long answers. > > 6. **Using $C_H$ formula incorrectly:** Some students write $C_H = 1.005 + 1.88 Y$ correctly but plug $Y$ in **g/kg** instead of kg/kg. This causes $C_H$ to be ~1000× wrong. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** > > ✅ **Core Definitions:** > - Drying = simultaneous heat + mass transfer operation. > - Equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$) = lower bound for product moisture at given $T$, RH. > - Free moisture = $X - X^*$ (actually removable portion). > - Bone-dry solid = invariant mass across the dryer. > > ✅ **Two Conversion Formulas (Must Memorize):** > - Wet → Dry: $X_d = X_w / (1 - X_w)$ > - Dry → Wet: $X_w = X_d / (1 + X_d)$ > > ✅ **Three Critical Mass Balance Equations:** > - $F = S + V$ (overall) > - $S = F(1 - X_{F,w})$ (bone-dry solid) > - $V = S(X_1 - X_2)$ (water evaporated, dry basis) > > ✅ **Air Flow Requirement:** > - $L = V / (Y_2 - Y_1)$ kg dry air/h > > ✅ **Psychrometric Formulas:** > - $C_H = 1.005 + 1.88 Y$ kJ/(kg dry air·K) > - $H = C_H T + 2501 Y$ kJ/kg dry air (T in °C) > > ✅ **Energy Balance Master Equation:** > - $Q = L(H_2 - H_1) + S c_{ps}\Delta T_S - F c_{pw} T_{F1} + Q_{loss}$ > > ✅ **Thermal Efficiency:** > - $\eta = Q_{useful} / Q_{input} \times 100\%$ > - $Q_{useful} = V \lambda_w + S c_{ps}\Delta T_S$ > > ✅ **Key Drying Concepts:** > - Period I (CRDP): surface-controlled, easy design. > - Period II (FRDP): diffusion-controlled, design via Page's or Fickian model. > - Critical moisture $X_c$: transition point between two periods. > > ✅ **Numerical Values to Remember:** > - $\lambda_w$ at 0°C = 2501 kJ/kg > - $\lambda_w$ at 100°C = 2257 kJ/kg > - $c_p$ of dry air = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K) > - $c_p$ of water vapor = 1.88 kJ/(kg·K) > - $c_{pw}$ = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K) > - Molecular ratio $M_w/M_{air}$ = 0.622 > > ✅ **Industrial Dryer Selection Guide:** > - **Liquid foods** → Spray dryer > - **Paste/viscous** → Drum dryer > - **Granular/crystalline** → Fluidized bed, Rotary > - **Heat-sensitive powders** → Freeze dryer (vacuum) > - **Fruits/vegetables slices** → Tray/Tunnel/Cabinet

topics.title: Psychrometrics, Drying Time Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT722 - FOOD PROCESS AND EQUIPMENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Psychrometrics, Drying Time Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Psychrometrics — Formal Definition

**Psychrometrics** is the branch of engineering thermodynamics and food process engineering that deals with the thermodynamic properties of **moist air** (a binary mixture of dry air and water vapour) and the simultaneous **heat and mass transfer** occurring between this air and the food material being dried. In the KTU 2024 scheme, psychrometrics forms the indispensable foundation for designing, analysing and scaling-up all drying operations (tray, fluidised bed, spray, drum and tunnel dryers) used in the food industry.

The discipline quantifies the state of humid air using a set of **primary psychrometric properties** (dry-bulb temperature, wet-bulb temperature, dew point, humidity ratio) and **derived psychrometric properties** (relative humidity, humid volume, humid heat, enthalpy, specific volume of dry air, adiabatic saturation temperature).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** *"Psychrometric chart, properties of moist air, wet bulb and dry bulb temperature, humidity measurement, drying principles, mechanism of moisture removal, drying rate curve, constant rate and falling rate periods, estimation of drying time."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Towel on a Sunny Day"

Imagine you hang a wet cotton towel on a clothesline on a **hot, dry, breezy** afternoon versus on a **cool, humid, still** morning. The towel dries within hours in the first case but remains damp all day in the second. **Why?**

- The **dry-bulb temperature** of air is like the *raw heat* available to evaporate water.
- The **relative humidity** is the *thirst* of the air — how much more moisture it can gulp down before becoming "full."
- The **wet-bulb temperature** is what the *towel itself feels* because the evaporating water cools it.
- The **humidity ratio** is the *actual amount of water* the air is carrying per kilogram of dry air.
- The **air velocity** is the *breeze* that keeps replacing saturated air around the towel with fresh dry air.

So psychrometrics is essentially the **"weather report" of drying air** that tells us how fast moisture will leave the food.

### 1.3 Drying Time Calculation — Formal Definition

**Drying time calculation** is the quantitative determination of the total residence time a food material must remain in a dryer to reduce its moisture content from an **initial value** $X_1$ (kg water/kg dry solid) to a **final value** $X_2$ (kg water/kg dry solid), using the principles of coupled heat-and-mass transfer. The calculation rests on interpreting the **drying rate curve** of the food and integrating the rate equation over the **constant-rate** and **falling-rate** periods.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used in Psychrometrics

- Molecular weight of dry air: $M_a = 28.97 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- Molecular weight of water: $M_w = 18.02 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$
- Gas constant for dry air: $R_a = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Gas constant for water vapour: $R_w = 0.4615 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Latent heat of vaporisation of water at **0 °C**: $h_{fg,0} = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (at **100 °C**: $h_{fg,100} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$)
- Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

> [!NOTE]
> In food drying, **dry-basis moisture content ($X$)** is the standard for engineering calculations, while **wet-basis moisture ($w$)** is used in commerce and quality control. The conversion: $X = w/(1-w)$ and $w = X/(1+X)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Composition & Key Properties of Moist Air

Moist air is treated as an **ideal binary gas mixture** of dry air (component $a$) and water vapour (component $w$). Its total pressure is given by **Dalton's Law of partial pressures**:

$$P_t = P_a + P_w = 101.325 \text{ kPa (at sea level)}$$

where:
- $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air (kPa)
- $P_w$ = partial pressure of water vapour (kPa)

The **humidity ratio** (also called absolute humidity, moisture content of air, or mixing ratio) is the mass of water vapour carried per unit mass of **bone-dry air**:

$$H = \frac{M_w \cdot P_w}{M_a \cdot (P_t - P_w)} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_t - P_w}$$

The **relative humidity (RH)** is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour to its saturation pressure at the same temperature:

$$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_s(T)} \times 100\%$$

The **percentage humidity** (psychrometric humidity) is the ratio of humidity ratio to the saturation humidity ratio at the same temperature:

$$H_p = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100\%$$

The **humid volume** $v_H$ is the volume occupied by 1 kg of **dry air plus the water vapour it carries**, in $\text{m}^3$/kg dry air:

$$v_H = \frac{R_a T}{P_a} = \frac{R_a T}{P_t - P_w}$$

The **humid heat** $c_H$ is the enthalpy needed to raise the temperature of 1 kg dry air (and its associated water vapour) by 1 K:

$$c_H = c_{pa} + H \cdot c_{pw} = 1.005 + 1.88 \cdot H \quad \text{[kJ/(kg dry air·K)]}$$

where $c_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $c_{pw} = 1.88 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.

The **enthalpy of moist air** per kg of dry air:

$$i = c_{pa}(T - T_0) + H \cdot [h_{fg,0} + c_{pw}(T - T_0)]$$

with reference at $T_0 = 0 °C$.

### 2.2 Temperature Triplets — The Holy Trinity of Drying Air

- **Dry-Bulb Temperature (DBT, $T$)**: The ordinary temperature of air measured by a normal mercury/thermocouple thermometer freely exposed to the air stream.
- **Wet-Bulb Temperature (WBT, $T_w$)**: The temperature a thermometer bulb reads when covered with a wick continuously wetted with water, with air flowing past it at > 4 m/s. The WBT is **lower than the DBT** because evaporating water consumes latent heat. The **wet-bulb depression** $T - T_w$ is a direct measure of the *drying potential* of the air.
- **Dew Point Temperature (DPT, $T_{dp}$)**: The temperature at which condensation begins if the air is cooled at constant pressure and constant humidity ratio. It is found from $H = 0.622 \cdot P_s(T_{dp})/[P_t - P_s(T_{dp})]$.

**Adiabatic Saturation Temperature ($T_{as}$)**: A theoretical construct — the temperature reached when a stream of air is contacted with water in a perfectly insulated, recirculating system until the air becomes saturated. For water–air systems, $T_{as} \approx T_w$, which is why **WBT is often read directly off the psychrometric chart to find $H$**.

### 2.3 Mechanism of Moisture Removal in Food

Drying of food involves **simultaneous heat and mass transfer**:
1. Heat flows **from** the air **to** the food surface (and interior by conduction).
2. Water (liquid/vapour) flows **from inside the food to the surface** (by capillary action, diffusion, or pressure gradients).
3. Water vapour diffuses **from the food surface into** the bulk air stream.

The **driving force** for moisture transfer is the **vapour pressure difference** $(p_{w,surface} - p_{w,air})$.

### 2.4 Drying Rate Curve — The Signature Plot

A typical drying experiment produces a plot of **drying rate $N$** (kg water removed/hr·m²) versus **moisture content $X$** (kg water/kg dry solid). The curve has four distinguishable regions:

1. **A–B: Initial transient / adjustment period** — material heats up to wet-bulb.
2. **B–C: Constant rate period (CRP)** — surface is saturated; drying rate is governed by external mass-transfer resistance (air-side). $N = $ constant $= N_c$.
3. **C–D: First falling rate period (FFRP)** — surface begins to dry; wet area recedes; rate falls.
4. **D–E: Second falling rate period (SFRP)** — internal moisture diffusion controls; rate approaches zero as $X \to X^*$.

The **critical moisture content** $X_c$ marks the transition between CRP and FRP. The **equilibrium moisture content** $X^*$ is the moisture the material will eventually reach in contact with air of given $T$ and $H$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Equation | Formula | Units | Boundary / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Humidity Ratio | $H = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_w}{P_t - P_w}$ | kg water / kg dry air | At $P_t = 101.325$ kPa |
| 2 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_w}{P_s(T)}$ | % | $P_s$ from steam tables |
| 3 | Saturation Humidity Ratio | $H_s = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_s(T)}{P_t - P_s(T)}$ | kg/kg d.a. | At dew point, $H = H_s$ |
| 4 | Humid Volume | $v_H = \dfrac{R_a T}{P_t - P_w}$ | m³/kg d.a. | $T$ in K, $P$ in kPa |
| 5 | Humid Heat | $c_H = 1.005 + 1.88 H$ | kJ/(kg d.a.·K) | At low pressure |
| 6 | Enthalpy of Moist Air | $i = 1.005(T-0) + H[2501 + 1.88(T-0)]$ | kJ/kg d.a. | Reference 0 °C |
| 7 | Specific Humidity (Humid Volume at saturation) | $v_{H,s} = \dfrac{R_a T}{P_t - P_s(T)}$ | m³/kg d.a. | Used in air-flow calc. |
| 8 | Dew-Point from RH | $P_w = \phi \cdot P_s(T_{db})$ | kPa | Then find $T_{dp}$ from $P_s$ table |
| 9 | Wet-Bulb Relation (Lewis) | $\dfrac{h}{k_y \rho} = c_s$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Lewis number relation |
| 10 | CRP Drying Time | $t_c = \dfrac{(X_1 - X_c) \cdot M_d}{N_c \cdot A}$ | h | kg dry solid basis |
| 11 | FRP Drying Time (linear) | $t_f = \dfrac{M_d \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | h | $X_c > X_2 > X^*$ |
| 12 | Constant Drying Rate | $N_c = \dfrac{h \cdot (T - T_w)}{h_{fg}}$ | kg/(h·m²) | Heat-transfer basis |
| 13 | Mass-Transfer Basis | $N_c = k_y \cdot (H_w - H)$ | kg/(h·m²) | Equivalent to #12 |
| 14 | Free Moisture | $X_f = X - X^*$ | kg/kg d.a. | Driv. force in FRP |
| 15 | Equilibrium to X\* via EMC | $X^* = f(\phi, T)$ | kg/kg d.a. | From EMC isotherm |

> [!NOTE]
> All equations #10 and #11 use the **dry basis** moisture content. Convert wet-basis $w$ to dry-basis $X$ before applying. Ensure $M_d$ = mass of **bone-dry** solid in the dryer, $A$ = exposed surface area for heat/mass transfer.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Designing **batch tray dryers** for fruits (banana, mango, apple).
- Sizing **spray dryers** for milk, coffee, whey, egg powders.
- Selecting fans and heaters for **fluidised-bed dryers** for grains.
- Predicting energy consumption of **belt and conveyor dryers** in biscuit/breakfast cereal plants.
- Estimating air requirements in **cabinet and tunnel dryers**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Humidity Ratio from Dalton's Law

Let $m_a$ = mass of dry air and $m_w$ = mass of water vapour in a sample of moist air. Using ideal-gas law for each component:

$$m_a = \frac{P_a V M_a}{R T}, \quad m_w = \frac{P_w V M_w}{R T}$$

The humidity ratio is, by definition, $H = m_w/m_a$. Substituting:

$$H = \frac{P_w V M_w / (RT)}{P_a V M_a / (RT)} = \frac{M_w}{M_a} \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_a}$$

Substituting $P_a = P_t - P_w$ (from Dalton's law) and $M_w/M_a = 18.02/28.97 = 0.622$:

$$H = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_t - P_w} \quad \text{[kg water/kg dry air]} \tag{D1}$$

This is the **workhorse** equation — used everywhere in psychrometric calculations.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Constant-Rate Drying Equation

During CRP the food surface behaves as a **free water surface** at temperature $T_w$. Energy balance on a control volume of area $A$:

**Heat transferred from air to surface** = **Latent heat required to vaporise water**

$$q = h A (T - T_w) = N_c A \cdot h_{fg}$$

Solving for the constant drying rate:

$$N_c = \frac{h \cdot (T - T_w)}{h_{fg}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h}}\right] \tag{D2}$$

By **Lewis's relation** (the heat/mass-transfer analogy for water–air systems), $h/(k_y c_s) \approx 1$, and the equivalent mass-transfer form is:

$$N_c = k_y \cdot (H_w - H) \tag{D3}$$

where $H_w$ is the saturation humidity at the wet-bulb temperature $T_w$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Drying Time in the Constant-Rate Period

Mass balance on a slice of food containing $M_d$ kg of dry solid, with moisture dropping from $X_1$ to $X_c$:

$$-\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{N_c A}{M_d}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $X_1$ at $t=0$ to $X_c$ at $t=t_c$:

$$\int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX = -\frac{N_c A}{M_d} \int_0^{t_c} dt$$

$$X_c - X_1 = -\frac{N_c A t_c}{M_d}$$

Solving for $t_c$:

$$t_c = \frac{M_d (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \cdot A} \quad \text{[hours]} \tag{D4}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Drying Time in the Falling-Rate Period (First-FRP, Linear-in-X Assumption)

Empirically, in FRP the rate $N$ is **proportional to the free moisture**:

$$N = -M_d \frac{dX}{dt} \cdot \frac{1}{A} = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

The minus sign accounts for the reduction of $X$ with time. Rearranging:

$$-\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{N_c A}{M_d} \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

Integrating from $X_c$ at $t=0$ (start of FRP) to $X_2$ at $t=t_f$:

$$\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{X - X^*} = -\frac{N_c A}{M_d(X_c - X^*)} \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

$$\left[\ln(X - X^*)\right]_{X_c}^{X_2} = -\frac{N_c A t_f}{M_d(X_c - X^*)}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*}\right) = -\frac{N_c A t_f}{M_d(X_c - X^*)}$$

Solving for $t_f$:

$$t_f = \frac{M_d (X_c - X^*)}{N_c A} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right) \quad \text{[hours]} \tag{D5}$$

### 3.5 Total Drying Time

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f \tag{D6}$$

For systems where only the FRP exists (e.g., very wet foods with no surface water), $t_c = 0$ and we use only D5 with $X_c = X_1$.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem.** A batch tray dryer is loaded with 100 kg of banana chips (bone-dry solid = 22 kg). Initial moisture $X_1 = 1.20$ kg/kg, critical moisture $X_c = 0.80$ kg/kg, equilibrium moisture $X^* = 0.05$ kg/kg, final required moisture $X_2 = 0.15$ kg/kg. Each tray offers 0.6 m² area, there are 10 trays (total $A = 6$ m²). The constant drying rate is $N_c = 1.8$ kg/(h·m²). Calculate the total drying time.

**Step 1 — Constant rate period (D4):**

$$t_c = \frac{M_d (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \cdot A} = \frac{22 \times (1.20 - 0.80)}{1.8 \times 6} = \frac{22 \times 0.40}{10.8} = \frac{8.80}{10.8} \approx 0.815 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2 — Falling rate period (D5):**

$$t_f = \frac{M_d (X_c - X^*)}{N_c A} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

Compute $X_c - X^* = 0.80 - 0.05 = 0.75$ and $X_2 - X^* = 0.15 - 0.05 = 0.10$.

$$t_f = \frac{22 \times 0.75}{1.8 \times 6} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.75}{0.10}\right) = \frac{16.5}{10.8} \times \ln(7.5) = 1.528 \times 2.015 = 3.078 \text{ h}$$

**Step 3 — Total time (D6):**

$$t_{total} = 0.815 + 3.078 \approx 3.89 \text{ h} \approx 3 \text{ h } 53 \text{ min}$$

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Periods

| Property | Constant Rate Period (CRP) | Falling Rate Period (FRP) |
|---|---|---|
| Controlling resistance | External (air film) | Internal (food matrix) |
| Surface condition | Saturated with water | Partially dry |
| Surface temperature | Equal to WBT ($T_w$) | Rises from $T_w$ to $T_{db}$ |
| Driving force for mass transfer | $(H_w - H)$ | $(X - X^*)$ |
| Driving force for heat transfer | $(T - T_w)$ | Decreasing |
| Rate-determining property | $h$, $k_y$ | $D_{eff}$ (effective diffusivity) |
| Equation | $N_c = h(T-T_w)/h_{fg}$ | $N = N_c (X-X^*)/(X_c - X^*)$ |
| Duration formula | Eq. (D4) | Eq. (D5) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Psychrometric Chart Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Input Air Stream] --> B1[DBT Tdb]
    A0 --> C1[WBT Twb]
    A0 --> D1[Pressure Pt]
    B1 --> E1[Psat from Steam Table]
    E1 --> F1[Relative Humidity phi]
    D1 --> G1[Dalton Law: Pa = Pt - Pw]
    C1 --> G1
    F1 --> H1[Pw = phi * Psat]
    H1 --> I1[Humidity Ratio H = 0.622 Pw / Pa]
    I1 --> J1[Plot Point on Psychrometric Chart]
    G1 --> K1[Humid Volume vH = Ra T / Pa]
    I1 --> L1[Humid Heat cH = 1.005 + 1.88 H]
    J1 --> M1[Enthalpy i = cpa T + H hfg]
    L1 --> M1
    M1 --> N1[Adiabatic Sat Temp Tas = Twb]
    J1 --> O1[Dew Point Tdp at constant H]
    N1 --> P1[Output: Drying Process Design]
    O1 --> P1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Drying Rate Curve Interpretation

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Initial Moisture X1] --> P2[Critical Moisture Xc]
    P2 --> P3[Final Required Moisture X2]
    P3 --> P4[Equilibrium Moisture Xstar]
    
    A1[A-B Initial Adjustment] --> A2[B-C Constant Rate Period CRP]
    A2 --> A3[C-D First Falling Rate FFRP]
    A3 --> A4[D-E Second Falling Rate SFRP]
    
    A2 --> Q1[Eq D4: tc = Md X1-Xc / Nc A]
    A3 --> Q2[Eq D5: tf = Md Xc-Xstar over Nc A times Ln]
    A4 --> Q3[Internal Diffusion Control]
    
    Q1 --> R1[Total Drying Time ttotal = tc + tf]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Moist Air Property Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Moist Air Binary System] --> PRIM[Primary Properties]
    ROOT --> DER[Derived Properties]
    ROOT --> REF[Reference Properties]
    
    PRIM --> P1[DBT Tdb]
    PRIM --> P2[WBT Twb]
    PRIM --> P3[Dew Point Tdp]
    PRIM --> P4[Total Pressure Pt]
    
    DER --> D1[Relative Humidity phi]
    DER --> D2[Absolute Humidity H]
    DER --> D3[Humid Volume vH]
    DER --> D4[Humid Heat cH]
    DER --> D5[Enthalpy i]
    DER --> D6[Adiabatic Sat Temp Tas]
    
    REF --> R1[Equilibrium Moisture Xstar]
    REF --> R2[Free Moisture X - Xstar]
    REF --> R3[Unbound Moisture]
    REF --> R4[Bound Moisture]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Air Heater and Dryer Interaction

```mermaid
graph TD
    FRESH[Fresh Ambient Air] --> FAN[Supply Fan]
    FAN --> HTR[Steam Heater or Electric Heater]
    HTR --> INLET[Heated Air Inlet State 1: Tdb1, H1]
    INLET --> DRYER[Dryer Chamber: Heat and Mass Transfer]
    DRYER --> OUTLET[Exhaust Air State 2: Tdb2, H2]
    OUTLET --> EITHER[Either Recycled to FAN or Vented]
    
    FOOD[Wet Food: X1 kg per kg] --> DRYER
    DRYER --> PROD[Final Product: X2 kg per kg]
    
    INLET --> PSY1[Psychrometric State 1]
    OUTLET --> PSY2[Psychrometric State 2]
    PSY1 --> CALC[Mass and Energy Balance]
    PSY2 --> CALC
    CALC --> AIRREQ[Calculate Air Flow Rate Required]
    CALC --> ENERGY[Calculate Heat Duty Q]
    AIRREQ --> DESIGN[Sizing of Fan Blower and Ducts]
    ENERGY --> DESIGN
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Derivation Logic Tree for Drying Time

```mermaid
graph TD
    MB[Mass Balance on Food Slice] --> EQ1[-dX per dt = N A per Md]
    EQ1 --> INT1[Integrate over CRP]
    INT1 --> OUT1[Equation D4: tc]
    
    MB2[Empirical FRP Relation] --> EQ2[N equals Nc times X-Xstar over Xc-Xstar]
    EQ2 --> INT2[Integrate over FRP]
    INT2 --> OUT2[Equation D5: tf with Ln]
    
    OUT1 --> TOT[Total Drying Time D6]
    OUT2 --> TOT
    TOT --> APPLY[Apply to Tray Fluidised or Spray Dryer]
```
