# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions mirror **actual KTU University Exam** difficulty for the **OEFTT722 — Food Process and Equipment Design** course under the **2024 Scheme**. Mark allocations strictly follow the ESE pattern: **Part A = 3 marks**, **Part B = 14 marks** (split as **7 + 7**).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a bucket elevator. List any four advantages it offers over other types of conveyors in food processing industries.**

**Model Answer:**

A bucket elevator is a continuous mechanical conveyor used for the vertical transport of bulk granular or powdered materials using discrete buckets fixed to an endless belt or chain, enclosed in a dust-tight casing.

Four advantages:
1. **Compact footprint** — delivers large vertical lifts in a small floor area.
2. **Dust-tight** — suitable for food-grade hygienic transport (no spillage, no contamination).
3. **High capacity** — can deliver 10 – 500 tonnes/hr depending on bucket size.
4. **Can handle a wide range of materials** — from light flours (ρ ≈ 450 kg/m³) to heavy grains (ρ ≈ 850 kg/m³).

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between centrifugal discharge and positive discharge bucket elevators on the basis of (i) belt speed, (ii) bucket shape, and (iii) suitability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Centrifugal Discharge | Positive Discharge |
|-------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Belt speed | High (1.0 – 2.5 m/s) | Low (0.4 – 0.8 m/s) |
| Bucket shape | Shallow, wide-mouthed | Deep, narrow |
| Suitability | Free-flowing dry grains, flour, sugar | Sticky, sluggish or wet materials |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **working principle and constructional features** of a centrifugal discharge bucket elevator with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bucket elevator is required to lift **rice** at **30 tonnes/hr** to a height of **20 m**. The buckets have a capacity of **6 litres each**, spaced at **0.4 m** apart. Belt speed is **1.6 m/s**. **Bulk density of rice** is **750 kg/m³** and **filling efficiency** is **80 %**. Calculate the **actual capacity delivered** and the **power required**, assuming **overall efficiency = 0.75**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle:**
A bucket elevator operates on the principle of **centrifugal discharge**. The bulk material is fed into the **boot section (bottom)** where the buckets scoop up the material as they round the **foot pulley**. The loaded buckets travel up the **up-leg** on one side of the casing, are carried over the **head pulley (drive end)**, and at the top the **centrifugal force** generated by the high belt speed exceeds gravity, throwing the material **tangentially out of the bucket** into a **discharge chute**. The empty buckets return down the **down-leg** to be re-filled.

**Constructional Features:**
- **Buckets** bolted to the belt at regular pitch (0.25 – 0.80 m).
- **Endless belt** of rubber/fabric with food-grade cover.
- **Head pulley** driven by motor through fluid coupling + helical gearbox.
- **Foot pulley** mounted on take-up frame with screw tensioner.
- **Casing** of mild-steel / SS-304 sheet with inspection windows and explosion-relief panels.
- **Drive assembly** with backstop to prevent reverse rotation.
- **Inlet spout** in the boot and **discharge chute** at the head.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $V_b = 6 \; \text{L}$, $a = 0.4 \; \text{m}$, $v = 1.6 \; \text{m/s}$
- $\rho = 750 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\phi = 0.80$
- $H = 20 \; \text{m}$, $\eta = 0.75$

**Step 1 — Capacity:**

$$Q = \dfrac{3.6 \times V_b \times \rho \times \phi \times v}{a} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 6 \times 750 \times 0.80 \times 1.6}{0.4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
3.6 \times 6 &= 21.6 \\
21.6 \times 750 &= 16200 \\
16200 \times 0.80 &= 12960 \\
12960 \times 1.6 &= 20736 \\
20736 \div 0.4 &= 51840 \; \text{kg/hr} \\
&= 51.84 \; \text{tonnes/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

> The actual capacity of **51.84 t/hr exceeds the demand of 30 t/hr** — the elevator is **over-designed**, which is acceptable (≈ 1.7× safety margin). If the exam demands exact 30 t/hr, bucket size must be reduced.

**Step 2 — Power (use the actual deliverable $Q$):**

$$P_{lift} = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta} = \dfrac{51.84 \times 20}{367 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{1036.8}{275.25} = 3.77 \; \text{kW}$$

Add 10 % for empty-belt losses:

$$P_{total} = 3.77 \times 1.10 \approx 4.15 \; \text{kW}$$

**Selected motor:** Standard **5.5 kW / 7.5 HP** 3-phase induction motor at 1440 rpm.

---

### 🔹 Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *(INTERNAL CHOICE)*

**(a)** Compare **centrifugal, positive, and continuous discharge bucket elevators**. State one food-industry example for each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design (i.e. determine the **bucket capacity and power**) for a bucket elevator to lift **maize** at **40 tonnes/hr** through a height of **28 m**. The **bulk density of maize** is **720 kg/m³**, **filling efficiency** is **0.85**, **belt speed = 1.5 m/s**, and **bucket pitch = 0.45 m**. Use **overall efficiency = 0.80**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table (with food examples):**

| Feature | Centrifugal Discharge | Positive Discharge | Continuous Discharge |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Discharge | Centrifugal throw | Bucket inverted by guide | Gravity overflow over lip |
| Belt speed | High (1–2.5 m/s) | Low (0.4–0.8 m/s) | Medium (0.6–1.2 m/s) |
| Bucket shape | Shallow & wide | Deep & narrow | Deep with extended lip |
| Material type | Dry, free-flowing | Sticky, sluggish | Friable, lumpy |
| Food example | Wheat / rice / flour | Wet sugar / oil cake | Malt / crumbs / flakes |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $Q = 40 \; \text{t/hr}$, $H = 28 \; \text{m}$
- $\rho = 720 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\phi = 0.85$
- $v = 1.5 \; \text{m/s}$, $a = 0.45 \; \text{m}$, $\eta = 0.80$

**Step 1 — Solve for required bucket capacity:**

Rearrange the master equation:

$$V_b = \dfrac{Q \cdot a}{3.6 \cdot \rho \cdot \phi \cdot v}$$

$$V_b = \dfrac{40 \times 0.45}{3.6 \times 720 \times 0.85 \times 1.5}$$

Compute denominator first:

$$\begin{aligned}
3.6 \times 720 &= 2592 \\
2592 \times 0.85 &= 2203.2 \\
2203.2 \times 1.5 &= 3304.8
\end{aligned}$$

Now the numerator:

$$40 \times 0.45 = 18$$

Therefore:

$$V_b = \dfrac{18}{3304.8} = 0.005446 \; \text{m}^{3} = 5.45 \; \text{litres}$$

**Standard bucket size to select:** $V_b = 5.5 \; \text{L}$ (next commercial size).

**Step 2 — Recompute actual delivered capacity** (using $V_b = 5.5$ L):

$$Q_{actual} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 5.5 \times 720 \times 0.85 \times 1.5}{0.45} = \dfrac{18206.4}{0.45 \times 1000} = 40.46 \; \text{t/hr} \; \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Power calculation:**

$$P_{lift} = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta} = \dfrac{40 \times 28}{367 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{1120}{293.6} = 3.81 \; \text{kW}$$

With 12 % allowance for $P_{empty}$:

$$P_{total} = 3.81 \times 1.12 = 4.27 \; \text{kW}$$

**Selected motor:** **5.5 kW / 7.5 HP** standard motor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — using $V_b$ in m³ instead of litres **without multiplying by 1000** (or vice versa) is the single biggest cause of numerical errors. Always show the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Plugging demand Q instead of actual Q into the power formula** — when the elevator is over-designed (Q_actual > Q_demand), use Q_actual for the power calculation; when under-designed, you must either re-specify or note that the demand is not met.
> 3. **Forgetting the efficiency term** $\eta$ — gives answers ~25–50 % too low.
> 4. **Confusing bucket pitch $a$ with bucket length** — they are different quantities.
> 5. **Using $g = 10$ instead of $g = 9.81$** — the constant $367 = 3600/g$ will then be wrong; recompute.
> 6. **Not adding $P_{empty}$** — examiners often dock 1 mark for this in 14-mark questions.
> 7. **Selecting wrong elevator type** — for food-grade dry grains, always default to **centrifugal discharge** unless specified otherwise.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bulletins for Last-Minute Recall**

- **Definition:** Bucket elevator = **endless belt + buckets + head/foot pulleys + casing**, used for **vertical transport** of **dry bulk granular food materials**.
- **Three types:** Centrifugal (fast, free-flowing), Positive (slow, sticky), Continuous (medium, friable).
- **Master Capacity Equation:** $Q = \dfrac{3.6 \cdot V_b \cdot \rho \cdot \phi \cdot v}{a}$ with units **t/hr**.
- **Master Power Equation:** $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta}$ with units **kW**.
- **The constant 367** = $3600/g = 3600/9.81$. It is **not** arbitrary.
- **Standard belt speed (centrifugal):** 1.0 – 2.5 m/s.
- **Standard bucket pitch:** 0.25 – 0.80 m.
- **Standard filling efficiency:** 0.75 (design value).
- **Always add $P_{empty}$** = 5 – 15 % of $P_{lift}$ for total installed power.
- **Select next standard motor size** (5.5 / 7.5 / 11 / 15 / 22 kW etc.).
- **Components to remember:** Head pulley, Foot pulley, Belt, Buckets, Casing, Drive motor, Gearbox, Fluid coupling, Backstop, Take-up screw, Inspection windows, Explosion-relief panels.
- **Food-industry applications:** Wheat, rice, maize, flour, sugar, salt, milk powder, spices, malt, animal feed.
- **Design sequence:** (i) Identify material → (ii) Pick elevator type → (iii) Estimate capacity → (iv) Choose bucket size & pitch → (v) Compute belt speed → (vi) Compute power → (vii) Select motor.
- **Centrifugal discharge works because** $F_c = \dfrac{m \cdot v^{2}}{r} > mg \Rightarrow v > \sqrt{g \cdot r}$.
- **Don't forget unit conversion** of $V_b$: **1 litre = $10^{-3}$ m³**.
- **For food-grade elevators:** use **SS-304 / SS-316 buckets**, **food-grade PVC/rubber belt**, **food-grade lubricants** only.
- **Always include explosion relief** in flour/sugar elevators (dust explosion risk per IS 962 / NFPA 61).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screw Conveyor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT722 - FOOD PROCESS AND EQUIPMENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Screw Conveyor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Screw Conveyor** (also called an **Auger Conveyor**) is a mechanical conveying device that uses a rotating helical screw blade, known as **flighting**, wound around a central shaft or fixed to a shaftless helix, contained within a **U-shaped trough** or **tubular casing**, to transport bulk particulate, semi-fluid, or granular food materials from one location to another through the principle of **positive displacement**.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme - OEFTT722 (Food Process and Equipment Design)**, the screw conveyor is formally classified under **Mechanical Handling and Conveying Equipment for Particulate Solids**, and its design involves the integration of **mass flow capacity**, **rotational kinematics**, **material rheology**, and **power transmission**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The design of a screw conveyor is a compulsory component of the Food Process Equipment Design course. Students are expected to derive capacity equations, calculate power consumption for horizontal and inclined conveyors, and perform a numerical design problem (typically 14 marks) in the End Semester Examination.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Flighting** | The continuous helical blade that moves the material. |
| **Pitch (S)** | The axial distance between two consecutive flight crests. |
| **Trough** | The stationary casing (U-type or tubular) that contains the material. |
| **Loading Coefficient (Ψ)** | The fraction of the cross-sectional area filled with material. |
| **Shaft | A central rotating rod that supports the flights. |
| **Rotor | The combined rotating assembly (shaft + flights). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Design Rule:** For most food-grade materials, the pitch of the flighting is taken as **S = 0.5 D to 1.0 D**, where **D** is the nominal screw diameter. The standard ratio is typically **S = 0.5 D** for efficient transport and adequate material agitation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing in your kitchen with a long plastic tube and a handful of rice grains. If you push the rice in one end and tilt the tube slightly, the grains slide through. Now, suppose you take a rotating drill bit and push it into a bowl of wheat flour — the bit pulls the flour up and out along its spiral grooves. That is essentially a **screw conveyor** in action.

The helical blade acts like a **spiral staircase inside a tube**: as the screw rotates, each turn of the flighting scoops a small volume of material and pushes it forward by one pitch length. The material does not need to be pushed externally; the rotation itself creates a forward **axial velocity component** through a combination of **drag-induced shear** and **centrifugal compaction**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy for KTU Viva:** When asked to explain a screw conveyor, answer: *"It is an Archimedes' screw. When immersed in water, the spiral turns lift water uphill. Replace the water with wheat, flour, sugar, or corn grits, and you have a food industry screw conveyor."*

In the food industry, screw conveyors are extensively used for:
- **Wheat and flour transfer** in roller mills and bakery plants.
- **Sugar conveying** in sugar refineries.
- **Spice and condiment transport** in processing lines.
- **Grain unloading** in silos and storage hoppers.
- **Meat and bone meal** transfer in rendering plants.
- **Wet sludge pumping** in dairy effluent treatment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constants to Memorize:**
> - Standard gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Standard motor efficiency: **η = 0.85 to 0.95**
> - Typical bulk densities: Wheat = **750 kg/m³**, Sugar = **800 kg/m³**, Rice = **720 kg/m³**, Maize = **700 kg/m³**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The screw conveyor operates on the principle of **continuous positive displacement**. The helical blade, when rotated, traps a fixed volume of material between successive flights and the trough wall. As the screw rotates, this trapped volume is translated axially along the conveyor length.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Material Movement:

1. **Material Loading:** Bulk material enters the trough at the inlet hopper and falls into the cavities formed between consecutive flights of the rotating screw.
2. **Radial Compaction:** Centrifugal force throws the material outward, pressing it against the trough wall. This creates a self-sealing "rubber" effect that minimizes backflow.
3. **Axial Translation:** Because the flighting is angled (helix angle α, typically 15°–25°), the rotation of the screw creates a tangential velocity **V_t** at the trough wall. This velocity is resolved into:
    - **Axial component:** $V_a = V_t \sin\alpha$ — pushes material forward.
    - **Tangential component:** $V_t \cos\alpha$ — produces friction drag that further compacts the material.
4. **Material Discharge:** At the outlet end, the material is discharged by centrifugal throw, gravity, or by an end bearing opening.

### The "Why" Behind the Design

- **Why a helix?** A helix is the only shape that converts rotary motion into linear motion with zero reciprocating parts.
- **Why U-shaped trough?** It is the cheapest, easiest to fabricate, and allows for visual inspection of the material.
- **Why limit the speed?** Beyond a critical speed, the material becomes pinned against the trough wall by centrifugal force and stops moving axially. This defines the **Critical Speed** $N_c$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **master cheat sheet** for any screw conveyor design problem. Every value, every unit, must be memorized for the KTU 2024 examination.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Volume Capacity** | $Q_v = 0.785 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \Psi \cdot 60$ | m³/h |
| 2 | **Mass Capacity** | $Q_m = Q_v \cdot \rho_b$ | tons/h |
| 3 | **Cross-sectional area of trough** | $A = 0.785 \cdot D^2$ | m² |
| 4 | **Critical Speed (limit)** | $N_c = \dfrac{A}{\sqrt{D}}$ with A = 50 to 75 (metric) | rpm |
| 5 | **Recommended Speed** | $N = 0.5 \cdot N_c$ to $0.85 \cdot N_c$ | rpm |
| 6 | **Power for Horizontal Transport** | $P_h = \dfrac{Q_m \cdot L \cdot \mu_g \cdot g}{367 \cdot \eta}$ | kW |
| 7 | **Power for Inclined (Additional)** | $P_v = \dfrac{Q_m \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta}$ | kW |
| 8 | **Total Power** | $P_{tot} = P_h + P_v$ | kW |
| 9 | **Motor Power (with safety factor)** | $P_m = 1.2 \cdot P_{tot}$ | kW |
| 10 | **Torque at the Shaft** | $T = \dfrac{9550 \cdot P_{tot}}{N}$ | N·m |
| 11 | **Pitch-to-Diameter Ratio** | $S = 0.5 \cdot D$ to $1.0 \cdot D$ | m |
| 12 | **Loading Coefficient (Ψ)** | $\Psi = 0.3$ to $0.45$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | **Helix Angle** | $\alpha = \arctan\left(\dfrac{S}{\pi D}\right)$ | degrees |
| 14 | **Inclination Factor (Capacity Reduction)** | $C_i = 1.0 - 0.015 \cdot \beta$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Speed Law:** If the screw rotates faster than the critical speed, the material simply revolves with the screw and ceases to move forward. The **maximum allowable speed is approximately 50% to 85% of the critical speed**, depending on the material's abrasive and cohesive nature.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **rice mill in Kerala**, a screw conveyor transports 5 tons/h of brown rice over a horizontal distance of 12 m, then lifts it vertically by 4 m into a storage silo. The designer must ensure:
- The capacity $Q_m$ is at least 5 tons/h.
- The trough diameter $D$ is large enough to prevent choking.
- The power $P$ accounts for friction losses (horizontal) **and** gravity lift (vertical).
- The motor HP is over-rated by ~20% to handle start-up surges.

This exact problem appears in the KTU End Semester Examination almost every academic year.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Capacity Equation

We want to find how much material (by mass or volume) flows through a screw conveyor per unit time.

### Step 1 — Define the Volumetric Sweep per Revolution

When the screw rotates once, every point on the flighting moves axially by exactly one pitch length **S**. The space between two consecutive flights is the **trough cross-section**, which is approximately circular with diameter **D**.

The area of this cross-section is:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = 0.785 \cdot D^2$$

> *The factor 0.785 (≈ π/4) represents the area of a circle. It is a standard engineering constant for circular cross-sections.*

### Step 2 — Volume Displaced per Revolution

In one complete rotation, the screw advances by one pitch length **S**, sweeping out a volume equal to:

$$V_{1} = A \cdot S = 0.785 \cdot D^2 \cdot S$$

> *The pitch S is the "stride length" of the screw per revolution, like a person walking.*

### Step 3 — Account for the Loading Coefficient

In practice, the trough is never 100% full. The **loading coefficient** $\Psi$ represents the fraction of the trough filled with material. For food materials, $\Psi$ is typically between **0.3 and 0.45**.

The actual volume conveyed per revolution is:

$$V_{2} = \Psi \cdot V_{1} = 0.785 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot \Psi$$

> *Ψ is dimensionless (no units). It is a calibration factor based on the bulk material's angle of repose and flowability.*

### Step 4 — Scale to Rotational Speed

If the screw rotates at **N revolutions per minute**, the volume conveyed per minute is:

$$V_{min} = V_{2} \cdot N = 0.785 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \Psi$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Per-Hour Basis

Multiplying by 60 minutes per hour gives the standard capacity in m³/h:

$$Q_v = 0.785 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \Psi \cdot 60 \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/h)}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Mass Flow Rate

Multiplying by the **bulk density** $\rho_b$ (in tons/m³) gives the mass flow rate:

$$Q_m = Q_v \cdot \rho_b = 0.785 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \Psi \cdot 60 \cdot \rho_b \quad \text{(tons/h)}$$

> **Final Result (Boxed):**
> $$\boxed{Q_m = 47.1 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \Psi \cdot \rho_b \quad \text{(tons/h)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Power Equation

The total power required by a screw conveyor has two components: **horizontal friction power** and **vertical lift power**.

### Step 3.2.1 — Horizontal Transport Power

When material is pushed horizontally, it experiences a frictional resistance against the trough. The force resisting motion is:

$$F_h = Q_m \cdot \mu_g \cdot g$$

where $\mu_g$ is the coefficient of friction and $g = 9.81$ m/s² is the gravitational acceleration.

The work done per unit time (power) is force times velocity. Since the material travels a length **L** in the total operational time, and the mass flow rate is $Q_m$ tons/h, the total frictional power is:

$$P_h = \frac{Q_m \cdot L \cdot \mu_g \cdot g}{367 \cdot \eta}$$

> **Derivation of the constant 367:**
> - Power in watts: $P = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta}$ where $F = Q_m \cdot \mu_g \cdot g$ and $v = \dfrac{L}{t}$.
> - $Q_m$ in tons/h: $1$ ton = $1000$ kg, and $1$ h = $3600$ s.
> - So $Q_m$ tons/h $= \dfrac{Q_m \cdot 1000}{3600}$ kg/s.
> - Substituting: $P_h = \dfrac{Q_m \cdot 1000 \cdot \mu_g \cdot 9.81}{3600 \cdot \eta} = \dfrac{Q_m \cdot \mu_g \cdot 9.81}{3.6 \cdot \eta}$.
> - Converting to **kW** (dividing by 1000): $P_h = \dfrac{Q_m \cdot \mu_g \cdot 9.81}{3600 \cdot \eta}$.
> - Using $g = 9.81$ m/s²: $9.81 / 3600 = 0.002725$. So $P_h = 0.002725 \cdot Q_m \cdot \mu_g \cdot L / \eta$.
> - Inverse of $0.002725 \approx 367$. Hence the empirical constant **367** appears.

### Step 3.2.2 — Vertical Lift Power

If the conveyor is inclined or vertical, an additional power is required to lift the material against gravity through a height **H**:

$$P_v = \frac{Q_m \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta}$$

> *Note: For purely horizontal conveyors, H = 0 and $P_v = 0$.*

### Step 3.2.3 — Total Power

$$P_{tot} = P_h + P_v = \frac{Q_m}{367 \cdot \eta} \left( L \cdot \mu_g \cdot g + H \right)$$

### Step 3.2.4 — Motor Power (Including Safety Factor)

Motors are always over-rated by 15–25% to handle start-up loads and stalling:

$$P_{motor} = (1.15 \text{ to } 1.25) \cdot P_{tot}$$

## 3.3 KTU Examiner's Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical

> **Worked Example (14-mark KTU standard):**
> Design a screw conveyor to transport **8 tons/h of wheat** over a horizontal length of **20 m** with a vertical lift of **5 m**. Wheat bulk density = **750 kg/m³**, coefficient of friction $\mu_g$ = **0.4**, drive efficiency $\eta$ = **0.85**, pitch-to-diameter ratio S/D = **0.8**, loading coefficient $\Psi$ = **0.4**, critical speed constant A = **60**.

### Solution Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Calculate the trough diameter D:**

Assume a nominal screw diameter. The problem implies an iterative design. Let us start with $D = 0.30$ m (300 mm) and verify the critical speed.

**Step 2 — Critical Speed Calculation:**

$$N_c = \frac{A}{\sqrt{D}} = \frac{60}{\sqrt{0.30}} = \frac{60}{0.5477} = 109.55 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Recommended Speed:**

Taking 70% of critical speed:

$$N = 0.7 \cdot N_c = 0.7 \cdot 109.55 = 76.7 \text{ rpm}$$

Let us round to **N = 75 rpm** for design simplicity.

**Step 4 — Verify Capacity with N = 75 rpm:**

Given $S = 0.8 \cdot D = 0.8 \cdot 0.30 = 0.24$ m, $\rho_b = 0.75$ tons/m³ (since 750 kg/m³):

$$Q_m = 47.1 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \Psi \cdot \rho_b$$

$$Q_m = 47.1 \cdot (0.30)^2 \cdot 0.24 \cdot 75 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.75$$

Calculating each factor:
- $D^2 = 0.09$
- $0.30^2 = 0.09$
- $47.1 \cdot 0.09 = 4.239$
- $4.239 \cdot 0.24 = 1.0174$
- $1.0174 \cdot 75 = 76.30$
- $76.30 \cdot 0.4 = 30.52$
- $30.52 \cdot 0.75 = 22.89$ tons/h

> **Result:** $Q_m = 22.89$ tons/h, which is well above the required 8 tons/h. The diameter is over-sized. We could downsize to $D = 0.20$ m, but for this KTU solution, we retain $D = 0.30$ m as a conservative design.

**Step 5 — Horizontal Transport Power:**

$$P_h = \frac{Q_m \cdot L \cdot \mu_g \cdot g}{367 \cdot \eta} = \frac{8 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 9.81}{367 \cdot 0.85}$$

Numerator: $8 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 9.81 = 627.84$

Denominator: $367 \cdot 0.85 = 311.95$

$$P_h = \frac{627.84}{311.95} = 2.01 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Vertical Lift Power:**

$$P_v = \frac{Q_m \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta} = \frac{8 \cdot 5}{367 \cdot 0.85} = \frac{40}{311.95} = 0.128 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Total Power:**

$$P_{tot} = P_h + P_v = 2.01 + 0.128 = 2.138 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Motor Power (with 20% safety):**

$$P_{motor} = 1.20 \cdot 2.138 = 2.566 \text{ kW}$$

We select the next standard motor size: **3.0 kW (4 HP)**.

**Step 9 — Torque on the Shaft:**

$$T = \frac{9550 \cdot P_{tot}}{N} = \frac{9550 \cdot 2.138}{75} = \frac{20417.9}{75} = 272.24 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Final Design Summary (Boxed):**
> $$\boxed{D = 0.30 \text{ m}, \quad S = 0.24 \text{ m}, \quad N = 75 \text{ rpm}, \quad P_{motor} = 3.0 \text{ kW}, \quad T = 272.24 \text{ N·m}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the **functional interactions** of a screw conveyor system from energy input to material output. It avoids physical drawing complexity and is fully compliant with Mermaid syntax rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source 415V 3Phase AC Supply] --> B[Electric Motor 3 kW 4 HP]
    B --> C[Gear Reducer Helical Worm Type Ratio 20 to 1]
    C --> D[Coupling Flexible Spider Type]
    D --> E[Drive Shaft EN8 Steel D equals 0.05 m]
    E --> F[End Thrust Bearing SKF 22209]
    F --> G[Flighted Screw Rotor D equals 0.30 m S equals 0.24 m]
    G --> H[Trough U Section SS304 Food Grade]
    G --> I[Inlet Hopper with Slide Gate]
    H --> J[Outlet Discharge Chute]
    G --> K[Tail End Bearing Plummer Block]
    G --> L[Material Stream Wheat 8 tons per hour]
    L --> M[Axial Forward Velocity Va]
    H --> N[Trough Friction Force mu times N]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#F38181,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#AA96DA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#3D5A80,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#E0FBFC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#E0FBFC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#E0FBFC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#AA96DA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#F4A261,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M fill:#2A9D8F,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style N fill:#264653,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram of Material Movement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Material Loading] --> P2[Step 2 Radial Compaction by Centrifugal Force]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Axial Translation by Helix Angle alpha]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Friction Drag at Trough Wall]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Discharge at Outlet]

    style P1 fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style P5 fill:#F7B801,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Power Transmission Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PT1[Electrical Energy Input] --> PT2[Electromagnetic Conversion in Motor]
    PT2 --> PT3[Mechanical Energy at High RPM 1440 rpm]
    PT3 --> PT4[Speed Reduction by Gearbox to 75 rpm]
    PT4 --> PT5[Transmission to Coupling]
    PT5 --> PT6[Drive to Screw Rotor]
    PT6 --> PT7[Useful Power Horizontal Transport]
    PT6 --> PT8[Useful Power Vertical Lift]
    PT6 --> PT9[Power Loss due to Friction]
    PT6 --> PT10[Power Loss due to Bearing Friction]

    style PT1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PT7 fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PT8 fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PT9 fill:#EF476F,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style PT10 fill:#EF476F,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU:** The block diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of a screw conveyor as required by Module 4 of the syllabus. In the exam, you can sketch a similar flowchart to score full marks on the **block diagram question (typically 2–3 marks within a 14-mark question).**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> **Q1. Define a screw conveyor. List any four applications of screw conveyors in food processing industries.**

**Model Answer:**
A screw conveyor is a mechanical device that uses a rotating helical blade (called flighting) inside a U-shaped or tubular trough to transport bulk food materials from one location to another through the principle of positive displacement.

**Four applications:**
1. Conveying wheat and flour in roller mills.
2. Transporting sugar granules in sugar refineries.
3. Transferring spices and condiments in processing lines.
4. Unloading grains from silos and storage hoppers.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. What is the significance of the critical speed of a screw conveyor?**

**Model Answer:**
The critical speed of a screw conveyor is the rotational speed beyond which the material gets pinned against the trough wall by centrifugal force and ceases to move axially forward. It depends on the screw diameter and the material's characteristics. The actual operating speed is maintained at **50% to 85% of the critical speed** to ensure efficient material flow without choking. For a screw of diameter D, the critical speed is given by:

$$N_c = \frac{A}{\sqrt{D}}$$

where A is a constant (50–75 for food materials).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, End Semester Examination, Module 4]**
> **Q3A.** Design a screw conveyor to handle **10 tons/h of rice** over a horizontal distance of **15 m** with a vertical lift of **6 m**. Assume the following data:
> - Bulk density of rice: $\rho_b$ = **720 kg/m³**
> - Coefficient of friction: $\mu_g$ = **0.45**
> - Drive efficiency: $\eta$ = **0.88**
> - Loading coefficient: $\Psi$ = **0.4**
> - Pitch-to-diameter ratio: S/D = **0.75**
> - Critical speed constant: A = **55**
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The screw diameter and recommended speed.
> (b) The motor power required and torque on the shaft.

### Part (a) — Screw Diameter and Speed (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Assume a trial diameter D = 0.25 m:**

**Step 2 — Calculate Critical Speed:**

$$N_c = \frac{A}{\sqrt{D}} = \frac{55}{\sqrt{0.25}} = \frac{55}{0.5} = 110 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Recommended Operating Speed (75% of critical):**

$$N = 0.75 \cdot 110 = 82.5 \text{ rpm} \approx 80 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Pitch:**

$$S = 0.75 \cdot D = 0.75 \cdot 0.25 = 0.1875 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the Capacity with D = 0.25 m:**

Using the mass capacity formula:

$$Q_m = 47.1 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \Psi \cdot \rho_b$$

$$Q_m = 47.1 \cdot (0.25)^2 \cdot 0.1875 \cdot 80 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.72$$

Step-by-step multiplication:
- $D^2 = 0.0625$
- $47.1 \cdot 0.0625 = 2.944$
- $2.944 \cdot 0.1875 = 0.552$
- $0.552 \cdot 80 = 44.16$
- $44.16 \cdot 0.4 = 17.66$
- $17.66 \cdot 0.72 = 12.72$ tons/h

> **Capacity obtained = 12.72 tons/h, which is greater than the required 10 tons/h. The design is acceptable.**

### Part (b) — Motor Power and Torque (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Horizontal Power:**

$$P_h = \frac{Q_m \cdot L \cdot \mu_g \cdot g}{367 \cdot \eta} = \frac{10 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 9.81}{367 \cdot 0.88}$$

- Numerator: $10 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 9.81 = 662.18$
- Denominator: $367 \cdot 0.88 = 322.96$
- $P_h = 662.18 / 322.96 = 2.05$ kW

**Step 2 — Vertical Lift Power:**

$$P_v = \frac{Q_m \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta} = \frac{10 \cdot 6}{367 \cdot 0.88} = \frac{60}{322.96} = 0.186 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Total Power:**

$$P_{tot} = 2.05 + 0.186 = 2.236 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Motor Power (with 20% safety factor):**

$$P_{motor} = 1.20 \cdot 2.236 = 2.68 \text{ kW}$$

Select next standard motor: **3.7 kW (5 HP)**.

**Step 5 — Torque on the Shaft:**

$$T = \frac{9550 \cdot P_{tot}}{N} = \frac{9550 \cdot 2.236}{80} = \frac{21353.8}{80} = 266.92 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Final Answer Summary (Boxed):**
> $$\boxed{D = 0.25 \text{ m}, \quad S = 0.1875 \text{ m}, \quad N = 80 \text{ rpm}, \quad P_{motor} = 3.7 \text{ kW}, \quad T = 266.92 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, End Semester Examination, Module 4]**
> **Q3B.** A screw conveyor is required to transport **6 tons/h of wheat flour** over a horizontal length of **25 m** with a vertical lift of **4 m**. Given data:
> - Bulk density of wheat flour: $\rho_b$ = **600 kg/m³**
> - Coefficient of friction: $\mu_g$ = **0.5**
> - Drive efficiency: $\eta$ = **0.85**
> - Loading coefficient: $\Psi$ = **0.35**
> - Pitch-to-diameter ratio: S/D = **0.8**
> - Critical speed constant: A = **60**
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The screw diameter, pitch, and recommended speed.
> (b) The total power required, motor rating, and torque.

### Part (a) — Diameter, Pitch, and Speed (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Trial diameter D = 0.20 m:**

**Step 2 — Critical Speed:**

$$N_c = \frac{60}{\sqrt{0.20}} = \frac{60}{0.4472} = 134.16 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Recommended Speed (80% of critical):**

$$N = 0.8 \cdot 134.16 = 107.33 \text{ rpm}$$

Round to **N = 105 rpm** for design.

**Step 4 — Pitch:**

$$S = 0.8 \cdot 0.20 = 0.16 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Capacity:**

$$Q_m = 47.1 \cdot (0.20)^2 \cdot 0.16 \cdot 105 \cdot 0.35 \cdot 0.60$$

- $D^2 = 0.04$
- $47.1 \cdot 0.04 = 1.884$
- $1.884 \cdot 0.16 = 0.3014$
- $0.3014 \cdot 105 = 31.65$
- $31.65 \cdot 0.35 = 11.08$
- $11.08 \cdot 0.60 = 6.65$ tons/h

> **Capacity = 6.65 tons/h, which is greater than required 6 tons/h. The design is valid.**

### Part (b) — Total Power, Motor, and Torque (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Horizontal Power:**

$$P_h = \frac{6 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9.81}{367 \cdot 0.85} = \frac{735.75}{311.95} = 2.36 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Vertical Power:**

$$P_v = \frac{6 \cdot 4}{367 \cdot 0.85} = \frac{24}{311.95} = 0.077 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Total Power:**

$$P_{tot} = 2.36 + 0.077 = 2.437 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Motor Power (with 20% safety):**

$$P_{motor} = 1.20 \cdot 2.437 = 2.92 \text{ kW}$$

Select next standard motor: **3.7 kW (5 HP)**.

**Step 5 — Torque:**

$$T = \frac{9550 \cdot 2.437}{105} = \frac{23273.35}{105} = 221.65 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Final Answer Summary (Boxed):**
> $$\boxed{D = 0.20 \text{ m}, \quad S = 0.16 \text{ m}, \quad N = 105 \text{ rpm}, \quad P_{motor} = 3.7 \text{ kW}, \quad T = 221.65 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Many students write $Q_m$ in tons/h but plug it into power equations as if it were in kg/s. **Always use tons/h consistently.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Safety Factor:** The motor power is the total power multiplied by 1.15–1.25, not the raw total power. Many students lose 1 mark by forgetting this.
> 3. **Wrong Sign on Vertical Lift:** For horizontal conveyors (H = 0), $P_v$ is zero, not negative. Do not subtract the vertical power.
> 4. **Loading Coefficient Misuse:** $\Psi$ is multiplied with the **volumetric** calculation, not directly with mass. The density is applied at the end.
> 5. **Critical Speed Violation:** Never exceed 90% of the critical speed. Operating above 90% is unsafe and will cause material grinding and overheating.
> 6. **Forgetting the Block Diagram:** In 14-mark design problems, KTU often allocates 1–2 marks for a labeled block diagram. **Always include it.**
> 7. **Pitch Calculation:** The pitch is **S = 0.5 D to 1.0 D**. Anything outside this range is a design error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Screw Conveyor = Helical Flighting + Trough + Drive System.**
- **Capacity Formula:** $Q_m = 47.1 \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \Psi \cdot \rho_b$ **(tons/h)**.
- **Critical Speed:** $N_c = A / \sqrt{D}$, with A = 50–75 for food materials.
- **Operating Speed:** 50–85% of $N_c$.
- **Pitch-to-Diameter Ratio:** $S = 0.5 \cdot D$ to $1.0 \cdot D$.
- **Loading Coefficient $\Psi$:** 0.3 to 0.45 for food materials.
- **Horizontal Power:** $P_h = Q_m \cdot L \cdot \mu_g \cdot g / (367 \cdot \eta)$.
- **Vertical Power:** $P_v = Q_m \cdot H / (367 \cdot \eta)$.
- **Total Power:** $P_{tot} = P_h + P_v$.
- **Motor Rating:** $P_{motor} = 1.2 \cdot P_{tot}$ (with 20% safety factor).
- **Torque:** $T = 9550 \cdot P_{tot} / N$.
- **Constant 367:** Comes from the unit conversion of $g$ and $Q_m$ from tons/h to kg/s.
- **Constant 47.1:** Comes from $0.785 \cdot 60$ (area factor × minutes to hours).
- **Constant 9550:** Comes from $60 \cdot 1000 / (2\pi)$ for converting power (kW) and speed (rpm) to torque (N·m).
- **Inclination Factor:** $C_i = 1 - 0.015 \cdot \beta$ where $\beta$ is the angle of inclination in degrees.
- **Material Limitations:** Avoid screw conveyors for **stringy, fibrous, sticky, or highly abrasive** food materials (use belt conveyors instead).
- **Block Diagram is Mandatory:** Always draw a labeled block diagram in design problems.
- **Final Answer Format:** Always box the final values of D, S, N, $P_{motor}$, and T in a single summary box.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chain Conveyor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT722 - FOOD PROCESS AND EQUIPMENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chain Conveyor

# 🔗 CHAIN CONVEYOR — KTU 2024 Module 4 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Chain Conveyor** is a material-handling device in which one or more endless chains, running over terminal and intermediate sprockets, are used as the pulling or pushing element to transport bulk food materials (grains, flours, granules, meals, slurries) in a continuous, en-masse, or flighted flow along a trough, casing, or pan. The chain acts as the tension member while attached **flights, paddles, slats, or the body of material itself** acts as the carrying medium.

The chain conveyor is officially grouped under **mechanical conveyors** in the KTU Food Process Equipment Design syllabus (Module 4) and is preferred in the food industry for **abrasive, dusty, hot, or hygroscopic bulk solids** where belt conveyors would wear out rapidly.

### 1.2 Sub-Taxonomy Recognised by KTU

| Type | Carrying Action | Typical Food Application |
|------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **En-Masse Conveyor** | Material moves as a compact slug filling the casing | Grain elevators, flour, malt |
| **Drag (Flight) Conveyor** | Flight bars scrape material along trough | Sugar, salt, starch |
| **Paddle Conveyor** | Paddles push material in semi-circular trough | Wet meals, slurries |
| **Apron / Slat Conveyor** | Material rides on metal pans between chains | Bags, boxes, packaged foods |
| **Redler Conveyor** (a sub-type of en-masse) | Mass-flow inside a closed casing | Hygroscopic powders |

> [!NOTE] The **En-Masse conveyor** is the most heavily tested variant in KTU ESE questions because it allows both capacity and power derivations in a single closed-form problem.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Moving Escalator Inside a Pipe"

Imagine a **box of cereal being carried up a sealed escalator** that is enclosed in a square tube. Instead of steps, the cereal sits on a **chain of paddle-like scoops** that drag the entire column forward as one solid mass. The cereal at the back pushes the cereal in the front — that's the essence of *en-masse* movement.

- The **chain** = the escalator's pulling chain
- The **cereal column** = the material slug
- The **square tube (casing)** = the trough/casing
- The **sprockets at both ends** = the top and bottom escalator wheels

This analogy is useful because it directly justifies why we treat the moving material as a **continuous body of mass** during power calculations, rather than as discrete particles.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Constants (Bold for Memory)

- Chain speed **v** for food-grade en-masse conveyor: **0.10 to 0.50 m/s**
- Fill factor (φ): **0.65 – 0.85** (typical design value **0.75**)
- Standard chain pitches: **63 mm, 80 mm, 100 mm, 125 mm, 160 mm, 200 mm**
- Factor of safety on chain breaking load: **6 – 10** (food industry = 8)
- Coefficient of friction **f** (chain on steel trough): **0.30 – 0.50**
- Drive efficiency η: **0.80 – 0.90**

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU examiners expect students to use the **design value of fill factor φ = 0.75** unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. Using an arbitrary value (e.g., 0.50) will lose a half-mark even if the rest of the calculation is correct.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The chain conveyor works on the principle of **positive mechanical displacement**:

1. **Drive Sprocket (Head End):** An electric motor, through a geared reducer, rotates the drive sprocket at a controlled RPM.
2. **Chain Engagement:** The chain, with flights/paddles attached, wraps around the drive sprocket and a tail sprocket separated by length **L**.
3. **Material Loading:** Bulk food is fed through an inlet chute at the tail (boot) end; gravity assists the initial loading.
4. **En-Masse Transport:** The flights drag the entire column of material forward as a single slug. No relative motion exists between adjacent particles — the whole mass behaves like a moving piston.
5. **Discharge:** At the head end, the chain turns over the drive sprocket, and the material is discharged by centrifugal action (or by a discharge chute for sticky foods).
6. **Return Path:** The empty chain returns below (or beside) the carrying casing, completing the loop.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Parameter

| Parameter | Why it Matters | Typical Range |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| **Chain Speed (v)** | Higher speed → more capacity, but more wear, dust, breakage of friable foods (chips, flakes) | 0.1 – 0.5 m/s |
| **Casing Cross-Section (A)** | Sets the volumetric capacity; determined by chain pitch | 0.01 – 0.30 m² |
| **Chain Pitch (p)** | Larger pitch = larger flights, but slower permissible speed | 63 – 200 mm |
| **Number of Chains** | Single for light loads; double for wide cross-section or heavy loads | 1 or 2 |
| **Sprocket Teeth (Z)** | Must be ≥ 7 to avoid chain jump; preferably ≥ 9 | 7 – 13 |
| **Take-Up Travel** | Compensates for chain elongation due to wear | 1.5 – 3 % of C.L. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT] Memorise this table — at least 3 of the 5 formulas appear in every KTU ESE question on chain conveyors.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Mass Capacity** | $Q_m = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot \phi$ | kg/hr | A in m², v in m/s, ρ in kg/m³, φ dimensionless |
| 2 | **Volumetric Capacity** | $Q_v = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \phi$ | m³/hr |  |
| 3 | **Power — Horizontal** | $P_h = \dfrac{Q \cdot L \cdot f}{367}$ | kW | Q in t/hr, L in m, f dimensionless |
| 4 | **Power — Inclined** | $P_i = \dfrac{Q \cdot L_h \cdot f}{367} + \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{367}$ | kW | H = vertical lift in m, L_h = horiz. projection in m |
| 5 | **Tractive Force** | $F_t = \dfrac{1000 \cdot P}{v}$ | N | P in kW, v in m/s |
| 6 | **Chain Breaking Load** | $F_b = F_o \times \text{FoS}$ | kN | FoS = 6–10 (food = 8) |
| 7 | **Maximum Working Load** | $F_o = \dfrac{F_t}{n_{strands}}$ | N | Per strand |
| 8 | **Sprocket Speed** | $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot v}{p \cdot Z}$ | rpm | p = pitch, Z = no. of teeth |
| 9 | **Chain Length** | $L_c = 2L + \dfrac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4L}$ | m | For two-sprocket system |
| 10 | **Cross-Section (en-masse)** | $A = W \times H$ where $H = 2p$ to $3p$ | m² | p = chain pitch |

> [!WARNING] The constant **367** in the power formula comes from the conversion $1000 \cdot 9.81 / (3600 / 3.6) = 1000 \cdot g \cdot 3.6 / 1000 \approx 9.81 \times 1000 / 9.81 = ...$. **It is not a typo** — write it as **367** in your answer booklet.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Flour Mills:** En-masse conveyors lift wheat and finished flour vertically with minimum dust.
- **Sugar Industry:** Drag conveyors move crystalline sugar without crystal breakage.
- **Brewing/Malting:** Horizontal en-masse conveyors transfer malt across the entire length of a kiln floor.
- **Dairy & Milk Powder Plants:** Hygroscopic powders (WMP, SMP) are moved in **totally enclosed** redler conveyors to prevent moisture pickup.
- **Rice & Pulse Processing:** Used for gentle handling of finished grains where breakage must be minimised.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Capacity Equation

Consider a chain conveyor with casing cross-sectional area **A** (m²) moving at chain speed **v** (m/s).

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow per second**

In one second, the chain advances a distance of **v** metres. The material column in front of the chain (cross-section A, fill factor φ) also advances by v metres.

$$Q_{vol,\,per\,sec} = A \cdot v \cdot \phi \quad \text{[m}^3/\text{s]}$$

**Step 2 — Convert seconds to hours**

$$1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ s}$$

$$Q_v = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \phi \quad \text{[m}^3/\text{hr]}$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to mass**

$$Q_m = Q_v \cdot \rho = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot \phi \quad \text{[kg/hr]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert kg/hr to tons/hr (1 ton = 1000 kg)**

$$Q = \dfrac{Q_m}{1000} = 3.6 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot \phi \quad \text{[t/hr]}$$

> This is the **canonical KTU capacity formula**. **Do not confuse** the constant 3.6 with 3600 — they are interchanged only when the units shift between kg/hr and t/hr.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Horizontal Power Equation

**Step 1 — Mass of material in motion**

The mass of material continuously carried on the conveyor (between head and tail sprockets) is:

$$m = \dfrac{Q_m \cdot L}{3600 \cdot v} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot Q \cdot L}{3600 \cdot v} \quad \text{[kg]}$$

where **L** is the conveyor centre-to-centre length in metres and **Q** is in t/hr.

**Step 2 — Weight of moving material**

$$W = m \cdot g = \dfrac{1000 \cdot Q \cdot L \cdot g}{3600 \cdot v} \quad \text{[N]}$$

**Step 3 — Frictional resistance at the trough**

The chain drags the material against the casing surface. The total frictional resistance is:

$$F_f = f \cdot W = \dfrac{1000 \cdot Q \cdot L \cdot g \cdot f}{3600 \cdot v} \quad \text{[N]}$$

where **f** = coefficient of friction between material and trough.

**Step 4 — Power at the chain (shaft power)**

$$P = F_f \cdot v = \dfrac{1000 \cdot Q \cdot L \cdot g \cdot f}{3600} \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute g = 9.81 m/s²**

$$P = \dfrac{1000 \cdot Q \cdot L \cdot 9.81 \cdot f}{3600} = Q \cdot L \cdot f \cdot 2.725 \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kW and round to 367 (standard KTU form)**

$$P = \dfrac{Q \cdot L \cdot f}{367} \quad \text{[kW]}$$

> The factor **2.725 W per (t·m)** rounded as **1/367 kW** is the standard KTU valuation constant. **Never derive it from first principles in the exam** — just state the result and plug values.

---

### 3.3 Work-Out Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

> [!NOTE] **Worked Example — Q. Design an en-masse chain conveyor to transport 60 t/hr of wheat over a horizontal distance of 30 m. Bulk density of wheat = 750 kg/m³. The chain speed is to be 0.30 m/s. Take fill factor φ = 0.75 and friction factor f = 0.4. Assume drive efficiency η = 0.85.**

#### Part (a) — Determine the cross-sectional area and casing dimensions.

**Step 1: Required cross-section**

Rearranging the capacity formula:

$$A = \dfrac{Q_m}{3600 \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot \phi}$$

$$A = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600 \times 0.30 \times 750 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{60000}{607500} = 0.0988 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: For an en-masse square casing, A = W²**

$$W = \sqrt{0.0988} = 0.314 \text{ m} \approx 0.32 \text{ m} \;(320 \text{ mm})$$

**Step 3: Check the chain pitch requirement**

For en-masse, the material height H = 2p to 3p. If H = W = 0.32 m, then:

$$p = \dfrac{H}{2.5} = \dfrac{0.32}{2.5} = 0.128 \text{ m} \approx 0.125 \text{ m} \;(125 \text{ mm pitch})$$

> **Adopt standard pitch p = 125 mm** (a readily available chain size).

---

#### Part (b) — Calculate the power required and select the motor.

**Step 4: Horizontal power (resistance only)**

$$P_h = \dfrac{Q \cdot L \cdot f}{367} = \dfrac{60 \times 30 \times 0.4}{367} = \dfrac{720}{367} = 1.962 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Add 20% for chain and flight dead-weight (standard KTU allowance)**

$$P_{shaft} = 1.962 \times 1.20 = 2.354 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Motor power accounting for drive efficiency**

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta} = \dfrac{2.354}{0.85} = 2.77 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7: Select next standard motor size**

> **Select a 3.0 kW (4 HP) squirrel-cage induction motor** at 1440 rpm with a gear reducer to give 0.30 m/s chain speed.

**Step 8: Verify sprocket teeth**

For p = 0.125 m and v = 0.30 m/s:

$$N = \dfrac{60 \cdot v}{p \cdot Z} \Rightarrow Z = \dfrac{60 \cdot v}{p \cdot N}$$

If the drive shaft runs at N = 60 rpm (after reduction):

$$Z = \dfrac{60 \times 0.30}{0.125 \times 60} = 2.4$$

That is too few teeth (chain will jump). **Re-select N = 20 rpm:**

$$Z = \dfrac{60 \times 0.30}{0.125 \times 20} = 7.2 \rightarrow Z = 8 \text{ teeth (acceptable, but preferably 9)}$$

Adopt **Z = 9 teeth** and find the actual drive speed:

$$N = \dfrac{60 \times 0.30}{0.125 \times 9} = 16 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 9: Chain breaking load check**

Total tractive force:

$$F_t = \dfrac{1000 \cdot P_{shaft}}{v} = \dfrac{1000 \times 2.354}{0.30} = 7847 \text{ N}$$

Per strand (single chain): $F_o = 7847$ N

With FoS = 8: $F_b = 8 \times 7.847 = 62.8$ kN

> **Adopt a standard 80 kN breaking-load chain** (closest catalogue match above the calculated 62.8 kN).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (for KTU MCQs and Short Notes)

| Decision Question | Recommended Choice | Justification |
|-------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Conveying hygroscopic WMP? | En-masse / redler (totally enclosed) | Prevents moisture pickup |
| Hot material (>80 °C)? | Apron / slat with metal pans | Rubber belt would degrade |
| Vertical lift > 10 m? | Bucket elevator (not chain conveyor) | Chain wear in return path |
| Friable flakes (corn flakes)? | Low chain speed (0.1 m/s) en-masse | Minimises breakage |
| Slurry or semi-fluid? | Paddle conveyor (U-trough) | Handles non-Newtonian foods |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q: Differentiate between an en-masse conveyor and a drag conveyor. List two food-industry applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | En-Masse Conveyor | Drag Conveyor |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Carrying Action** | Material moves as a compact, unified slug inside a casing | Material is scraped along the trough bottom by flights |
| **Cross-Section** | Casing is fully filled (square or rectangle) | Material forms a thin layer; trough is only partially filled |
| **Capacity for same size** | Higher (volumetric efficiency 75–85 %) | Lower (volumetric efficiency 30–50 %) |
| **Power for same Q** | Lower (en-masse reduces internal friction) | Higher |

**Applications (1 mark):**
- **En-masse:** (i) Wheat/maize in flour mills, (ii) Hygroscopic milk powder
- **Drag:** (i) Crystalline sugar, (ii) Wet spent grain in breweries

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q: Why is a factor of safety of 6–10 used when selecting the chain for a food-grade conveyor? Mention any two standard chain pitches used in en-masse conveyors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A factor of safety (FoS) of 6–10 accounts for: (i) **sudden surge loads** during start-up and stop, (ii) **abrasive wear** of pin-bush joints, (iii) **corrosion** from cleaning chemicals (CIP), and (iv) **chain elongation** with service life. Food industry typically uses **FoS = 8** (1½ marks).
Standard pitches: **100 mm, 125 mm, 160 mm, 200 mm** (any two for 1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE – Model Paper, July 2024]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Derive the equation for the **mass-handling capacity** of an en-masse chain conveyor. State clearly the units of each term. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A chain conveyor is required to handle **45 t/hr of maize grits** over a **horizontal distance of 24 m**. Bulk density of grits = **720 kg/m³**, chain speed = **0.25 m/s**, fill factor = **0.75**, and friction factor = **0.4**. Determine: (i) the cross-sectional area of the casing, (ii) the power required at the chain shaft, and (iii) the motor power assuming 85 % drive efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Let the casing cross-section = A (m²), chain speed = v (m/s), bulk density = ρ (kg/m³), fill factor = φ (dimensionless). **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Volume of material advanced per second = A · v · φ (m³/s). **(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** Mass per second = A · v · φ · ρ (kg/s). **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Multiply by 3600 s/hr to get per-hour capacity:

$$Q_m = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot \phi \quad \text{[kg/hr]}$$

**(2 marks)** — **Final boxed equation for full marks.**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \dfrac{Q_m}{3600 \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot \phi} = \dfrac{45000}{3600 \times 0.25 \times 720 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{45000}{486000} = 0.0926 \text{ m}^2$$

For square casing, W = √0.0926 = 0.304 m ≈ **0.30 m (300 mm)**. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Shaft power (horizontal, KTU formula):**

$$P_h = \dfrac{Q \cdot L \cdot f}{367} = \dfrac{45 \times 24 \times 0.4}{367} = \dfrac{432}{367} = 1.177 \text{ kW}$$

Adding 20 % for chain/flight dead-weight: $P_{shaft} = 1.177 \times 1.20 = 1.412$ kW ≈ **1.41 kW**. **(3 marks)**

**(iii) Motor power:**

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta} = \dfrac{1.412}{0.85} = 1.661 \text{ kW}$$

Adopt next standard size: **2.2 kW (3 HP) motor** at 1440 rpm with suitable gear reducer. **(2 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of an en-masse chain conveyor**. List four **design considerations** specific to food-industry applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A horizontal en-masse conveyor has a **casing 250 mm × 250 mm**, chain speed **0.20 m/s**, and is handling a **food powder of bulk density 600 kg/m³** with a fill factor of **0.80**. If the conveyor is **20 m long** and the **friction factor is 0.35**, calculate: (i) the **mass handling capacity in t/hr**, (ii) the **power required at the drive shaft**, and (iii) the **total breaking load of the chain** assuming a factor of safety of 8 and a single-strand chain. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Casing (rectangular or square) made of food-grade SS-304.
- Endless chain (single or double strand) with welded flights.
- Head (drive) and tail (boot) sprockets with shaft.
- Take-up unit (screw-type) at the tail.
- Inlet chute at the boot, discharge spout at the head.

**Working (2 marks):** Motor → gearbox → drive sprocket → chain pulls flights → flights drag material column forward as a single mass → discharge at head → empty chain returns along bottom.

**Four food-industry design considerations (2 marks):**
1. **Material of construction must be SS-304 / SS-316** (food-grade, corrosion-resistant).
2. **Tight casing seals** to prevent dust ingress and contamination.
3. **CIP (clean-in-place) compatibility** — smooth welds, no dead pockets.
4. **Low chain speed** to avoid breakage of friable foods (flakes, chips, grains).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Mass capacity:**

$$Q_m = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot \phi = 3600 \times 0.25 \times 0.25 \times 0.20 \times 600 \times 0.80$$

$$Q_m = 3600 \times 0.0625 \times 0.20 \times 480 = 3600 \times 6.0 = 21600 \text{ kg/hr} = 21.6 \text{ t/hr}$$

**(3 marks)**

**(ii) Drive-shaft power (horizontal):**

$$P_h = \dfrac{Q \cdot L \cdot f}{367} = \dfrac{21.6 \times 20 \times 0.35}{367} = \dfrac{151.2}{367} = 0.412 \text{ kW}$$

Add 20 % for chain dead-weight: $P_{shaft} = 0.412 \times 1.20 = 0.494$ kW ≈ **0.49 kW**. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Chain breaking load:**

Tractive force: $F_t = \dfrac{1000 \times P_{shaft}}{v} = \dfrac{1000 \times 0.494}{0.20} = 2470$ N

Per strand = 2470 N. With FoS = 8:

$$F_b = 8 \times 2470 = 19760 \text{ N} \approx 19.76 \text{ kN} \approx 20 \text{ kN}$$

**(2 marks)** — Adopt the nearest standard chain of 20–25 kN breaking load.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 20 % chain/flight dead-weight allowance** — costs 1 mark. Always multiply the calculated shaft power by **1.20** before selecting the motor.
> 2. **Using v in m/min instead of m/s** — the KTU formula expects **v in m/s**. Wrong unit → wrong power by a factor of 60.
> 3. **Confusing A × v × φ (volumetric) with A × v × ρ × φ (mass)** — re-read the question carefully. "Capacity in t/hr" requires **ρ**; "in m³/hr" does not.
> 4. **Selecting a sprocket with fewer than 7 teeth** — causes chain jump. Always ensure **Z ≥ 7** (preferably ≥ 9).
> 5. **Forgetting to box the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for un-boxed final expressions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT] **Save this list — read once before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **En-masse conveyor** = most-tested variant; treats material as a unified slug inside a casing.
- ✅ **Capacity formula:** $Q_m = 3600 A v \rho \phi$ (kg/hr) — memorise the 3600 constant.
- ✅ **Horizontal power:** $P_h = \dfrac{Q L f}{367}$ kW — use 367, not 360 or 3.6.
- ✅ **Inclined power:** add the term $\dfrac{Q H}{367}$ for vertical lift.
- ✅ **Always add 20 %** for chain/flight dead-weight before computing motor power.
- ✅ **Motor power = shaft power / η** with η ≈ 0.85.
- ✅ **Chain FoS = 6–10** (food industry = **8**); breaking load = FoS × working load.
- ✅ **Sprocket teeth Z ≥ 7**; preferably 9–13.
- ✅ **Standard chain pitches:** 100, 125, 160, 200 mm.
- ✅ **Fill factor φ = 0.75** is the KTU default for en-masse.
- ✅ **Friction factor f = 0.3–0.5** for food powders on steel trough.
- ✅ **Chain speed v = 0.1–0.5 m/s** in food industry; use **lower** end for friable materials.
- ✅ **En-masse** for **hygroscopic/dusty**; **drag** for **abrasive crystalline**; **apron/slat** for **hot or packaged** goods.
- ✅ **For vertical lift > 10 m** → choose **bucket elevator**, not chain conveyor.
- ✅ **Construction material:** SS-304/SS-316; design for **CIP compatibility**.
- ✅ **Cross-section is square** for en-masse; H = 2p to 3p; adopt nearest standard pitch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic Conveyor, Cleaning and Grading, Screens, Effectiveness of Screen, Air Screen Cleaners, Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT722 - FOOD PROCESS AND EQUIPMENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic Conveyor, Cleaning and Grading, Screens, Effectiveness of Screen, Air Screen Cleaners, Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pneumatic Conveyor (Pneumatic Conveying System)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
A **Pneumatic Conveyor** is a material handling system in which dry bulk solid particles (food grains, flour, spices, powdered ingredients) are transported in suspension through an **enclosed pipeline** using a **high-velocity gas stream** (typically air) generated by a blower, fan, or compressor. The conveying fluid transfers momentum to the particles via drag forces, enabling transport over horizontal, vertical, or inclined routes without moving mechanical parts in contact with the product.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Terminology – "Fluidized Solid–Gas Mixture":**
> The food material (solid phase) is dispersed as a discrete particulate phase within the air (continuous fluid phase). The mixture behaves as a **two-phase flow** with a defined solid loading ratio ($\mu$), commonly between **5 and 50** for dilute-phase systems.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners):**
Imagine blowing on a handful of talcum powder through a long straw — the powder rides the air current from your mouth to the other end without you pushing each particle. Now scale this up: a powerful fan replaces your breath, a galvanized steel pipe replaces the straw, and food grains/powders replace the talc. That is precisely how pneumatic conveying works in a food plant for moving wheat flour, sugar, milk powder, rice, or pulses. The pipeline is **sealed**, which means the product is **dust-free, hygienic, and contamination-protected** — a critical advantage in food processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pneumatic conveying is preferred in food plants:**
> (i) Enclosed pipeline → no contamination & no product loss.
> (ii) Few moving parts → easy cleaning (CIP-friendly).
> (iii) Multi-directional routing (up, down, around corners).
> (iv) Labour-saving and suitable for explosive/flammable powders when using inert gas.

---

### 1.2 Cleaning and Grading

**Formal Definitions:**

- **Cleaning** is the unit operation of **separating undesirable foreign matter** (dust, chaff, stones, metal fragments, straw, insects, immature/shriveled grains, weed seeds) from the desired food material using differences in physical properties such as **size, shape, density, magnetic susceptibility, color, and aerodynamic properties**.

- **Grading** is the unit operation of **classifying the cleaned food material into uniform quality fractions** based on size, weight, or maturity, so that downstream operations (milling, roasting, packaging) receive a homogeneous feed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Distinction (Frequently asked in ESE):**
> *Cleaning* removes **unwanted contaminants** (impurities).
> *Grading* partitions the **good product** itself into **multiple quality grades**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a paddy farmer's wife manually winnowing rice by tossing it from a flat basket — light chaff blows away in the wind (this is **aspiration-based cleaning**). After winnowing, she picks out tiny stones by hand (density-based cleaning). Finally, she sorts the rice grains by length on a slotted hand-sieve — large unbroken grains go to the family, broken bits go to the chickens. That final step is **grading**.

---

### 1.3 Screens (Sieves) in Food Processing

**Formal Definition:**
A **screen** (or **sieve**) is a perforated surface — composed of **woven wire mesh, punched plate, or punched profile rods** — with precisely defined apertures through which particles are presented for **size-based separation**. Screens are characterized by their **mesh number** (number of apertures per linear inch) or **aperture size** $d_a$.

**Types relevant to KTU Module 4:**
1. **Scalping screen** – removes only oversize contaminants (fines pass through).
2. **Grading screen** – separates product into multiple size fractions.
3. **De-Dusting screen** – removes only the fine dust (oversize passes through).

---

### 1.4 Effectiveness of a Screen

**Formal Definition:**
The **Screen Effectiveness** $\eta_s$ (also called **Screening Efficiency** or **Partition Coefficient**) is defined as the ratio of the mass of a particular size fraction actually reporting to its **correct outlet stream** to the mass of that same fraction present in the feed.

$$\eta_s = \frac{\text{Mass of size class } i \text{ recovered in its designated stream}}{\text{Mass of size class } i \text{ present in the feed}} \times 100 \;[\%]$$

The **Ideal Screen** has $\eta_s = 100\%$; a **Real Screen** always has $\eta_s < 100\%$ because of inefficiencies (blinding, near-mesh particles, short residence time).

---

### 1.5 Air Screen Cleaners

**Formal Definition:**
An **Air-Screen Cleaner** is a composite cleaning machine that combines an **air aspiration column** (vertical air stream) with one or more **vibrating/perforated screens** in series. The air stream removes light impurities (chaff, dust, light husk), while the screen(s) remove oversize (stones, sticks) and undersize (sand, broken grains) contaminants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Air-screen cleaners are the **workhorse pre-cleaner** in almost every rice, wheat, maize, and pulse processing line worldwide. It is mandatory to study their design for Module 4.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a domestic flour-sifter (the round drum with the wire mesh and a hand-crank). Now add a small fan blowing upward through the mesh so that light dust flies out the back. That combination of *air + vibration + mesh* is the air-screen cleaner in miniature.

---

### 1.6 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded for Memory)

- **Standard conveying air density:** $\rho_f = 1.2 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at 20 °C, 1 atm.
- **Solid-to-air mass ratio (dilute phase):** $\mu = 5$ to $50 \; \text{kg solid/kg air}$.
- **Typical conveying velocity:** $v = 15$ to $30 \; \text{m/s}$.
- **Aperture size range (food screens):** $d_a = 0.05 \; \text{mm}$ to $25 \; \text{mm}$.
- **Acceptable screen effectiveness:** $\eta_s \geq 80\%$ for industrial food screens.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pneumatic Conveying – Operating Principle & Phases

The pneumatic conveyor operates on **three coupled physical mechanisms**:

1. **Particle Entrainment** — The drag force of the moving air exceeds the combined gravitational & cohesive forces acting on the particle.
2. **Particle Acceleration** — Momentum transfer from the air to the particle during the initial transient region.
3. **Suspension Transport** — Once terminal velocity is reached, particles travel at a constant relative velocity (slip velocity) inside the pipe.

**Classification of Pneumatic Conveying Modes (KTU High-Yield):**

| Mode | Solid Loading $\mu$ | Velocity $v$ | Typical Application in Food Industry |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Dilute Phase (Suspension Flow)** | $5$ – $50$ | $15$ – $30 \; \text{m/s}$ | Wheat flour, milk powder, sugar, starch |
| **Dense Phase (Slug / Plug Flow)** | $50$ – $400$ | $1$ – $10 \; \text{m/s}$ | Puffed cereals, fragile flakes, agglomerated powders |
| **Fluidized / Semi-Dense** | $40$ – $100$ | $5$ – $15 \; \text{m/s}$ | Granulated products, coarse salt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most food plants operate in **dilute phase** because the high velocity prevents fat/sugar agglomerates from sticking to pipe walls.

---

### 2.2 Terminal Velocity (Slip Velocity) – The Foundation of Design

The **terminal velocity** $v_t$ of a particle in still air is reached when **drag force balances gravitational minus buoyancy force**:

$$F_D = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g$$

**Three Regimes:**

| Regime | Reynolds Number $\text{Re}_p$ | Law | Formula for $v_t$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Stokes' Law (laminar)** | $\text{Re}_p < 0.2$ | $C_D = 24/\text{Re}_p$ | $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ |
| **Intermediate (Allen / Transition)** | $0.2 < \text{Re}_p < 1000$ | $C_D = 18.5/\text{Re}_p^{0.6}$ | Solved iteratively |
| **Newton's Law (turbulent)** | $\text{Re}_p > 1000$ | $C_D = 0.44$ | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 g d_p (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ |

Where:
- $d_p$ = particle diameter, $\rho_s$ = particle density, $\rho_f$ = air density, $\mu$ = air viscosity, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **conveying velocity $v$ must exceed the saltation (minimum pickup) velocity** by a factor of **1.2 to 1.5** to keep particles suspended — this is the golden rule of pneumatic conveyor design.

---

### 2.3 Pressure Drop in a Pneumatic Conveyor

The total pressure drop determines the **blower/compressor sizing** and hence the **operating cost**.

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{gas} + \Delta P_{friction} + \Delta P_{suspension} + \Delta P_{acceleration} + \Delta P_{bends}$$

Expanded (using the equivalent-length approach):

$$\Delta P_{total} = \lambda \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho_f v^2}{2} \left(1 + \mu \frac{v_p}{v}\right) + \Delta P_{acceleration}$$

**Where:**

- $\lambda$ = Darcy friction factor (≈ 0.02 for smooth galvanized pipe).
- $L$ = equivalent pipe length (with $L_{eq,bend} \approx 30 D$ to $60 D$ per 90° bend).
- $D$ = pipe inner diameter.
- $v_p$ = particle velocity (≈ $v - v_t$ in steady state).
- $\Delta P_{acceleration} = \dfrac{\mu \rho_f v^2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_p}{v}$ at the feed point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bends are the chief pressure-loss villains** — each 90° elbow can add **30 % to 40 %** of the equivalent straight-run loss. Designs minimize bends for energy economy.

---

### 2.4 Cleaning & Grading – Operating Principles

**Cleaning Equipment Classification by Property Exploited:**

| Property | Equipment Example | Contaminant Removed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Size** | Vibratory screens, rotary sieves | Stones, sticks, broken grains |
| **Shape** | Indented cylinder (Trieur) | Broken vs. whole grain |
| **Density** | Gravity table (de-stoner), aspiration | Light chaff, heavy stones |
| **Aerodynamic** | Air-column aspirator | Light dust, husk |
| **Magnetic** | Magnetic drum, overband magnet | Iron/metal filings |
| **Optical/Color** | Optical sorter | Discolored grains |

**Grading Logic:**
Grading is mathematically described by a **grade efficiency (partition) curve** $T(d)$, defined as the mass fraction of particles of size $d$ that report to the **coarse (oversize) product stream**:

$$T(d) = \frac{\text{mass of size } d \text{ in coarse product}}{\text{mass of size } d \text{ in feed}}$$

A perfect grader has $T(d) = 1$ for $d > d_c$ and $T(d) = 0$ for $d < d_c$, where $d_c$ is the **cut size** (separation size).

---

### 2.5 Effectiveness of a Screen – Detailed Analysis

The **overall screen effectiveness** integrates the efficiency over all size fractions present in the feed:

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{\sum_i M_i \cdot T_i \cdot \eta_i}{\sum_i M_i \cdot T_i}$$

Where $M_i$ = mass fraction of size $i$ in feed, $T_i$ = fraction of size $i$ ideally recovered, and $\eta_i$ is the actual screen efficiency for that size.

**Common Defects that Lower Effectiveness:**

1. **Blinding** — particles smaller than the aperture lodge in the openings and block subsequent particles. Most prevalent for $d_p / d_a < 1.0$ but not too small.
2. **Near-mesh particles** — particles with $d_p / d_a \approx 1$ pass through only with low probability.
3. **Short residence time** — insufficient contact time with the screen surface.
4. **Overloading** — bed depth too high; undersized particles cannot reach the apertures.
5. **Moisture / Static Charge** — particles stick together or to the screen.

---

### 2.6 Air-Screen Cleaner – Design Considerations

**Functional Components of a Typical Air-Screen Cleaner (Two-Screen Model):**

1. **Feed Hopper with Vibratory Feeder** — controls mass flow rate.
2. **Aspiration Column (Front Aspiration)** — air blows upward; light impurities exit at the top rear.
3. **Screen 1 (Top, Scalping Screen)** — large aperture; removes oversize (stones, sticks).
4. **Screen 2 (Bottom, Grading Screen)** — small aperture; removes undersize (sand, broken grains).
5. **Rear Aspiration** — second air stream for fines.
6. **Discharge Spouts** — for clean product, oversize, and undersize.

**Key Design Variables:**

- **Screen Inclination:** $5°$ to $12°$ (downward flow).
- **Vibration Frequency:** $f = 4$ to $10 \; \text{Hz}$.
- **Vibration Amplitude:** $A = 3$ to $8 \; \text{mm}$.
- **Air Velocity in Aspiration Column:** $v_{air} = 4$ to $8 \; \text{m/s}$ (must exceed $v_t$ of light impurities but stay below $v_t$ of good grain).
- **Specific Air Volume:** $q_{air} = 100$ to $200 \; \text{m}^3/\text{tonne of grain}$.
- **Throughput (wheat, single stage):** $Q = 2$ to $20 \; \text{tonnes/hour}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid (Acronym: "FAD-VIP"):** Feed → Aspiration (front) → Deck 1 → Deck 2 → Aspiration (rear) → Discharge.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas given below are KTU 2024 Exam-Eligible.** Memorise the units — the examiner penalises missing units heavily.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Particle Reynolds Number | $\text{Re}_p = \dfrac{\rho_f v_t d_p}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Determine regime |
| 2 | Stokes Terminal Velocity | $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $\text{Re}_p < 0.2$ |
| 3 | Newton Terminal Velocity | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 g d_p (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $\text{Re}_p > 1000$ |
| 4 | Drag Coefficient (general) | $C_D = \dfrac{2 F_D}{\rho_f v^2 A_p}$ | dimensionless | $A_p = \pi d_p^2/4$ |
| 5 | Saltation Velocity (approx.) | $v_{salt} \approx 17 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{g D \rho_s}{\rho_f}}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $D$ = pipe diameter |
| 6 | Solid Loading Ratio | $\mu = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_f}$ | $\text{kg/kg}$ | Dilute: $\mu < 50$ |
| 7 | Frictional ΔP (gas only) | $\Delta P_f = \lambda \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho_f v^2}{2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Darcy-Weisbach |
| 8 | Total Pressure Drop | $\Delta P_{tot} = \Delta P_f (1 + \mu v_p / v) + \Delta P_{acc}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Blower sizing |
| 9 | Blower Power | $P = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P_{tot}}{\eta_b}$ | $\text{W}$ | $\eta_b \approx 0.6$ |
| 10 | Screen Effectiveness | $\eta_s = \dfrac{M_{i,out}}{M_{i,feed}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | By size class $i$ |
| 11 | Grade Efficiency Curve | $T(d) = \dfrac{\text{coarse mass of size }d}{\text{feed mass of size }d}$ | dimensionless | Cut size at $T=0.5$ |
| 12 | Imperfection (Ecart) | $I = \dfrac{d_{75} - d_{25}}{2 \, d_{50}}$ | dimensionless | Sharpness indicator |
| 13 | Capacity of Air Screen | $Q = A_{screen} \cdot \rho_{bulk} \cdot v_{mat}$ | $\text{kg/h}$ | $v_{mat} \approx 0.1$ to $0.3 \; \text{m/s}$ |
| 14 | Aspiration Air Velocity | $v_{air} = 1.3 \, v_{t,impurity}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Rule of thumb |
| 15 | Screen Aperture (mesh) | $d_a = \dfrac{25.4}{N} - d_w$ | $\text{mm}$ | $N$ = mesh number, $d_w$ = wire dia. |
