## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
*State the working principle of a cyclone separator. List any four standard geometric ratios used in Stairmand high-efficiency cyclone design.*

**Model Answer:**
A cyclone separator separates solid particles from a gas stream using centrifugal force. Dusty gas enters tangentially into a cylindrical body, creating a swirling vortex. Denser particles are thrown outward against the wall by centrifugal force, slide down into a hopper, while the cleaned gas escapes through the central vortex finder.

**Stairmand High-Efficiency Geometric Ratios (w.r.t. body diameter D):**
1. Inlet height: $a/D = 0.5$
2. Inlet width: $b/D = 0.2$
3. Outlet diameter: $D_e/D = 0.5$
4. Cylinder height: $h/D = 1.5$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
*Distinguish between a magnetic separator and a colour separator based on the principle of separation and typical food industry applications.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Magnetic Separator | Colour Separator |
|---------|--------------------|------------------|
| Principle | Difference in magnetic susceptibility (χ) | Difference in optical reflectance / colour |
| Driving force | Magnetic force (F_m) in non-uniform field | Pneumatic jet based on optical signal |
| Energy source | Permanent magnet or electromagnet | LED + CCD camera + compressed air |
| Removes | Tramp iron, steel fragments, ferrite dust | Discoloured, broken, fungal, foreign grains |
| Typical foods | Flour, sugar, spices, oil, cereals | Rice, dal, coffee, nuts, sesame |
| Capacity | 1 – 50 T/h | 0.5 – 20 T/h |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE of Q.A or Q.B**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cyclone Separator Design**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A Stairmand high-efficiency cyclone is to handle **1.0 m³/s** of dust-laden air at **20 °C** and **1 atm**. The dust has a density of **2200 kg/m³**, and the inlet velocity is to be **18 m/s**. Determine the **body diameter, cut-size, and pressure drop** of the cyclone. Use μ = **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**, Nₑ = **5**, and ξ = **8**.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **factors affecting cyclone efficiency** and discuss the **limitations of cyclones** in food processing applications.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A**

#### **Part (a) — Numerical Design (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Q = 1.0 m³/s
- vᵢ = 18 m/s
- ρₚ = 2200 kg/m³
- ρ = 1.2 kg/m³ (air at 20 °C)
- μ = 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s
- Nₑ = 5
- ξ = 8

**Step 1 — Inlet area:**

$$A_i = \frac{Q}{v_i} = \frac{1.0}{18} = 0.0556 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Body diameter (using a × b = 0.1 D²):**

$$0.1 D^2 = 0.0556 \implies D^2 = 0.556 \implies \boxed{D = 0.745 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Other dimensions:**

- a = 0.5 × 0.745 = 0.373 m
- b = 0.2 × 0.745 = 0.149 m
- Dₑ = 0.5 × 0.745 = 0.373 m
- h = 1.5 × 0.745 = 1.118 m
- H = 2.5 × 0.745 = 1.863 m
- D_j = 0.375 × 0.745 = 0.279 m

**Step 4 — Cut-size d₅₀:**

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu b}{2 \pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}$$

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.149}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 18 \times (2200 - 1.2)}}$$

Numerator: $9 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.149 = 2.427 \times 10^{-5}$
Denominator: $2 \pi \times 5 \times 18 \times 2198.8 = 1.243 \times 10^{6}$

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{2.427 \times 10^{-5}}{1.243 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{1.953 \times 10^{-11}} = 4.42 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = \boxed{4.42 \text{ μm}}$$

**Step 5 — Pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P = \xi \times \frac{\rho v_i^2}{2} = 8 \times \frac{1.2 \times 18^2}{2} = 8 \times 194.4 = \boxed{1555.2 \text{ Pa}}$$

#### **Part (b) — Theoretical (7 Marks)**

**Factors affecting cyclone efficiency:**

1. **Inlet velocity (vᵢ):** Higher vᵢ → higher centrifugal force → better separation, but excessive vᵢ causes re-entrainment and higher pressure drop. Optimal range: **15 – 25 m/s**.
2. **Particle density (ρₚ):** Higher density particles are collected more efficiently. Heavier particles reach the wall faster.
3. **Particle size (dₚ):** Efficiency increases sharply with particle size. Sub-micron particles (< 5 μm) are very poorly collected.
4. **Body diameter (D):** Smaller D → higher centrifugal acceleration → higher efficiency, but smaller capacity.
5. **Pressure drop (ΔP):** Higher ΔP implies higher inlet velocity and higher efficiency, but at the cost of blower power.
6. **Dust loading and concentration:** High dust load can cause blockage at the dust outlet.
7. **Gas viscosity (μ):** Higher viscosity (hot gases) reduces efficiency — important in spray dryer cyclones.

**Limitations of Cyclones in Food Processing:**

1. **Low efficiency for fine particles** (< 10 μm) — cannot meet FSSAI dust emission standards alone.
2. **High pressure drop** → high blower power cost.
3. **Cannot handle sticky/ hygroscopic powders** (e.g., sugar, milk powder) — wall buildup.
4. **Erosion of cone wall** by abrasive dust (e.g., rice husk, spices).
5. **Not suitable for separating particles of similar density** (e.g., husk vs. broken grain).
6. **Cannot separate liquid droplets from gas at high efficiency** — needs demister pad.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Magnetic & Colour Separator**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** A **dry magnetic drum separator** is used to remove iron particles from a **wheat flour stream**. The drum radius is **150 mm**, the working gap is **8 mm**, and the magnetic field intensity at the surface is **H = 6 × 10⁵ A/m**. Estimate the **magnetic force on a 200 μm iron particle** (χ = 5000, ρₚ = 7870 kg/m³) at the gap. Comment on whether the particle will be captured.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of an optical colour separator** used in the rice industry. List the **key design parameters** affecting its sorting accuracy.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B**

#### **Part (a) — Magnetic Force Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Drum radius R = 0.15 m
- Working gap δ = 8 mm = 0.008 m
- H = 6 × 10⁵ A/m
- dₚ = 200 μm = 2 × 10⁻⁴ m
- χ = 5000
- ρₚ = 7870 kg/m³

**Step 1 — Particle volume:**

$$V_p = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} = \frac{\pi (2 \times 10^{-4})^3}{6} = \frac{\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-12}}{6} = 4.19 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Field gradient (linear approximation over gap):**

$$\frac{dH}{dx} \approx \frac{H}{\delta} = \frac{6 \times 10^5}{0.008} = 7.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ A/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Magnetic force:**

$$F_m = \mu_0 V_p \chi H \frac{dH}{dx}$$

$$F_m = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4.19 \times 10^{-12} \times 5000 \times 6 \times 10^{5} \times 7.5 \times 10^{7}$$

Compute term by term:
- $4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.257 \times 10^{-6}$
- $V_p = 4.19 \times 10^{-12}$
- $\chi = 5 \times 10^{3}$
- $H = 6 \times 10^{5}$
- $dH/dx = 7.5 \times 10^{7}$

$$F_m = 1.257 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.19 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{3} \times 6 \times 10^{5} \times 7.5 \times 10^{7}$$

Multiplying stepwise:
- $1.257 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.19 \times 10^{-12} = 5.27 \times 10^{-18}$
- $\times 5 \times 10^{3} = 2.63 \times 10^{-14}$
- $\times 6 \times 10^{5} = 1.58 \times 10^{-8}$
- $\times 7.5 \times 10^{7} = 1.186 \text{ N}$

Wait — let me recompute: $1.58 \times 10^{-8} \times 7.5 \times 10^{7} = 1.185 \times 10^{0} \approx 1.18$ N

$$\boxed{F_m \approx 1.18 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4 — Gravity force on particle:**

$$F_g = V_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 4.19 \times 10^{-12} \times (7870 - 1.2) \times 9.81$$

$$F_g = 4.19 \times 10^{-12} \times 7868.8 \times 9.81 = 3.23 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$F_m = 1.18 \text{ N} \gg F_g = 3.23 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N}$$

Ratio: $F_m / F_g \approx 3.65 \times 10^{6}$ — the magnetic force is **over a million times greater** than gravity. **The iron particle will definitely be captured** and lifted cleanly off the belt.

#### **Part (b) — Optical Colour Separator (7 Marks)**

**Construction (Block diagram already provided in Section 4.5 above).**

**Key Components:**

1. **Feed Hopper with Vibratory Feeder:** Presents grains in a uniform monolayer on the inclined chute.
2. **Inclined Chute:** Accelerates grains by gravity; ensures proper spacing and orientation.
3. **Optical Inspection Chamber:**
   - **Background panel** (typically white or black for contrast)
   - **High-intensity LED illumination** (often bichromatic — red + green, or full RGB + NIR)
   - **CCD line-scan or area-scan cameras** capturing reflected light
4. **Signal Processor (DSP):** Compares each pixel's color to the reference using pre-set tolerance windows (HSV or Lab color space).
5. **Pneumatic Ejector System:** High-speed solenoid valve + nozzle firing compressed air (~0.4 MPa) with **1 – 3 ms response time**.
6. **Accept and Reject Chutes:** Separate output streams.

**Working Principle:**
- Grains slide down the chute one by one.
- As each grain crosses the optical zone, the camera captures its image in **< 5 ms**.
- The DSP classifies the grain as "accept" or "reject" based on pre-set color thresholds.
- After a calculated delay (to allow the grain to travel to the nozzle position), the ejector fires a short burst of compressed air to deflect the defective grain into the reject chute.
- Good grains follow a ballistic path into the accept bin.

**Key Design Parameters affecting Sorting Accuracy:**

1. **Color contrast:** Sufficient difference between good and defective grains (set by LED wavelength and background).
2. **Grain velocity on chute:** Higher v → higher throughput, but lower inspection time → reduced accuracy.
3. **Camera resolution and frame rate:** Higher resolution → smaller defects detected.
4. **Ejector response time:** Must be < 1 ms for accurate targeting at high speeds.
5. **Air pressure and nozzle diameter:** Determines impulse imparted to grain.
6. **Number of channels (n):** More channels → higher throughput, but higher cost.
7. **Feed uniformity:** Monolayer presentation is critical to avoid grain overlap.
8. **Tolerance window setting:** Tight tolerance → higher reject ratio (lower yield), loose tolerance → lower purity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Forgetting to convert mm to m in cyclone inlet width (b) or magnetic gap (δ) — a single unit slip can cost **3 – 4 marks**.
> 2. **Using wrong Nₑ:** Students often use Nₑ = 4 for high-efficiency design; it should be **5** (Stairmand) or **4** (Swift conventional).
> 3. **Forgetting (ρₚ − ρ):** In d₅₀ and collection efficiency equations, it is density **difference**, not particle density alone.
> 4. **Confusing A_i = ab with Q/vᵢ:** Many students use the wrong formula for body diameter.
> 5. **No internal choice attempted:** In Part B, attempting BOTH Q.A and Q.B instead of ONE will result in **deduction of 14 marks** (full question) per KTU 2024 valuation norms.
> 6. **Mermaid in answer sheet:** Don't draw flowcharts using ASCII or Mermaid syntax in written exams — use **labelled block diagrams with arrows**.
> 7. **Forgetting to comment on capture feasibility** in magnetic separator numericals — examiners allocate **2 marks** for the "comment" portion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Cyclone Separator:**
- Uses centrifugal sedimentation; **tangential inlet** is the key.
- **Lapple cut-size equation** is THE formula: $d_{50} = \sqrt{9\mu b / (2\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho))}$.
- Stairmand ratios: a = 0.5D, b = 0.2D, Dₑ = 0.5D, h = 1.5D, H = 2.5D, D_j = 0.375D.
- Pressure drop: $\Delta P = 8 \rho v_i^2 / 2$ for high-efficiency.
- Efficiency depends strongly on particle size and inlet velocity.

✅ **Magnetic Separator:**
- Force on particle: $F_m = \mu_0 V_p \chi H (dH/dx)$.
- Capture condition: $F_m \geq F_g + F_d$.
- Three types: **drum, belt, and plate** (overhead).
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m — must be remembered.
- Used for **tramp iron removal** in food lines.

✅ **Colour Separator:**
- Uses **CCD + LED + pneumatic ejector** system.
- Throughput: Q = 60 n m, where n = number of channels.
- Sorting accuracy depends on **color contrast, ejector response time, and feed uniformity**.
- Ejector air pressure: **0.3 – 0.6 MPa**; response time: **< 3 ms**.

✅ **Cross-Cutting Concepts:**
- All three are **continuous-flow separators** used as post-harvest / post-processing purification steps.
- All are sized based on a **mass balance** plus an **equipment-specific force/kinetic model**.
- All have a **figure of merit**: cut-size (cyclone), susceptibility threshold (magnetic), and reject ratio (colour).

✅ **KTU 2024 Examination Strategy:**
- Module 4 carries **~15–20% weightage** in the ESE (End Semester Exam).
- Expect at least **one full 14-mark question** from this module.
- Always draw a **neat block diagram** in design questions — earns 1–2 free marks.
- State **assumptions** clearly (Stairmand high-efficiency, Nₑ = 5, Stokes regime, etc.) before solving.
